text:
The correct order of acidic strength of the major products formed in the given reactions, is :

$\pmb{\text{A.}}$ $\text{PhNH}_2 \xrightarrow[\substack{\text { (2) } \text{CuCN} \\ \text { (3) } \text{H}_3 \text{O}^{+} / \Delta}]{\text { (1) } \text{NaNO}_2+\text{HCl}\left(<5^{\circ} \text{C}\right)}[\text{A}]$

$\pmb{\text{B.}}$ $\text{CH}_3 \text{CH}_2 \text{CHO} \xrightarrow[ \Delta]{\left[\text{Ag}\left(\text{NH}_3\right)_2\right]^{+}, \text{OH}^{-}}[\text{B}]$

$\pmb{\text{C.}}$ $\text{CH}_4+\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow[\text { (ii) } \text{Na}_2 \text{Cr}_2 \text{O}_7 / \text{H}^{+}]{\text {(i) } \text{Mo}_2 \text{O}_3}[\text{C}]$

$\pmb{\text{D.}}$ $\text{PhCH}_2 \text{MgBr}+\text{CO}_2 \xrightarrow[\text{H}_3 \text{O}^{+}]{\text{Dry ether }}[\text{D}]$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Identify the Major Product of each reaction}}$

$$\begin{array}{|c|l|l|} 
\hline 
\text{Reaction} & \text{Chemical Pathway} & \text{Major Product} \\ \hline 
\text{A} & \text{Aniline} \rightarrow \text{Benzene diazonium chloride} \rightarrow \text{Benzonitrile} \xrightarrow{\text{H}_3\text{O}^+} & \text{Benzoic Acid (PhCOOH)} \\ 
\text{B} & \text{Propanal} \xrightarrow{\text{Tollen's Reagent}} & \text{Propanoic Acid (CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COOH)} \\ 
\text{C} & \text{Methane} \xrightarrow{\text{Mo}_2\text{O}_3} \text{Methanal} \xrightarrow{\text{Na}_2\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_7 / \text{H}^+} & \text{Methanoic Acid (HCOOH)} \\ 
\text{D} & \text{Benzyl magnesium bromide} + \text{CO}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{H}_3\text{O}^+} & \text{Phenylacetic Acid (PhCH}_2\text{COOH)} \\ \hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Compare the Acidic Strength}}$

Acidic strength is determined by the $pK_a$ values and the stability of the conjugate base (carboxylate ion):

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{C: Methanoic Acid (HCOOH)}}$ is the strongest among these ($pK_a \approx 3.75$) because it has no alkyl group to provide a $+I$ (inductive) effect, which would otherwise destabilize the carboxylate anion.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{A: Benzoic Acid (PhCOOH)}}$ ($pK_a \approx 4.20$) is stronger than aliphatic acids like propanoic acid because the $sp^2$ hybridized carbon of the phenyl ring is more electron-withdrawing than an $sp^3$ hybridized carbon.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{D: Phenylacetic Acid (PhCH}_2\text{COOH)}}$ ($pK_a \approx 4.31$) is slightly weaker than benzoic acid because the $-I$ effect of the phenyl ring is dampened by the intervening $-\text{CH}_2-$ group.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{B: Propanoic Acid (CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COOH)}}$ ($pK_a \approx 4.87$) is the weakest due to the electron-donating $+I$ effect of the ethyl group, which destabilizes the conjugate base.



$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Establish the final sequence}}$

Based on the electronic effects and $pK_a$ data:
$$\text{Methanoic acid (C)} > \text{Benzoic acid (A)} > \text{Phenylacetic acid (D)} > \text{Propanoic acid (B)}$$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

The correct order of acidic strength is C > A > D > B.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{C > A > D > B}}$.

text: $ \text { Identify (P) } $

Iodoform test can differentiate between :

$\text{A. Methanol and Ethanol}$

$\text{B. } \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \text{ and } \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{COOH}$

$\text{C. Cyclohexene and cyclohexanone}$

$\text{D. Diethyl ether and Pentan-3-one}$

$\text{E. Anisole and acetone}$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Identify gas x from Reaction (A)}}$

$\bullet$ Phenol reacts with active metals such as sodium ($\mathrm{Na}$) to form sodium phenoxide and release hydrogen gas.

$\bullet$ Reaction: $\mathrm{2C_6H_5OH + 2Na \rightarrow 2C_6H_5ONa + H_2(g)}$.

$\bullet$ Thus, gas $\pmb{\text{x}}$ is $\pmb{\mathrm{H_2}}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Molar mass of x}}$ ($\mathrm{H_2}$) = $2 \times 1 = \pmb{2 \text{ g/mol}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Identify gas y from Reaction (B)}}$

$\bullet$ Benzoic acid reacts with sodium bicarbonate ($\mathrm{NaHCO_3}$) to form sodium benzoate, water, and carbon dioxide gas.

$\bullet$ Reaction: $\mathrm{C_6H_5COOH + NaHCO_3 \rightarrow C_6H_5COONa + H_2O + CO_2(g)}$.

$\bullet$ Thus, gas $\pmb{\text{y}}$ is $\pmb{\mathrm{CO_2}}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Molar mass of y}}$ ($\mathrm{CO_2}$) = $12 + (2 \times 16) = \pmb{44 \text{ g/mol}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate the Final Sum}}$

$\bullet$ Numerical value = [molar mass of x + molar mass of y].

$\bullet$ Sum = $2 + 44 = \pmb{46}$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{46}$.

text: ' $x$ ' is the product which is obtained from propanenitrile and stannous chloride in the presence of hydrochloric acid followed by hydrolysis. ' $y$ ' is the product which is obtained from the but-2-ene by the ozonolysis followed by hydrolysis. From the following, which product is not obtained when one mole of ' $x$ ' and one mole of ' $y$ ' react with each other in the presence of alkali followed by heating?

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Identify Chemical Species x and y}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Formation of x:}}$ Propanenitrile reacts with $\mathrm{SnCl_2/HCl}$ followed by hydrolysis (Stephen reduction) to form propanal.
$$\mathrm{CH_3CH_2C\equiv N \xrightarrow[(ii)H_3O^+]{(i)SnCl_2/HCl} CH_3CH_2CHO \text{ (x)}}$$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Formation of y:}}$ Reductive ozonolysis of but-2-ene cleaves the double bond to yield ethanal.
$$\mathrm{CH_3CH=CHCH_3 \xrightarrow[(ii)Zn/H_2O]{(i)O_3} 2CH_3CHO \text{ (y)}}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze Aldol Condensation Products}}$

When a mixture of propanal (x) and ethanal (y) is treated with alkali and heated, the following products are formed:

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Self-Aldol of x:}}$ $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CHO + CH_3CH_2CHO \xrightarrow[\Delta]{OH^-} CH_3CH_2CH=C(CH_3)CHO}$ (2-Methylpent-2-enal).

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Self-Aldol of y:}}$ $\mathrm{CH_3CHO + CH_3CHO \xrightarrow[\Delta]{OH^-} CH_3CH=CHCHO}$ (But-2-enal).

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Cross-Aldol (y as electrophile):}}$ $\mathrm{CH_3CHO + CH_3CH_2CHO \xrightarrow[\Delta]{OH^-} CH_3CH=C(CH_3)CHO}$ (2-Methylbut-2-enal).

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Cross-Aldol (x as electrophile):}}$ $\mathrm{CH_3CH_2CHO + CH_3CHO \xrightarrow[\Delta]{OH^-} CH_3CH_2CH=CHCHO}$ (Pent-2-enal).

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Identify the Impossible Product}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{3-Methylbut-2-enal}}$ requires the carbonyl carbon to be part of a branched system (like acetone) to have a methyl group at the 3-position. Since both x and y are linear aldehydes, this product cannot be formed.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{C}}$.

text: From the given following (A to D) cyclic structures, those which will not react with Tollen's reagent are:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Understanding Tollen's Reactivity in Cyclic Sugars}}$
$\bullet$ $\textbf{Reducing Sugars:}$ Cyclic hemiacetals (where the anomeric carbon is bonded to an $\mathrm{-OH}$ group) are in equilibrium with their open-chain aldehyde forms. These react with Tollen's reagent.
$\bullet$ $\textbf{Non-Reducing Sugars:}$ Cyclic acetals (where the anomeric carbon is bonded to an $\mathrm{-OR}$ group, such as $\mathrm{-OCH_3}$) cannot open into an aldehyde form in alkaline conditions and therefore do not react with Tollen's reagent.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyzing Structures A through D}}$
$\bullet$ $\textbf{Structure A:}$ This is a hemiacetal (anomeric $\mathrm{C}$ has an $\mathrm{-OH}$ group). It is a reducing sugar and $\textbf{will}$ react with Tollen's reagent.
$\bullet$ $\textbf{Structure B:}$ This is an acetal (anomeric $\mathrm{C}$ has an $\mathrm{-OCH_3}$ group). It is a non-reducing sugar and $\textbf{will not}$ react with Tollen's reagent.
$\bullet$ $\textbf{Structure C:}$ This is also an acetal (anomeric $\mathrm{C}$ has an $\mathrm{-OCH_3}$ group). It is a non-reducing sugar and $\textbf{will not}$ react with Tollen's reagent.
$\bullet$ $\textbf{Structure D:}$ This is a hemiacetal (anomeric $\mathrm{C}$ has an $\mathrm{-OH}$ group). It is a reducing sugar and $\textbf{will}$ react with Tollen's reagent.

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
Structures $\textbf{B}$ and $\textbf{C}$ are acetals and will not react with Tollen's reagent.

Final Answer: $\boxed{D}$

The compound $\text{A}$, $\text{C}_8\text{H}_8\text{O}_2$, reacts with acetophenone to form a single product via cross-Aldol condensation. The compound $\text{A}$ on reaction with conc. $\text{NaOH}$ forms a substituted benzyl alcohol as one of the two products. The compound $\text{A}$ is:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Chemical Analysis of Compound A}}$

$\bullet$ $\textbf{Molecular Formula:}$ $\text{C}_8\text{H}_8\text{O}_2$ matches $\textbf{4-methoxy benzaldehyde}$.
$\bullet$ $\textbf{Cross-Aldol Condensation:}$ Aldehydes lacking $\alpha\text{-H}$ atoms, like $\text{A}$, react with acetophenone (which has $\alpha\text{-H}$) to give a single condensation product.
$\bullet$ $\textbf{Cannizzaro Reaction:}$ Treatment with conc. $\text{NaOH}$ produces a substituted benzyl alcohol and a carboxylate salt, confirming $\text{A}$ is an aldehyde without $\alpha\text{-H}$.

$\pmb{\text{Reaction Summary}}$

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} 
\hline 
\textbf{Reaction Type} & \textbf{Result for 4-methoxy benzaldehyde} \\ 
\hline 
\text{Cross-Aldol} & \text{Forms 4-methoxy-chalcone (single product)} \\ 
\hline 
\text{Cannizzaro} & \text{Forms 4-methoxybenzyl alcohol + salt} \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$\textbf{Statement I:}$ $\text{A}$ must be an aldehyde to undergo these nucleophilic additions.
$\textbf{Statement II:}$ The absence of $\alpha\text{-H}$ restricts self-aldol, ensuring a single cross-product.

$\pmb{\text{Correct Option: (D)}}$

text:
Match the reagents in $\pmb{\text{List-I}}$ with the reaction names in $\pmb{\text{List-II}}$ involving carbonyl compounds:

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{List-I (Reagents)}} & \pmb{\text{List-II (Name of Reaction)}} \\ \hline 
\pmb{\text{A. }} \text{NH}_2\text{-NH}_2, \text{ KOH} & \pmb{\text{I. }} \text{Tollen's Test} \\ 
\pmb{\text{B. }} \text{[Ag(NH}_3\text{)}_2\text{]OH} & \pmb{\text{II. }} \text{Clemmensen Reduction} \\ 
\pmb{\text{C. }} \text{Aq. CuSO}_4, \text{ Sodium Potassium Tartrate, KOH} & \pmb{\text{III. }} \text{Wolff-Kishner Reduction} \\ 
\pmb{\text{D. }} \text{Zn-Hg, HCl} & \pmb{\text{IV. }} \text{Fehling's Test} \\ \hline 
\end{array}$$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Match each reagent with its corresponding chemical reaction}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{A. NH}_2\text{-NH}_2, \text{ KOH:}}$ This is the reagent for the $\pmb{\text{Wolff-Kishner Reduction}}$. It is a base-catalyzed method to reduce carbonyl groups ($>\text{C=O}$) into methylene groups ($-\text{CH}_2-$). 


$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{B. [Ag(NH}_3\text{)}_2\text{]OH:}}$ Known as $\pmb{\text{Tollen's Reagent}}$. It is used to identify aldehydes. The aldehyde is oxidized to a carboxylate ion while $\text{Ag}^+$ is reduced to metallic silver, creating a silver mirror on the test tube.


$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{C. Aq. CuSO}_4, \text{ Sodium Potassium Tartrate, KOH:}}$ These are the components of $\pmb{\text{Fehling's Solution}}$ (Solution A and B). It is used to detect aliphatic aldehydes. The $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ ions are reduced to a reddish-brown precipitate of $\text{Cu}_2\text{O}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{D. Zn-Hg, HCl:}}$ This is the reagent for the $\pmb{\text{Clemmensen Reduction}}$. It reduces aldehydes and ketones to alkanes under strongly acidic conditions.


$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Final Mapping Table}}$

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|l|} 
\hline 
\text{List-I} & \text{List-II} & \text{Mapping Result} \\ \hline 
\text{A} & \text{III} & \text{A-III} \\ 
\text{B} & \text{I} & \text{B-I} \\ 
\text{C} & \text{IV} & \text{C-IV} \\ 
\text{D} & \text{II} & \text{D-II} \\ \hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
The correct match is $\pmb{\text{A-III, B-I, C-IV, D-II}}$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{B}}$.

text:
Match List - I with List - II.

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{List - I (Reagents)} & \textbf{List - II (Reaction Name)} \\
\hline
\text{A. H}_2, \text{Pd–BaSO}_4 & \text{I. Etard Reaction} \\
\hline
\text{B. SnCl}_2, \text{HCl} & \text{II. Rosenmund Reduction} \\
\hline
\text{C. CrO}_2\text{Cl}_2, \text{CS}_2 & \text{III. Gattermann–Koch Reaction} \\
\hline
\text{D. CO, HCl, Anhyd. AlCl}_3 & \text{IV. Stephen Reaction} \\
\hline
\end{array}$$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Reaction Mapping Summary}}$

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} 
\hline 
\textbf{Reagent System} & \textbf{Named Reaction} \\ 
\hline 
\text{H}_2, \text{Pd–BaSO}_4 & \text{Rosenmund Reduction} \\ 
\hline 
\text{SnCl}_2, \text{HCl} & \text{Stephen Reaction} \\ 
\hline 
\text{CrO}_2\text{Cl}_2, \text{CS}_2 & \text{Etard Reaction} \\ 
\hline 
\text{CO, HCl, Anhyd. AlCl}_3 & \text{Gattermann–Koch Reaction} \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Detailed Verification:}}$

$\bullet$ $\text{A-II:}$ $\textbf{Rosenmund Reduction}$ involves the partial reduction of acyl chlorides to aldehydes using hydrogen gas over palladium supported on barium sulfate.
$\bullet$ $\text{B-IV:}$ $\textbf{Stephen Reaction}$ utilizes tin(II) chloride and hydrochloric acid to reduce nitriles to imines, which are subsequently hydrolyzed to aldehydes.
$\bullet$ $\text{C-I:}$ $\textbf{Etard Reaction}$ employs chromyl chloride ($\text{CrO}_2\text{Cl}_2$) to oxidize a methyl group on an aromatic ring to an aldehyde group.
$\bullet$ $\text{D-III:}$ $\textbf{Gattermann–Koch Reaction}$ introduces a formyl group ($-\text{CHO}$) into benzene or its derivatives using $\text{CO}$ and $\text{HCl}$ in the presence of anhydrous $\text{AlCl}_3$.

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$ The correct match is $\textbf{A-II, B-IV, C-I, D-III}$.

$\pmb{\text{Correct Option: (D)}}$

text: Which of the following reactions is NOT correctly represented?

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Analysis of Reaction Options:}}$

$\bullet$ $\textbf{Option A (Incorrect):}$ Free radical bromination of $\textbf{1-methylcyclohexene}$ with $\text{Br}_2$ and $\text{h}\nu$ occurs at the $±b{\text{allylic position}}$ within the ring (C3 or C6) to form a more stable allylic radical. Substitution on the methyl group is not the preferred path for this cyclic alkene.
$\bullet$ $\textbf{Option B (Correct):}$ This is a standard $\pmb{\text{Sandmeyer reaction}}$ where a diazonium salt is converted to an aryl bromide using $\text{Cu}_2\text{Br}_2/\text{HBr}$.
$\bullet$ $\textbf{Option C (Correct):}$ Reaction of toluene with $\text{Br}_2$ under light/heat leads to $\pmb{\text{side-chain substitution}}$ at the benzylic position, forming benzyl bromide.
$\bullet$ $\textbf{Option D (Correct):}$ In the presence of a Lewis acid catalyst ($\text{Fe}$) and darkness, toluene undergoes $\pmb{\text{electrophilic aromatic substitution}}$ to yield ortho and para isomers.

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$ Reaction $\textbf{A}$ is incorrectly represented.

$\pmb{\text{Correct Option: (A)}}$

text: An organic compound $\mathbf{P}$ of molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_3$ gives positive Iodoform test but negative Tollen's test. When $\mathbf{P}$ is treated with dilute acid, it produces $\mathbf{Q}$. $\mathbf{Q}$ gives positive Tollen's test and also iodoform test. The structure of $\mathbf{P}$ is:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analysis of Compound P}}$
$\bullet$ $\textbf{P}$ has a positive iodoform test, indicating it contains a $\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CO}-$ group.
$\bullet$ $\textbf{P}$ has a negative Tollen's test, indicating the absence of a free $\mathrm{-CHO}$ group.
$\bullet$ This suggests $\mathbf{P}$ contains an acetal group protecting an aldehyde.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analysis of Compound Q}}$
$\bullet$ Acid hydrolysis of $\mathbf{P}$ yields $\mathbf{Q}$.
$\bullet$ $\textbf{Q}$ gives a positive Tollen's test, meaning it now has a free $\mathrm{-CHO}$ group.
$\bullet$ $\textbf{Q}$ also gives a positive iodoform test, meaning the $\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CO}-$ group is still present.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Evaluating the Options}}$
$\bullet$ $\textbf{Option C}$ shows $\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{COCH}_2\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OCH}_3)_2$.
$\bullet$ It has the $\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{CO}-$ group (positive iodoform) and an acetal group (negative Tollen's).
$\bullet$ Hydrolysis of the acetal $\mathrm{-CH(OCH_3)_2}$ yields the aldehyde $\mathrm{-CHO}$.
$\bullet$ The resulting molecule $\mathrm{Q}$ is $\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{COCH}_2\mathrm{CHO}$, which gives both tests.

Therefore, the structure of $\mathbf{P}$ is $\mathrm{CH}_3\mathrm{COCH}_2\mathrm{CH}(\mathrm{OCH}_3)_2$.

Final Answer: $\boxed{C}$

text:
Identify the structure of compound $\mathbf{A}$ based on the following reaction sequence:



Choose the correct option:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Analysis of Reaction Products:}}$

1. $\textbf{Hydrogenation:}$ Compound $\mathbf{A}$ reacts with $2\text{ mol}$ of $\text{H}_2$ in the presence of $\text{Pt}$ to form $\textbf{decalin}$ (bicyclo[4.4.0]decane). This confirms that $\mathbf{A}$ is a bicyclic system containing $\textbf{two double bonds}$.

2. $\textbf{Oxidative Cleavage:}$ Reaction with hot $\text{KMnO}_4$ causes oxidative cleavage of the double bonds. The products formed are $\textbf{cyclohexane-1,2-dicarboxylic acid}$ and $\textbf{oxalic acid}$ ($\text{HOOC-COOH}$).

$\pmb{\text{Structural Determination:}}$

$\bullet$ To obtain $\textbf{cyclohexane-1,2-dicarboxylic acid}$, the six-membered saturated ring must remain intact, and the cleavage must occur at the points where it is fused to the second ring.
$\bullet$ The presence of $\textbf{oxalic acid}$ (a 2-carbon dicarboxylic acid) as the other product indicates that the second ring contained two carbons between its two double bonds, which were cleaved.
$\bullet$ In $\textbf{Option A}$ (1,2,3,4,5,8-hexahydronaphthalene), the double bonds are located such that vigorous oxidation breaks the unsaturated ring specifically at the double bond positions. This leaves the saturated cyclohexane ring with carboxyl groups at positions 1 and 2, and releases the 2-carbon fragment as oxalic acid.

Therefore, the structure in $\textbf{Option A}$ is the only one consistent with both the hydrogenation and the specific oxidative cleavage products.

Final Answer: $\boxed{A}$

text:
The unsaturated ether $\text{CH}_3-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{O}-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)=\text{CH}_2$ on acidic hydrolysis produces carbonyl compounds as shown below:

$$\text{CH}_3-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{O}-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)=\text{CH}_2 \xrightarrow[\Delta]{\text{H}_3\text{O}^{(+)}} \text{P} + \text{Q}$$

Based on this, predict the reagent that will help to distinguish the products 'P' and 'Q' obtained in the reaction:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Reaction Analysis:}}$

Acidic hydrolysis of vinyl ethers involves the addition of water across the double bonds followed by the elimination of alcohol/phenol to form carbonyl compounds. The hydrolysis occurs at both vinyl ether linkages in the starting material:

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} 
\hline 
\textbf{Fragment} & \textbf{Hydrolysis Product} \\ 
\hline 
\text{Left part: } \text{CH}_3-\text{CH}=\text{CH}-\text{O}- & \text{Propanal: } \text{CH}_3-\text{CH}_2-\text{CHO (P)} \\ 
\hline 
\text{Right part: } -\text{O}-\text{C}(\text{CH}_3)=\text{CH}_2 & \text{Acetone: } \text{CH}_3-\text{CO}-\text{CH}_3 \text{ (Q)} \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Distinguishing P and Q:}}$

$\bullet$ $\text{P}$ is an $\textbf{aldehyde}$ (propanal) and $\text{Q}$ is a $\textbf{ketone}$ (acetone).
$\bullet$ $\textbf{Fehling solution}$ is used to distinguish between aldehydes and ketones. Aldehydes (except benzaldehyde) reduce Fehling solution to give a red precipitate of $\text{Cu}_2\text{O}$, while ketones do not react.
$\bullet$ $\textbf{Lucas reagent}$ is used for alcohols, not carbonyls.
$\bullet$ $\textbf{2,4-DNP}$ reacts with both aldehydes and ketones to give colored precipitates.
$\bullet$ $\textbf{Saturated } \text{NaHSO}_3$ reacts with most aldehydes and methyl ketones, making it less suitable for distinguishing these specific products compared to Fehling solution.

$\pmb{\text{Correct Option: B}}$

text:
Given below are two statements :
$\textbf{Statement I }$: Cross aldol condensation between two different aldehydes will always produce four different products.
$\textbf{Statement II }$: When semicarbazide reacts with a mixture of benzaldehyde and acetophenone under optimum pH , it forms a condensation product with acetophenone only.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below

options.text: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true

solution.text:
$\pmb{Validation\ of\ Statement\ I}$

$\bullet$ Aldol condensation requires $\alpha$-hydrogen atoms.
$\bullet$ Cross aldol yields four products only if both aldehydes have $\alpha$-hydrogens (two self and two cross).
$\bullet$ If one aldehyde lacks $\alpha$-hydrogens (e.g., benzaldehyde), only two products are typically formed.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{Result:}$ Statement I is False (due to the word 'always').

$\pmb{Validation\ of\ Statement\ II}$

$\bullet$ Semicarbazide reacts with carbonyls to form semicarbazones.
$\bullet$ Aldehydes are more reactive than ketones due to lower steric hindrance and higher electrophilicity.
$\bullet$ Benzaldehyde (aldehyde) reacts faster than acetophenone (ketone).
$\bullet$ $\pmb{Result:}$ Statement II is False (benzaldehyde reacts preferentially).

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} 
\hline 
\pmb{Statement} & \pmb{Status} \\ 
\hline 
Statement\ I & False\ (Depends\ on\ \alpha-H\ availability) \\ 
\hline 
Statement\ II & False\ (Aldehydes\ are\ more\ reactive) \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{Correct\ Option:\ (B)}$

text:
Consider the following two reactions A and B.
Numerical value of [molar mass of x + molar mass of y] is ____

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Identify gas x from Reaction (A)}}$

$\bullet$ Phenol reacts with active metals such as sodium ($\mathrm{Na}$) to form sodium phenoxide and release hydrogen gas.

$\bullet$ Reaction: $\mathrm{2C_6H_5OH + 2Na \rightarrow 2C_6H_5ONa + H_2(g)}$.

$\bullet$ Thus, gas $\pmb{\text{x}}$ is $\pmb{\mathrm{H_2}}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Molar mass of x}}$ ($\mathrm{H_2}$) = $2 \times 1 = \pmb{2 \text{ g/mol}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Identify gas y from Reaction (B)}}$

$\bullet$ Benzoic acid reacts with sodium bicarbonate ($\mathrm{NaHCO_3}$) to form sodium benzoate, water, and carbon dioxide gas.

$\bullet$ Reaction: $\mathrm{C_6H_5COOH + NaHCO_3 \rightarrow C_6H_5COONa + H_2O + CO_2(g)}$.

$\bullet$ Thus, gas $\pmb{\text{y}}$ is $\pmb{\mathrm{CO_2}}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Molar mass of y}}$ ($\mathrm{CO_2}$) = $12 + (2 \times 16) = \pmb{44 \text{ g/mol}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate the Final Sum}}$

$\bullet$ Numerical value = [molar mass of x + molar mass of y].

$\bullet$ Sum = $2 + 44 = \pmb{46}$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{46}$.

text: A student is given four compounds (A, B, C, and D) and asked to identify which one gives a positive test with Tollen's reagent. Based on the chemical structures provided, which compound is it?

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Analysis of Structures:}}$

$$\begin{array}{|c|l|l|} 
\hline 
\textbf{Compound} & \textbf{Functional Group Type} & \textbf{Reactivity with Tollen's} \\ 
\hline 
A & \text{Cyclic Acetal} & \text{Stable in basic medium; No reaction} \\ 
\hline 
B & \text{Cyclic Ether (substituted)} & \text{Non-reducing; No reaction} \\ 
\hline 
C & \pmb{\text{Cyclic Hemiacetal}} & \pmb{\text{Positive Result}} \\ 
\hline 
D & \text{Cyclic Alcohol / Ether} & \text{Non-reducing; No reaction} \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Detailed Explanation:}}$

$\bullet$ $\textbf{Mechanism:}$ Tollen's reagent is an alkaline solution of silver nitrate and ammonia. While ketones and ethers are generally unreactive, $\pmb{\text{hemiacetals}}$ (like Compound C) exist in equilibrium with their open-chain aldehyde forms in basic media.
$\bullet$ $\textbf{Ring Opening:}$ In the presence of the base ($\text{OH}^-$) provided by the reagent, the cyclic hemiacetal undergoes ring-opening to form a free $\textbf{aldehyde}$ group.
$\bullet$ $\textbf{Silver Mirror Formation:}$ The resulting aldehyde is oxidized to a carboxylate ion, simultaneously reducing $\text{Ag}^+$ to metallic silver, which deposits as a $\textbf{silver mirror}$.

$$\text{Hemiacetal} \rightleftharpoons \text{Open-chain Aldehyde} \xrightarrow{\text{Tollen's Reagent}} \text{Silver Mirror (Ag)} \downarrow$$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$ Compound C is the only one that can generate an aldehyde group under the test conditions.

$\pmb{\text{Correct Option: (D)}}$

text:
Which of the following statements are TRUE about Haloform reaction?

$\pmb{\text{A.}}$ Sodium hypochlorite reacts with KI to give KOI.
$\pmb{\text{B.}}$ KOI is a reducing agent.
$\pmb{\text{C.}}$ $\alpha, \beta$-unsaturated methylketone will give iodoform reaction.
$\pmb{\text{D.}}$ Isopropyl alcohol will not give iodoform test.
$\pmb{\text{E.}}$ Methanoic acid will give positive iodoform test.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Evaluation of Statements}}$

$$\begin{array}{|c|l|c|} 
\hline 
\text{Stmt} & \text{Reasoning} & \text{Status} \\ \hline 
\pmb{\text{A}} & \text{NaOCl} + \text{KI} \rightarrow \text{KOI} + \text{NaCl}. \text{ Hypoiodite is the active halogenating agent.} & \text{True} \\ 
\pmb{\text{B}} & \text{KOI is an oxidizing agent (it oxidizes alcohols to ketones).} & \text{False} \\ 
\pmb{\text{C}} & \text{Compounds with } \text{CH}_3\text{CO- group (even if unsaturated) give the test.} & \text{True} \\ 
\pmb{\text{D}} & \text{Isopropyl alcohol (CH}_3\text{CH(OH)CH}_3\text{) contains the required group and gives the test.} & \text{False} \\ 
\pmb{\text{E}} & \text{Methanoic acid (HCOOH) lacks the methyl group needed for haloform formation.} & \text{False} \\ \hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Key Analysis}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement C:}}$ In $\alpha, \beta$-unsaturated methyl ketones, the methyl group undergoes tri-iodination followed by cleavage to form $\text{CHI}_3$. The $\text{C=C}$ bond is not cleaved.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement D:}}$ Secondary alcohols like isopropyl alcohol are first oxidized to acetone by the reagent, which then produces the yellow $\text{CHI}_3$ precipitate.

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$ Only statements A and C are correct.

Therefore, the correct answer is A & C Only.

text:
A student performed analysis of aliphatic organic compound ‘X’ which on analysis gave C = 61.01%, H = 15.25%, N = 23.74%.
This compound, on treatment with HNO$_{2}$/H$_{2}$O produced another compound ‘Y’ which did not contain any nitrogen atom. However, the compound ‘Y’ upon controlled oxidation produced another compound ‘Z’ that responded to iodoform test.
The structure of ‘X’ is :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Determine the Empirical Formula of X}}$

We calculate the relative number of moles of each element:
$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|} 
\hline 
\text{Element} & \text{\% Composition} & \text{At. Mass} & \text{Moles (\% / At. Mass)} & \text{Simple Ratio} \\ \hline 
\text{C} & 61.01 & 12 & 61.01 / 12 = 5.08 & 5.08 / 1.69 = 3 \\ 
\text{H} & 15.25 & 1 & 15.25 / 1 = 15.25 & 15.25 / 1.69 = 9 \\ 
\text{N} & 23.74 & 14 & 23.74 / 14 = 1.69 & 1.69 / 1.69 = 1 \\ \hline 
\end{array}$$
$\bullet$ The empirical formula is $\pmb{\text{C}_3\text{H}_9\text{N}}$. 
$\bullet$ Since the compound is aliphatic and reacts with $\text{HNO}_2$ to release nitrogen, it is a $\pmb{\text{primary aliphatic amine}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze the Reactions}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{X } \xrightarrow{\text{HNO}_2 / \text{H}_2\text{O}} \text{ Y:}}$ Primary aliphatic amines react with nitrous acid to form alcohols (Y) and release $\text{N}_2$ gas.
$$\text{C}_3\text{H}_9\text{N} + \text{HNO}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_3\text{H}_7\text{OH (Y)} + \text{N}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Y } \xrightarrow{\text{Oxidation}} \text{ Z:}}$ Controlled oxidation of alcohol (Y) produces a carbonyl compound (Z).

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Z reacts with Iodoform test:}}$ For Z to give a positive iodoform test, it must be a $\pmb{\text{methyl ketone}}$ (or acetaldehyde). Since Z has 3 carbons, it must be $\pmb{\text{acetone (CH}_3\text{COCH}_3\text{)}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Identify X via Back-calculation}}$

$\bullet$ If $\text{Z}$ is $\text{CH}_3\text{COCH}_3$, then $\text{Y}$ must be $\pmb{\text{isopropyl alcohol [(CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{CH-OH]}}$, because secondary alcohols oxidize to ketones.
$\bullet$ If $\text{Y}$ is isopropyl alcohol, the original amine $\text{X}$ must be $\pmb{\text{isopropylamine [(CH}_3\text{)}_2\text{CH-NH}_2\text{]}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
The structure corresponds to Option D: $(CH_3)_2CH-NH_2$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{D}}$.

text:
Consider the following sequence of reactions:

$$\text{Nitrobenzene} \xrightarrow[\begin{matrix} (4) \text{ Br}_2/\text{H}_2\text{O} \\ (5) \text{ NaNO}_2/\text{HBr, } 0-5^{\circ}\text{C} \\ (6) \text{ CuBr/NaBr} \end{matrix}]{\begin{matrix} (1) \text{ Br}_2/\text{FeBr}_3/\Delta \\ (2) \text{ Sn/HCl}/\Delta \\ (3) \text{ pH neutralisation} \end{matrix}} \text{ Major Product (P)}$$

The number of bromine atom(s) in the final major product (P) will be :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step-by-Step Reaction Analysis:}}$

To determine the total number of bromine atoms in the final product $\pmb{(P)}$, we track the substitutions occurring at each stage of the synthetic route.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Step 1: Electrophilic Substitution on Nitrobenzene}}$
Nitrobenzene reacts with $\text{Br}_2/\text{FeBr}_3$. The nitro group $(-\text{NO}_2)$ is a $\pmb{\text{meta-directing}}$ deactivator. One bromine atom is introduced at the meta position to form $\pmb{\text{3-bromonitrobenzene}}$.
$\text{Br count = 1}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Steps 2,3: Reduction and Neutralisation}}$
$\text{Sn/HCl}$ followed by pH adjustment reduces the $(-\text{NO}_2)$ group to an amino group $(-\text{NH}_2)$, yielding $\pmb{\text{3-bromoaniline}}$.
$\text{Br count = 1}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Step 4: Activation and Polybromination}}$
The $(-\text{NH}_2)$ group is a very strong $\pmb{\text{ortho/para-directing}}$ activator. In 3-bromoaniline, the positions $2, 4,$ and $6$ (relative to the amine) are vacant and highly activated. Treatment with bromine water $(\text{Br}_2/\text{H}_2\text{O})$ causes substitution at $\pmb{\text{all}}$ three available positions simultaneously, forming $\pmb{\text{2,3,4,6-tetrabromoaniline}}$.
$\text{Br count = 1 (original) + 3 (new) = 4}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Steps 5,6: Diazotization and Sandmeyer Reaction}}$
The $(-\text{NH}_2)$ group is converted to a diazonium salt $(-\text{N}_2^+)$ and then replaced by a bromine atom using $\text{CuBr/NaBr}$. This replaces the amine group with the $\pmb{\text{fifth}}$ bromine atom.
$\text{Br count = 4 + 1 = 5}$

$\pmb{\text{Reaction Summary Table:}}$

$\begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \textbf{Intermediate Stage} & \textbf{Total Bromine Atoms} \\ \hline \text{After Step 1 (3-Bromonitrobenzene)} & 1 \\ \hline \text{After Step 3 (3-Bromoaniline)} & 1 \\ \hline \text{After Step 4 (2,3,4,6-Tetrabromoaniline)} & 4 \\ \hline \pmb{\text{Final Product (1,2,3,4,5-Pentabromobenzene)}} & \pmb{5} \\ \hline \end{array}$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

The final major product $\pmb{(P)}$ is $\text{1,2,3,4,5-pentabromobenzene}$, which contains exactly $\pmb{5}$ bromine atoms.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{B}}$.

text: An organic compound $(\text{P})$ on treatment with aqueous ammonia under hot condition forms compound $(\text{Q})$ which on heating with $\text{Br}_2$ and KOH forms compound $(\text{R})$ having molecular formula $\text{C}_6 \text{H}_7 \text{N}$. Names of P, Q and R respectively are.

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Identify compound R}}$

$\bullet$ The molecular formula of $\pmb{\text{R}}$ is $\text{C}_6\text{H}_7\text{N}$.
$\bullet$ This formula corresponds to $\pmb{\text{aniline}}$ (phenylamine), where a phenyl group ($\text{C}_6\text{H}_5$) is attached to an amine group ($\text{NH}_2$).


$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Identify compound Q using Hoffmann Bromamide Degradation}}$

$\bullet$ Compound $\pmb{\text{R}}$ (aniline) is formed by heating compound $\pmb{\text{Q}}$ with $\text{Br}_2$ and $\text{KOH}$.
$\bullet$ This is the $\pmb{\text{Hoffmann bromamide degradation reaction}}$, which converts an amide into a primary amine with one fewer carbon atom.
$\bullet$ Since aniline has 6 carbons in the ring, the starting amide must have the same ring and one carbonyl carbon (total 7 carbons). Thus, $\pmb{\text{Q}}$ is $\pmb{\text{benzamide}}$ ($\text{PhCONH}_2$).


$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Identify compound P}}$

$\bullet$ Compound $\pmb{\text{Q}}$ (benzamide) is formed by treating compound $\pmb{\text{P}}$ with aqueous ammonia under hot conditions.
$\bullet$ Carboxylic acids react with ammonia to form ammonium salts, which upon heating dehydrate to form amides.
$$\text{PhCOOH} + \text{NH}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{PhCONH}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
$\bullet$ Therefore, $\pmb{\text{P}}$ is $\pmb{\text{benzoic acid}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Summary of Transformations:}}$

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|} 
\hline 
\text{Compound} & \text{Identity} & \text{Chemical Formula} \\ \hline 
\pmb{\text{P}} & \text{Benzoic acid} & \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COOH} \\ 
\pmb{\text{Q}} & \text{Benzamide} & \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CONH}_2 \\ 
\pmb{\text{R}} & \text{Aniline} & \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{NH}_2 \\ \hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

The names of P, Q, and R are Benzoic acid, benzamide, and aniline respectively.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{B}}$.

text:
A hydrocarbon 'P' ($C_4H_8$) on reaction with $HCl$ gives an optically active compound 'Q' ($C_4H_9Cl$) which on reaction with one mole of ammonia gives compound 'R' ($C_4H_{11}N$). 'R' on diazotization followed by hydrolysis gives 'S'. 

Identify P, Q, R and S (Note: The triangle symbol $\triangle$ in options represents a cyclopropyl group).

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Identification of P and Q}}$

Hydrocarbon $P$ ($C_4H_8$) reacts with $HCl$ to form $Q$ ($C_4H_9Cl$). For $Q$ to be optically active, it must contain a chiral center. 
If $P$ is but-2-ene:
$$CH_3-CH=CH-CH_3 + HCl \rightarrow CH_3-CH(Cl)-CH_2-CH_3 \text{ (2-chlorobutane)}$$

In 2-chlorobutane, the $C$-$2$ atom is a chiral center as it is bonded to four different groups: $-H$, $-Cl$, $-CH_3$, and $-C_2H_5$. 

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Identification of R}}$

$Q$ reacts with one mole of $NH_3$ via nucleophilic substitution to yield $R$ ($C_4H_{11}N$):
$$CH_3-CH(Cl)-C_2H_5 + NH_3 \rightarrow CH_3-CH(NH_2)-C_2H_5 \text{ (butan-2-amine)}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Identification of S}}$

Primary aliphatic amines like $R$ undergo diazotization with $NaNO_2/HCl$ followed by hydrolysis to produce the corresponding alcohol $S$:
$$CH_3-CH(NH_2)-C_2H_5 \xrightarrow[2. H_2O]{1. NaNO_2/HCl} CH_3-CH(OH)-C_2H_5 \text{ (butan-2-ol)}$$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

$P = \text{but-2-ene}, Q = \text{2-chlorobutane}, R = \text{butan-2-amine}, S = \text{butan-2-ol}$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{(D)}$.

text:
Consider the following reaction sequence: 
 The percentage of nitrogen in product '$\text{T}$' formed is $\text{\textunderscore\textunderscore\textunderscore\textunderscore}$ %. (Nearest integer) 
 (Given molar mass in $\text{g mol}^{-1}$ $H: 1, C: 12, N: 14, O: 16$)

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Reaction Pathway Analysis:}}$

$\bullet$ $\textbf{Benzene} \rightarrow \textbf{P:}$ $\text{Nitrobenzene } (C_6H_5NO_2)$
$\bullet$ $\textbf{P} \rightarrow \textbf{Q:}$ $\text{Aniline } (C_6H_5NH_2)$
$\bullet$ $\textbf{Q} \rightarrow \textbf{R:}$ $\text{Acetanilide } (C_6H_5NHCOCH_3)$
$\bullet$ $\textbf{R} \rightarrow \textbf{S:}$ $p\text{-Nitroacetanilide } (O_2NC_6H_4NHCOCH_3)$
$\bullet$ $\textbf{S} \rightarrow \textbf{T:}$ $p\text{-Nitroaniline } (O_2NC_6H_4NH_2)$

$\pmb{\text{Mass Calculation for Product T (} C_6H_6N_2O_2 \pmb{):}}$

$\textbf{1. Molar Mass of T} = (6 \times 12) + (6 \times 1) + (2 \times 14) + (2 \times 16) = 138 \text{ g/mol}$

$\textbf{2. Mass of Nitrogen} = 2 \times 14 = 28 \text{ g/mol}$

$\textbf{3. Percentage of Nitrogen} = \left( \frac{28}{138} \right) \times 100 \approx 20.28\%$

$$\text{Nearest Integer} = \pmb{20}$$

text:
Total number of alkali insoluble solid sulphonamides obtained by reaction of given amines with Hinsberg's reagent is ________.
Aniline, N-Methylaniline, Methanamine, N,N-Dimethylmethanamine, N-Methyl methanamine, Phenylmethanamine, N-propylaniline, N-phenylaniline, N,N-Dimethylaniline, Allyl amine, Isopropyl amine

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Understand the stability of Arenediazonium salts}}$

The stability of arenediazonium salts $(\text{Ar-N}_2^+)$ is enhanced by $\textbf{Electron Donating Groups (EDG)}$ which disperse the positive charge through resonance $(+M 	ext{ effect})$ and decreased by $\textbf{Electron Withdrawing Groups (EWG)}$ via the $-M$ or $-I$ effect.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze the substituents in the given compounds}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{A (-OCH_3):}$ Exhibits a strong $+M$ effect. It increases electron density on the ring, providing the $\textbf{highest stability}$.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{C (-H):}$ Standard reference with no additional substituent effect.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{D (-CN):}$ Strong EWG via $-M$ and $-I$ effects. It decreases stability relative to benzene.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{B (-NO_2):}$ Very strong EWG $(-M, -I)$. It withdraws electron density most effectively, leading to the $\textbf{lowest stability}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Determine the stability order}}$

Based on the electronic effects $(+M > H > -M)$:
$$\text{Order of stability: } A (-OCH_3) > C (-H) > D (-CN) > B (-NO_2)$$

Therefore, the correct stability order is $\boxed{A > C > D > B}$.

text:
Given below are two statements:

$\pmb{\text{Statement I:}}$ The dipole moment of R-CN is greater than R-NC and R-NC can undergo hydrolysis in acidic medium to produce R-COOH.

$\pmb{\text{Statement II:}}$ R-CN hydrolyses under acidic medium to produce a compound which on treatment with $\text{SOCl}_2$, followed by the addition of $\text{NH}_3$ gives another compound (x). This compound (x) on treatment with $\text{NaOCl} / \text{NaOH}$ gives a product, that on treatment with $\text{CHCl}_3 / \text{KOH} / \Delta$ produces R-NC.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:

options.text: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Evaluation of Statement I}}$

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} 
\hline 
\text{Component} & \text{Analysis} \\ 
\hline 
\text{Dipole Moment} & \text{R-CN (alkyl cyanides) have a higher dipole moment (}\mu \approx 3.9 \text{ D) than R-NC (}\mu \approx 3.0 \text{ D).} \\ 
\hline 
\text{Hydrolysis} & \text{Acidic hydrolysis of R-NC produces a primary amine and formic acid, } \pmb{\text{not}} \text{ R-COOH.} \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$\bullet$ The hydrolysis reaction is: $R-NC + 2H_2O \xrightarrow{H^+} R-NH_2 + HCOOH$.
$\bullet$ Since the second part of the statement is incorrect, $\pmb{\text{Statement I is false.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Evaluation of Statement II}}$

Statement II describes a sequence of organic transformations converting a cyanide into an isocyanide:

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|} 
\hline 
\text{Step} & \text{Reaction Type} & \text{Chemical Transformation} \\ 
\hline 
1 & \text{Acidic Hydrolysis} & R-CN \xrightarrow{H_3O^+} R-COOH \\ 
2 & \text{Chlorination} & R-COOH \xrightarrow{SOCl_2} R-COCl \\ 
3 & \text{Ammonolysis} & R-COCl \xrightarrow{NH_3} R-CONH_2 \text{ (Compound } x\text{)} \\ 
4 & \text{Hofmann Degradation} & R-CONH_2 \xrightarrow{NaOCl/NaOH} R-NH_2 \\ 
5 & \text{Carbylamine Reaction} & R-NH_2 \xrightarrow{CHCl_3/KOH/\Delta} R-NC \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$\bullet$ Every step in the sequence is a standard and valid chemical transformation. $\pmb{\text{Statement II is true.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

Statement I is false and Statement II is true.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{Statement I is false but Statement II is true}}$.

text:
A student has planned to prepare acetanilide from aniline using acetic anhydride.
The student has started from 9.3 g of aniline. However, the student has managed to obtain 11 g of dry acetanilide.
The % yield of this reaction is :-

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Write the balanced chemical equation}}$

The reaction involves the acetylation of aniline with acetic anhydride:
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{NH}_2 + (\text{CH}_3\text{CO})_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{NHCOCH}_3 + \text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$$


$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate Molar Masses}}$

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|} 
\hline 
\text{Compound} & \text{Formula} & \text{Molar Mass (g/mol)} \\ \hline 
\text{Aniline} & \text{C}_6\text{H}_7\text{N} & (6 \times 12) + (7 \times 1) + 14 = \pmb{93} \\ 
\text{Acetanilide} & \text{C}_8\text{H}_9\text{NO} & (8 \times 12) + (9 \times 1) + 14 + 16 = \pmb{135} \\ \hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate the Theoretical Yield}}$

$\bullet$ Moles of Aniline used = $\frac{\text{Given mass}}{\text{Molar mass}} = \frac{9.3 \text{ g}}{93 \text{ g/mol}} = \pmb{0.1 \text{ mol}}$.
$\bullet$ From the stoichiometry, $1 \text{ mol}$ of Aniline gives $1 \text{ mol}$ of Acetanilide.
$\bullet$ Theoretical moles of Acetanilide = $0.1 \text{ mol}$.
$\bullet$ Theoretical mass of Acetanilide = $0.1 \text{ mol} \times 135 \text{ g/mol} = \pmb{13.5 \text{ g}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Calculate the Percentage Yield}}$

$$\text{Percentage Yield} = \left( \frac{\text{Actual Yield}}{\text{Theoretical Yield}} \right) \times 100$$
$$\text{Percentage Yield} = \left( \frac{11 \text{ g}}{13.5 \text{ g}} \right) \times 100 \approx \pmb{81.48\%}$$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

The calculated yield is approximately $81.5\%$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{D}}$.

text: The correct stability order of the following diazonium salts is:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Understand the stability of Arenediazonium salts}}$

The stability of arenediazonium salts $(\text{Ar-N}_2^+)$ is enhanced by $\textbf{Electron Donating Groups (EDG)}$ which disperse the positive charge through resonance $(+M 	ext{ effect})$ and decreased by $\textbf{Electron Withdrawing Groups (EWG)}$ via the $-M$ or $-I$ effect.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze the substituents in the given compounds}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{A (-OCH_3):}$ Exhibits a strong $+M$ effect. It increases electron density on the ring, providing the $\textbf{highest stability}$.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{C (-H):}$ Standard reference with no additional substituent effect.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{D (-CN):}$ Strong EWG via $-M$ and $-I$ effects. It decreases stability relative to benzene.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{B (-NO_2):}$ Very strong EWG $(-M, -I)$. It withdraws electron density most effectively, leading to the $\textbf{lowest stability}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Determine the stability order}}$

Based on the electronic effects $(+M > H > -M)$:
$$\text{Order of stability: } A (-OCH_3) > C (-H) > D (-CN) > B (-NO_2)$$

Therefore, the correct stability order is $\boxed{A > C > D > B}$.

text:
Given below are two statements:

$\textbf{Statement I:}$ The reaction of 1,2-dibutylbenzene with acidic $KMnO_4$, followed by $NH_3/\Delta$ and then $Br_2/KOH$ yields $o$-phenylenediamine.

$\textbf{Statement II:}$ $p$-toluidine reacting with $Br_2/H_2O$ followed by $NaNO_2/HCl$ and $H_3PO_2$ yields 3,5-dibromotoluene.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:

options.text: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false

solution.text:
### Analysis of Statement I

The chemical transformations for 1,2-dibutylbenzene are summarized below:

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Step} & \textbf{Reagent} & \textbf{Intermediate/Product} \\
\hline
1. \text{ Side chain oxidation} & \text{acidic } KMnO_4 & \text{Phthalic acid} \\
\hline
2. \text{ Ammonolysis} & NH_3, \Delta & \text{Phthalamide} \\
\hline
3. \text{ Hofmann degradation} & Br_2/KOH & o\text{-phenylenediamine} \\
\hline
\end{array}$$

$\textbf{Statement I is True.}$

### Analysis of Statement II

The reaction sequence for $p$-toluidine involves electrophilic substitution followed by deamination:

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Step} & \textbf{Reagent} & \textbf{Intermediate/Product} \\
\hline
1. \text{ Bromination} & Br_2/H_2O & 2,6\text{-dibromo-4-methylaniline} \\
\hline
2. \text{ Diazotization} & NaNO_2/HCl, 273\text{ K} & \text{Diazonium salt} \\
\hline
3. \text{ Reductive deamination} & H_3PO_2/H_2O & 3,5\text{-dibromotoluene} \\
\hline
\end{array}$$

$\textbf{Statement II is True.}$

### Final Conclusion

Since both reaction pathways correctly produce the stated compounds, the answer is $\textbf{Option A}$.

text: A student has been given a compound 'x' of molecular formula $\text{C}_6\text{H}_7\text{N}$. 'x' is sparingly soluble in water. However, on addition of dilute mineral acid, 'x' becomes soluble in water. 'x' when treated with $\text{CHCl}_3$ and $\text{KOH(alc)}$, 'y' is produced. 'y' has a specific unpleasant smell. On treatment with benzenesulphonyl chloride, 'x' gives a compound 'z' which is soluble in alkali. The number of different 'H' atoms present in 'z' is:-

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Identify Compound 'x'}}$

$\bullet$ The molecular formula $\text{C}_6\text{H}_7\text{N}$ and the property of being soluble in dilute mineral acid (due to salt formation) suggests an amine.
$\bullet$ The reaction with $\text{CHCl}_3$ and $\text{KOH(alc)}$ to produce an unpleasant smelling compound 'y' is the $\pmb{\text{Carbylamine reaction}}$, which is specific to $\pmb{\text{primary (1}^{\circ}\text{) amines}}$.
$\bullet$ Compound 'x' reacts with benzenesulphonyl chloride (Hinsberg's reagent) to give 'z', which is soluble in alkali. This confirms 'x' is a $\pmb{\text{primary amine}}$.
$\bullet$ Given the formula $\text{C}_6\text{H}_7\text{N}$, 'x' is $\pmb{\text{Aniline (C}_6\text{H}_5\text{NH}_2\text{)}}$.



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Identify Compound 'z'}}$

Aniline reacts with benzenesulphonyl chloride to form $\pmb{\text{N-phenylbenzenesulphonamide}}$:
$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{SO}_2\text{Cl} + \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{NH}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{SO}_2\text{NHC}_6\text{H}_5 + \text{HCl}$$



$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Count different 'H' atoms in 'z'}}$

The structure of 'z' is $\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{-SO}_2\text{-NH-C}_6\text{H}_5$. We count the chemically distinct hydrogen environments:

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|c|} 
\hline 
\text{Location} & \text{Hydrogen Types} & \text{Count} \\ \hline 
\text{Benzene ring 1 (Sulphonyl side)} & \text{Ortho, Meta, Para} & 3 \\ 
\text{Nitrogen atom} & \text{N-H} & 1 \\ 
\text{Benzene ring 2 (Amine side)} & \text{Ortho, Meta, Para} & 3 \\ \hline 
\text{Total Environments} & & \pmb{7} \\ \hline 
\end{array}$$

$\bullet$ Ring 1 ($-\text{SO}_2$ attached): 3 types ($H_a, H_b, H_c$).
$\bullet$ Ring 2 ($-\text{NH}$ attached): 3 types ($H_d, H_e, H_f$).
$\bullet$ The acidic proton on Nitrogen: 1 type ($H_g$).

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

There are 7 different types of hydrogen atoms in compound 'z'.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{D}}$.

text:
Consider the following sequence of reactions.
Assuming that the reaction proceeds to completion, then $137 \text{ mg}$ of $4$-nitrotoluene will produce $\text{\textunderscore\textunderscore\textunderscore\textunderscore}$ $\text{mg}$ of $B$.
(Given molar mass in $\text{g mol}^{-1}$: $\text{H}: 1, \text{C}: 12, \text{N}: 14, \text{O}: 16, \text{Br}: 80$)

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Identify product (A) and (B)}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{4-Nitrotoluene}} \xrightarrow{(\text{i}) \text{ Sn, HCl/}\Delta, (\text{ii}) \text{ pH neutralisation}}$ $\pmb{\text{(A)}}$: The nitro group $(-\text{NO}_2)$ is reduced to an amino group $(-\text{NH}_2)$, forming $\pmb{\text{p-toluidine}}$ $(C_7H_9N)$.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{(A)}} \xrightarrow{(\text{i}) (\text{H}_3\text{CCO})_2\text{O}, (\text{ii}) \text{ Br}_2/\text{AcOH}}$ $\pmb{\text{(B)}}$:
    1. Reaction with acetic anhydride protects the amine as an acetanilide: $\pmb{\text{N-acetyl-p-toluidine}}$.
    2. Bromination with $\text{Br}_2/\text{AcOH}$ occurs at the $\text{ortho}$ position to the activating acetamido group.
    Product $\pmb{\text{(B)}}$ is $\pmb{\text{2-bromo-4-methylacetanilide}}$ $(C_9H_{10}BrNO)$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate Molar Masses}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Molar mass of 4-nitrotoluene}}$ $(C_7H_7NO_2)$:
    $$(7 \times 12) + (7 \times 1) + 14 + (2 \times 16) = 84 + 7 + 14 + 32 = 137 \text{ g/mol}$$
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Molar mass of Product (B)}}$ $(C_9H_{10}BrNO)$:
    $$(9 \times 12) + (10 \times 1) + 80 + 14 + 16 = 108 + 10 + 80 + 14 + 16 = 228 \text{ g/mol}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate the mass of (B) produced}}$

$\bullet$ Moles of $4$-nitrotoluene $= \frac{137 \text{ mg}}{137 \text{ g/mol}} = 1 \text{ mmol}$
$\bullet$ Since the reaction goes to completion, moles of $\pmb{\text{(B)}} = 1 \text{ mmol}$
$\bullet$ Mass of $\pmb{\text{(B)}} = 1 \text{ mmol} \times 228 \text{ mg/mmol} = 228 \text{ mg}$

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{228}$.

text: 'A' is a neutral organic compound (M. F : $\mathrm{C}_8 \mathrm{H}_9 \mathrm{ON}$ ). On treatment with aqueous $\mathrm{Br}_2 / \mathrm{HO}^{(-)}$, ' A ' forms a compound ' B ' which is soluble in dilute acid. ' B ' on treatment with aqueous $\mathrm{NaNO}_2 / \mathrm{HCl}\left(0-5^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\right)$ produces a compound ' C ' which on treatment with $\mathrm{CuCN} / \mathrm{NaCN}$ produces ' D '. Hydrolysis of ' D ' produces ' E ' which is also obtainable from the hydrolysis of ' A '. ' E ' on treatment with acidified $\mathrm{KMnO}_4$ produces ' F '. ' F ' contains two different types of hydrogen atoms. The structure of ' A ' is

The reactions which produce alcohol as the product are :

$\pmb{\text{A.}}$ $\text{CH}_4 + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Mo}_2\text{O}_3, \Delta}$
$\pmb{\text{B.}}$ $2\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_3 + 3\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{(\text{CH}_3\text{COO})_2\text{Mn}, \Delta}$
$\pmb{\text{C.}}$ $(\text{CH}_3)_3\text{CH} \xrightarrow{\text{KMnO}_4}$
$\pmb{\text{D.}}$ $2\text{CH}_4 + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Cu}/523\text{ K}/100\text{ atm.}}$
$\pmb{\text{E.}}$ $\text{CH}_3\text{-CH=CH-CH}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{KMnO}_4/\text{H}^+}$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze controlled oxidation of Alkanes (A, B, C, D)}}$

Controlled oxidation of alkanes yields different products based on the catalyst used. The specific outcomes are:

$$\begin{array}{|c|l|l|} 
\hline 
\text{Reaction} & \text{Chemical Equation} & \text{Product Formed} \\ \hline 
\text{A} & \text{CH}_4 + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Mo}_2\text{O}_3, \Delta} \text{HCHO} + \text{H}_2\text{O} & \text{Formaldehyde (Aldehyde)} \\ 
\text{B} & 2\text{C}_2\text{H}_6 + 3\text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{(\text{CH}_3\text{COO})_2\text{Mn}, \Delta} 2\text{CH}_3\text{COOH} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} & \text{Ethanoic acid (Carboxylic Acid)} \\ 
\text{C} & (\text{CH}_3)_3\text{CH} \xrightarrow{\text{KMnO}_4} (\text{CH}_3)_3\text{C-OH} & \text{2-Methylpropan-2-ol (Alcohol)} \\ 
\text{D} & 2\text{CH}_4 + \text{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Cu}/523\text{ K}/100\text{ atm}} 2\text{CH}_3\text{OH} & \text{Methanol (Alcohol)} \\ \hline 
\end{array}$$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement C:}}$ Tertiary hydrogen atoms in alkanes are easily oxidized to hydroxyl groups by $\text{KMnO}_4$. $\pmb{\text{Produces alcohol.}}$
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement D:}}$ High-pressure oxidation of methane over a copper catalyst at 523 K is the industrial method for methanol. $\pmb{\text{Produces alcohol.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze oxidative cleavage of Alkenes (E)}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement E:}}$ Strong oxidizing agents like acidified $\text{KMnO}_4$ cause vigorous oxidation and cleavage of the double bond in But-2-ene:
$$\text{CH}_3\text{-CH=CH-CH}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{KMnO}_4/\text{H}^+} 2\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}$$
This produces carboxylic acids, not alcohols. $\pmb{\text{Does not produce alcohol.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

Only reactions C and D result in the formation of an alcohol as the major organic product.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{C and D Only}}$.

text:
Consider the following reaction sequence: 

$$\text{Compound (x)} \xrightarrow[\text{(ii) } \mathrm{H_3O^+}]{\text{(i) } \mathrm{CO_2, NaOH, 120^{\circ}C, high pressure}} \text{Compound (y) (Major Product)}$$

Compound (x) [76.6% C, 6.38% H, vapour density 47] develops a characteristic colour with neutral $\mathrm{FeCl_3}$ solution. Identify the INCORRECT statement from the following for the above sequence.

options.text: Compound y will dissolve in $\mathrm{NaHCO_3}$ and evolve a gas.
Compound x is more acidic than compound y.
Both compounds x and y will dissolve in NaOH.
Both compounds x and y will burn with sooty flame.

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Identify Compound (x)}}$

$\bullet$ First, calculate the molecular weight from the vapour density: $\text{Molecular weight} = 2 \times \text{Vapour density} = 2 \times 47 = 94$.

$\bullet$ Determine the number of atoms: 
    * $\pmb{\text{Carbon (C)}}$: $94 \times 0.766 = 72 \text{ u} \implies 6 \text{ atoms}$.
    * $\pmb{\text{Hydrogen (H)}}$: $94 \times 0.0638 = 6 \text{ u} \implies 6 \text{ atoms}$.
    * $\pmb{\text{Oxygen (O)}}$: $94 - (72 + 6) = 16 \text{ u} \implies 1 \text{ atom}$.

$\bullet$ The molecular formula $\mathrm{C_6H_6O}$ and the positive $\mathrm{FeCl_3}$ test (characteristic colour) identify Compound (x) as $\pmb{\text{Phenol}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Identify Compound (y)}}$

$\bullet$ The reaction of phenol with $\mathrm{NaOH}$ and $\mathrm{CO_2}$ at high temperature and pressure followed by acidification is the $\pmb{\text{Kolbe-Schmitt reaction}}$.

$\bullet$ The major product (y) is $\pmb{\text{Salicylic acid}}$ (2-hydroxybenzoic acid).

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Evaluate the Statements}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement A}}$: Salicylic acid contains a carboxylic acid group ($-\mathrm{COOH}$), which is strong enough to decompose $\mathrm{NaHCO_3}$ and evolve $\mathrm{CO_2}$ gas. This statement is correct.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement B}}$: Salicylic acid (y) is a much stronger acid than phenol (x) due to the presence of the electron-withdrawing carboxyl group and the stability of the salicylate ion. Therefore, saying x is more acidic than y is $\pmb{\text{incorrect}}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement C}}$: Both phenol and salicylic acid are acidic and will react with $\mathrm{NaOH}$ to form water-soluble salts. This statement is correct.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement D}}$: Both compounds are aromatic (containing a benzene ring) and have a high carbon-to-hydrogen ratio, which causes them to burn with a smoky or sooty flame. This statement is correct.

Therefore, the incorrect statement is $\boxed{\text{B}}$.

text:
From the following, how many compounds contain at least one secondary alcohol?
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Understand Alcohol Classification}}$

$\bullet$ A $\pmb{\text{secondary alcohol (2}^\circ\text{ alcohol)}}$ is one where the hydroxyl group ($-\text{OH}$) is attached to a carbon atom that is directly bonded to exactly two other carbon atoms.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze each given structure}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Structure (I)}}$: Contains two $-\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$ groups (primary alcohols) and a carboxylic acid group ($-\text{COOH}$). $\pmb{\text{No secondary alcohol}}$ is present.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Structure (II)}}$: The $-\text{OH}$ group is attached to a carbon bonded to a methyl group and a hydrogen (primary position). It is a $\pmb{\text{primary alcohol}}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Structure (III)}}$: One $-\text{OH}$ is on the ring (secondary position) and another $-\text{OH}$ is on a carbon bonded to three other carbons (tertiary). It contains $\pmb{\text{one secondary alcohol}}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Structure (IV)}}$: This is a carbohydrate derivative. The $-\text{OH}$ groups on the furanose ring and the side chain are attached to carbons bonded to two other carbons. It contains $\pmb{\text{secondary alcohol}}$ groups.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Structure (V)}}$: The $-\text{OH}$ on the right is attached to a carbon bonded to two other carbons (a $-\text{CH}_3$ and the chiral center). It is a $\pmb{\text{secondary alcohol}}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Structure (VI)}}$: Both $-\text{OH}$ groups are attached to carbons bonded to three other carbons. These are $\pmb{\text{tertiary alcohols}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Count and Conclude}}$

$\bullet$ Compounds containing at least one secondary alcohol are $\pmb{\text{(III), (IV), and (V)}}$.

$\bullet$ Total count = $\pmb{\text{Three}}$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{A}}$.

text: A hydroxy compound $(\mathrm{X})$ with molar mass $122 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ is acetylated with acetic anhydride, using a large excess of the reagent ensuring complete acetylation of all hydroxyl groups. The product obtained has a molar mass of $290 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. The number of hydroxyl groups present in compound $(\mathrm{X})$ is:

A mixed ether $(\mathrm{P})$, when heated with excess of hot concentrated hydrogen iodide produces two different alkyl iodides which when treated with aq. NaOH give compounds $(\mathrm{Q})$ and $(\mathrm{R})$. Both $(\mathrm{Q})$ and $(\mathrm{R})$ give yellow precipitate with NaOI . Identify the mixed ether $(\mathrm{P})$ :

$ \text { 3, 3-Dimethyl-2-butanol cannot be prepared by : } $
$ \text { Choose the correct answer from the options given below : } $

solution.text: A, C & D will form 3, 3-Dimethyl-2-butanol

text:
Given below are two statements :

$\pmb{\text{Statement I :}}$ Phenol on treatment with $\text{CHCl}_3$/aq. KOH under refluxing condition, followed by acidification produces $p$-hydroxy benzaldehyde as the major product and $o$-hydroxy benzaldehyde as the minor product.

$\pmb{\text{Statement II :}}$ The mixture of $p$-hydroxybenzaldehyde and $o$-hydroxybenzaldehyde can be easily separated through steam distillation.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below

options.text: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze Statement I (Reimer-Tiemann Reaction)}}$

Reaction of phenol with $\text{CHCl}_3$ and aqueous KOH is the $\pmb{\text{Reimer-Tiemann reaction}}$. The intermediate is dichlorocarbene ($:\text{CCl}_2$).

$\bullet$ This reaction introduces an aldehyde group ($\text{-CHO}$) primarily at the $\pmb{\text{ortho}}$ position due to the stabilizing effect of the phenoxide ion and the transition state coordination.
$\bullet$ Therefore, $\pmb{o\text{-hydroxybenzaldehyde}}$ (salicylaldehyde) is the $\pmb{\text{major}}$ product, and $p$-hydroxybenzaldehyde is the minor product.
$\pmb{\text{Statement I is false.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze Statement II (Separation by Steam Distillation)}}$

The isomers differ significantly in their physical properties due to hydrogen bonding:

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|} 
\hline 
\text{Isomer} & \text{Type of H-Bonding} & \text{Volatility} \\ \hline 
\text{ortho (Salicylaldehyde)} & \text{Intramolecular H-bonding} & \text{Steam volatile} \\ 
\text{para (p-hydroxybenzaldehyde)} & \text{Intermolecular H-bonding} & \text{Non-steam volatile} \\ \hline 
\end{array}$$

$\bullet$ $o$-Hydroxybenzaldehyde forms a 6-membered ring via $\pmb{\text{intramolecular}}$ hydrogen bonding, reducing its interaction with water/other molecules, making it $\pmb{\text{steam volatile}}$.
$\bullet$ $p$-Hydroxybenzaldehyde forms $\pmb{\text{intermolecular}}$ hydrogen bonds, leading to association and a higher boiling point. It is not steam volatile.
$\bullet$ This difference allows for effective separation via steam distillation.
$\pmb{\text{Statement II is true.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

Statement I is false, but Statement II is true.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{Statement I is false but Statement II is true}}$.

text: Structures of four disaccharides are given below. Among the given disaccharides, the non-reducing sugar is:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Identify the criteria for a non-reducing sugar}}$

A sugar is $\pmb{\text{non-reducing}}$ if it lacks a free $\pmb{\text{anomeric carbon}}$ (hemiacetal or hemiketal group). In disaccharides, this occurs when the glycosidic linkage involves the anomeric carbons of both monosaccharide units.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze the structures}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Option A:}}$ This structure represents $\pmb{\text{sucrose}}$. The linkage is between the $C_1$ of glucose and the $C_2$ of fructose. Both anomeric centers are tied up in the glycosidic bond. Since there is no free hemiacetal group to open into an aldehyde or ketone, it is a $\pmb{\text{non-reducing sugar}}$.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Options B, C, \& D:}}$ These structures show linkages (such as $1 \rightarrow 4$) where the second sugar unit retains a free $\pmb{\text{hemiacetal}}$ group at its anomeric carbon. This allows the ring to open and reduce Tollen's or Fehling's reagent, making them $\pmb{\text{reducing sugars}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

Structure (A) is the only one where both anomeric carbons are involved in the bond, leaving no free reducing group.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{A}}$.

text:
In the given pentapeptide, find out an essential amino acid $(Y)$ and the sequence present in the pentapeptide:

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Identifying the Amino Acid Sequence by R-groups}}$
By analyzing the side chains ($R$-groups) attached to the peptide backbone from the $N$-terminus to the $C$-terminus, we identify the following components:

$\bullet$ $\textbf{Threonine (Thr):}$ Characterized by the side chain $-\text{CH(OH)CH}_3$.

$\bullet$ $\textbf{Serine (Ser):}$ Characterized by the side chain $-\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$.

$\bullet$ $\textbf{Aspartic Acid (Asp):}$ Characterized by the side chain $-\text{CH}_2\text{COOH}$.

$\bullet$ $\textbf{Glycine (Gly):}$ The simplest side chain, which is just $-\text{H}$.

$\bullet$ $\textbf{Alanine (Ala):}$ Characterized by the side chain $-\text{CH}_3$.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|} \hline \textbf{Position} & \textbf{Amino Acid Identified} \\ \hline 1 & \text{Threonine (Thr)} \\ \hline 2 & \text{Serine (Ser)} \\ \hline 3 & \text{Aspartic Acid (Asp)} \\ \hline 4 & \text{Glycine (Gly)} \\ \hline 5 & \text{Alanine (Ala)} \\ \hline \end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Identifying the Essential Amino Acid}}$

$\bullet$ Essential amino acids are those that cannot be synthesized by the human body and must be obtained through diet.

$\bullet$ From the list above, $\textbf{Threonine}$ is an essential amino acid, while Serine, Aspartic acid, Glycine, and Alanine are non-essential.

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|} \hline \text{Essential Amino Acid (Y)} & \text{Sequence} \\ \hline \text{Threonine} & \text{Thr-Ser-Asp-Gly-Ala} \\ \hline \end{array}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{D}}$.

text: The number of possible tripeptides formed involving alanine (ala), glycine (gly) and valine (val), where no amino acid has been used more than once is:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Analysis of the Problem:}}$

$\bullet$ We are given three distinct amino acids: $\text{Alanine (Ala)}$, $\text{Glycine (Gly)}$, and $\text{Valine (Val)}$.
$\bullet$ A tripeptide is formed by linking these three amino acids via peptide bonds. Since the problem states that $\pmb{\text{no amino acid}}$ is used more than once, we need to find the number of unique linear arrangements (permutations) of these three distinct units.

$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Apply Permutation Formula}}$

$\bullet$ The number of ways to arrange $n$ distinct objects in a sequence is given by $n!$ ($n$-factorial).
$\bullet$ In this case, $n = 3$ (Ala, Gly, Val).
$$\text{Total tripeptides} = 3! = 3 \times 2 \times 1 = 6$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: List of Possible Sequences}}$

$$\begin{array}{|c|l|} \hline \pmb{\text{No.}} & \pmb{\text{Possible Tripeptide Sequence}} \\ \hline 1 & \text{Ala-Gly-Val} \\ \hline 2 & \text{Ala-Val-Gly} \\ \hline 3 & \text{Gly-Ala-Val} \\ \hline 4 & \text{Gly-Val-Ala} \\ \hline 5 & \text{Val-Ala-Gly} \\ \hline 6 & \text{Val-Gly-Ala} \\ \hline \end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Reasoning for Distinctness:}}$

$\bullet$ Each sequence is distinct because peptide chains have a specific directionality, starting from the $\pmb{\text{N-terminal}}$ (amino group) and ending at the $\pmb{\text{C-terminal}}$ (carboxyl group). Therefore, $\text{Ala-Gly-Val}$ is chemically different from $\text{Val-Gly-Ala}$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{6}$.

text: Both human DNA and RNA are chiral molecules. The chirality in DNA and RNA arises due to the presence of

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Analysis:}}$

DNA and RNA are composed of three main units:
1.  $\textbf{Nitrogenous Base:}$ These are planar, achiral heterocyclic compounds (Adenine, Guanine, Cytosine, Thymine/Uracil).
2.  $\textbf{Phosphate Group:}$ The phosphate unit ($\text{PO}_4^{3-}$) is symmetric and achiral.
3.  $\textbf{Sugar Component:}$ In DNA, the sugar is $\beta\text{-D-2-deoxyribose}$, and in RNA, it is $\beta\text{-D-ribose}$. 



$\pmb{\text{Detailed Explanation:}}$

Both ribose and deoxyribose are pentose sugars that exist in the $\text{D-configuration}$ in nature. These sugar molecules contain multiple asymmetric carbon atoms (chiral centers), which impart chirality to the entire polynucleotide strand. The "D" in D-sugar refers to the stereochemistry of the chiral center furthest from the carbonyl group.

Since the backbone of DNA and RNA consists of repeating sugar-phosphate units where the sugar is inherently chiral, the entire macromolecule becomes chiral.

Therefore, the chirality arises due to the $\boxed{\text{D-sugar component}}$.

text:
Match List - I with List - II.

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|}
\hline
\pmb{\text{List - I (Reaction of Glucose with)}} & \pmb{\text{List - II (Product formed)}} \\
\hline
\text{A. Hydroxylamine} & \text{I. Gluconic acid} \\
\hline
\text{B. Br}_2 \text{ water} & \text{II. Glucose pentaacetate} \\
\hline
\text{C. Excess acetic anhydride} & \text{III. Saccharic acid} \\
\hline
\text{D. Concentrated HNO}_3 & \text{IV. Glucoxime} \\
\hline
\end{array}$$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Reaction with Hydroxylamine (A)}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Mechanism:}}$ Glucose reacts with hydroxylamine ($\text{NH}_2\text{OH}$) to form an oxime. This reaction confirms the presence of a carbonyl group in glucose.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Result:}}$ The product is $\pmb{\text{Glucoxime}}$. $\pmb{\text{(A-IV)}}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Reaction with Bromine Water (B)}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Mechanism:}}$ Bromine water is a mild oxidizing agent. It oxidizes the aldehyde group ($-\text{CHO}$) of glucose to a carboxylic acid group ($-\text{COOH}$).
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Result:}}$ The product is $\pmb{\text{Gluconic acid}}$. $\pmb{\text{(B-I)}}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Reaction with Excess Acetic Anhydride (C)}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Mechanism:}}$ Acetylation of glucose with acetic anhydride results in the formation of a pentaacetyl derivative, confirming the presence of five $-\text{OH}$ groups.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Result:}}$ The product is $\pmb{\text{Glucose pentaacetate}}$. $\pmb{\text{(C-II)}}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Reaction with Concentrated Nitric Acid (D)}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Mechanism:}}$ $\text{HNO}_3$ is a strong oxidizing agent. It oxidizes both the terminal aldehyde group and the primary alcoholic group ($-\text{CH}_2\text{OH}$) to carboxylic acid groups.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Result:}}$ The product is $\pmb{\text{Saccharic acid}}$. $\pmb{\text{(D-III)}}$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

The correct matching is $\pmb{\text{A-IV, B-I, C-II, D-III}}$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{A-IV, B-I, C-II, D-III}}$.

text:
Given below are two statements :

Statement I : Sucrose is dextrorotatory. However, sucrose upon hydrolysis gives a solution having mixture of products. This solution shows laevorotation.

Statement II : Hydrolysis of sucrose gives glucose and fructose. Since the laevorotation of glucose is more than the dextrorotation of fructose, the resulting solution becomes laevorotatory.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :

options.text: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze Statement I}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Optical Rotation:}}$ Sucrose is dextrorotatory ($[\alpha]_D = +66.5^\circ$). Upon hydrolysis, it breaks down into equimolar amounts of glucose and fructose.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Inversion:}}$ The resulting mixture is laevorotatory because the laevorotation of fructose is more intense than the dextrorotation of glucose. This process is called the inversion of sugar.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$ Statement I is true.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze Statement II}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Specific Rotations:}}$ 
    $\pmb{\bullet}$ D-glucose (dextrorotatory): $+52.7^\circ$
    $\pmb{\bullet}$ D-fructose (laevorotatory): $-92.4^\circ$
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Error in Statement II:}}$ The statement claims glucose is laevorotatory and fructose is dextrorotatory, which is the opposite of the truth. Furthermore, it is the magnitude of laevorotation from fructose ($-92.4^\circ$) that outweighs the dextrorotation of glucose ($+52.7^\circ$).
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$ Statement II is false.

$\pmb{\text{Final Summary:}}$

Statement I accurately describes the change in optical rotation after hydrolysis. Statement II correctly identifies the products but misidentifies which sugar is laevorotatory/dextrorotatory.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{Statement I is true but Statement II is false}}$.

text:
The correct statements are :
A. Activation energy for enzyme catalysed hydrolysis of sucrose is lower than that of acid catalysed hydrolysis.
B. During denaturation, secondary and tertiary structures of a protein are destroyed but primary structure remains intact.
C. Nucleotides are joined together by glycosidic linkage between $\mathrm{C}_1$ and $\mathrm{C}_4$ carbons of the pentose sugar.
D. Quaternary structure of proteins represents overall folding of the polypeptide chain.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Analysis of Statements:}}$

$$\begin{array}{|c|l|l|} 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{Stmt}} & \pmb{\text{Scientific Fact}} & \pmb{\text{Conclusion}} \\ 
\hline 
\text{A} & \text{Enzymes lower } E_a \text{ more effectively than inorganic catalysts.} & \text{Correct} \\ 
\hline 
\text{B} & \text{Denaturation breaks H-bonds/disulfide bridges, not peptide bonds.} & \text{Correct} \\ 
\hline 
\text{C} & \text{Nucleotides are joined by phosphodiester linkages (3'-5').} & \text{Incorrect} \\ 
\hline 
\text{D} & \text{Quaternary structure is the spatial arrangement of subunits.} & \text{Incorrect} \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Detailed Verification:}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement A:}}$ Enzymes like sucrase provide a highly efficient biochemical pathway that significantly reduces the activation energy ($E_a$) compared to mineral acid catalysis.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement B:}}$ Denaturation involves the disruption of non-covalent interactions (hydrogen bonds, hydrophobic interactions) that maintain higher-order structures. The covalent peptide bonds of the primary sequence remain unbroken.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement C:}}$ Nucleotides in a nucleic acid chain are linked by $\pmb{\text{phosphodiester bonds}}$ between the $3'$ hydroxyl of one sugar and the $5'$ phosphate of the next. Glycosidic bonds only link the base to the sugar.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement D:}}$ The overall folding of a single polypeptide chain is the $\pmb{\text{tertiary structure}}$. Quaternary structure specifically refers to the relative orientation of multiple polypeptide subunits.

$\pmb{\text{Final Conclusion:}}$

Statements $\pmb{\text{A}}$ and $\pmb{\text{B}}$ are the only correct statements provided.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{A and B Only}}$.

text:
Identify the correct statements.
A. Arginine and Tryptophan are essential amino acids.
B. Histidine does not contain heterocyclic ring in its structure.
C. Proline is a six membered cyclic ring amino acid.
D. Glycine does not have chiral centre.
E. Cysteine has characteristic feature of side chain as $\mathrm{MeS}-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}_2-$.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Analysis of Statements:}}$

$$\begin{array}{|c|l|l|} 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{Stmt}} & \pmb{\text{Fact Check}} & \pmb{\text{Conclusion}} \\ 
\hline 
\text{A} & \text{Essential amino acids include Arginine and Tryptophan.} & \text{Correct} \\ 
\hline 
\text{B} & \text{Histidine contains an imidazole (heterocyclic) ring.} & \text{Incorrect} \\ 
\hline 
\text{C} & \text{Proline contains a five-membered pyrrolidine ring.} & \text{Incorrect} \\ 
\hline 
\text{D} & \text{Glycine has two H-atoms on } \alpha\text{-C; no chiral centre.} & \text{Correct} \\ 
\hline 
\text{E} & \text{Cysteine side chain is } -\text{CH}_2\text{SH. MeS-CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{- is Methionine.} & \text{Incorrect} \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step-by-step Verification:}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement A:}}$ Arginine and Tryptophan belong to the group of 10 essential amino acids required by the human body.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement D:}}$ Glycine ($\text{H}_2\text{N-CH}_2\text{-COOH}$) is the only achiral alpha-amino acid because the central carbon is bonded to two identical hydrogen atoms.



$\pmb{\text{Final Conclusion:}}$

Statements $\pmb{\text{A}}$ and $\pmb{\text{D}}$ are the only true statements.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{A and D Only}}$.

text:
Match $\pmb{\text{List - I}}$ with $\pmb{\text{List - II}}$ according to their molecular shapes:

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \text{List - I} & \text{List - II} \\ \hline \text{A. } \mathrm{XeO}_3 & \text{I. } \mathrm{BrF}_5 \\ \text{B. } \mathrm{XeF}_2 & \text{II. } \mathrm{NH}_3 \\ \text{C. } \mathrm{XeO}_2\mathrm{F}_2 & \text{III. } [\mathrm{I}_3]^− \\ \text{D. } \mathrm{XeOF}_4 & \text{IV. } \mathrm{SF}_4 \\ \hline \end{array}$$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Calculate Total Electrons and Bond Order}}$

Using Molecular Orbital (MO) theory, the bond order is determined by the total number of electrons. For species with $13$ to $17$ electrons, we can use the following relationship where the bond order decreases by $0.5$ for every electron added to or removed from the stable $\mathrm{N}_2$ configuration ($14$ electrons, B.O. = $3.0$):

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline \text{Total Electrons} & 13 & 14 & 15 & 16 & 17 \\ \hline \text{Bond Order} & 2.5 & 3.0 & 2.5 & 2.0 & 1.5 \\ \hline \end{array}$$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\mathrm{O}_2^{+}:}$ $16 - 1 = 15$ electrons. $\pmb{\text{Bond Order} = 2.5}$.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\mathrm{N}_2^{-}:}$ $14 + 1 = 15$ electrons. $\pmb{\text{Bond Order} = 2.5}$.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\mathrm{O}_2^{-}:}$ $16 + 1 = 17$ electrons. $\pmb{\text{Bond Order} = 1.5}$.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\mathrm{N}_2^{+}:}$ $14 - 1 = 13$ electrons. $\pmb{\text{Bond Order} = 2.5}$.



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Determine Magnetic Character}}$

Any species with an $\pmb{\text{odd number}}$ of total electrons must have at least one unpaired electron in its molecular orbitals and is therefore $\pmb{\text{paramagnetic}}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\mathrm{O}_2^{+}}$ ($15$ electrons) has an odd number of electrons $\rightarrow$ $\pmb{\text{Paramagnetic}}$.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\mathrm{N}_2^{-}}$ ($15$ electrons) has an odd number of electrons $\rightarrow$ $\pmb{\text{Paramagnetic}}$.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\mathrm{N}_2^{2-}}$ ($16$ electrons) has an even number of electrons, but like $\mathrm{O}_2$, it is $\pmb{\text{paramagnetic}}$ due to two unpaired electrons in $\pi^*$ orbitals.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Compare results with options}}$

In $\pmb{\text{Option D}}$, both $\mathrm{O}_2^{+}$ and $\mathrm{N}_2^{-}$ possess $15$ electrons each, resulting in the $\pmb{\text{same bond order (2.5)}}$ and $\pmb{\text{paramagnetic character}}$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{D}}$.

text: The formal charges on the atoms marked as (1) to (4) in the Lewis representation of $\text{HNO}_3$ molecule respectively are:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Conceptual Background:}}$

The formal charge (F.C.) of an atom in a Lewis structure is calculated using the formula:
$$\text{F.C.} = [\text{Total valence electrons}] - [\text{Non-bonding (lone pair) electrons}] - \frac{1}{2}[\text{Bonding (shared) electrons}]$$

$\pmb{\text{Analysis of HNO}_3 \text{ Structure:}}$

In the standard Lewis structure of nitric acid:
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Atom 1 (Oxygen in O-H):}}$ Has 2 lone pairs (4 electrons) and 2 single bonds.
$\text{F.C.} = 6 - 4 - \frac{1}{2}(4) = 0$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Atom 2 (Nitrogen):}}$ Has 0 lone pairs and 4 bonds (one double, two single).
$\text{F.C.} = 5 - 0 - \frac{1}{2}(8) = +1$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Atom 3 (Oxygen double-bonded to N):}}$ Has 2 lone pairs (4 electrons) and 1 double bond.
$\text{F.C.} = 6 - 4 - \frac{1}{2}(4) = 0$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Atom 4 (Oxygen single-bonded to N):}}$ Has 3 lone pairs (6 electrons) and 1 single bond.
$\text{F.C.} = 6 - 6 - \frac{1}{2}(2) = -1$

$\pmb{\text{Summary Table:}}$

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|} 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{Atom}} & \pmb{\text{Calculation}} & \pmb{\text{Formal Charge}} \\ 
\hline 
\text{O (1)} & 6 - 4 - 2 & 0 \\ 
\hline 
\text{N (2)} & 5 - 0 - 4 & +1 \\ 
\hline 
\text{O (3)} & 6 - 4 - 2 & 0 \\ 
\hline 
\text{O (4)} & 6 - 6 - 1 & -1 \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

The formal charges are $0, +1, 0, -1$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{C}}$.

text: The correct increasing order of $\text{C–H(A)}$, $\text{C–O(B)}$, $\text{C=O(C)}$ and $\text{C≡N(D)}$ bonds in terms of covalent bond length is:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Analysis of Bond Length Factors:}}$

Bond length is primarily influenced by two factors: **Bond Order** (multiplicity) and the **Atomic Radii** of the bonded atoms.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Effect of Bond Order:}}$ As bond order increases, the attractive force between the nuclei increases, pulling them closer together. Generally, $\text{Single Bond} > \text{Double Bond} > \text{Triple Bond}$.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Effect of Atomic Radii:}}$ Smaller atoms form shorter bonds. Hydrogen has the smallest atomic radius among the atoms listed (H, C, N, O).



$\pmb{\text{Comparison of the given bonds:}}$

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|} 
\hline
