$\bullet$ The tetracyanocuprate(I) complex is extremely stable ($K_{\text{instability}}$ is very low).
$\bullet$ When $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ gas is passed through this solution, the concentration of free $\text{Cu}^+$ ions is so low that the ionic product does not exceed the solubility product ($K_{\text{sp}}$) of $\text{Cu}_2\text{S}$.
$\bullet$ Consequently, $\pmb{\text{no precipitate}}$ of metal sulphide is formed.

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
Since no metal sulphide (MS) is precipitated due to the stability of the cyanide complex, the amount formed is $0 \text{ mol}$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{0}$.

text:
Given below are two statements:

$\textbf{Statement I :}$ Crystal Field Stabilization Energy (CFSE) of $[\text{Cr}(\text{H}_2\text{O})_6]^{2+}$ is greater than that of $[\text{Mn}(\text{H}_2\text{O})_6]^{2+}$.

$\textbf{Statement II :}$ Potassium ferricyanide has a greater spin-only magnetic moment than sodium ferrocyanide.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:

options.text: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze Statement I (CFSE Comparison)}}$

Both complexes contain weak field ligands $(\text{H}_2\text{O})$, so they will follow high-spin configurations.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{[\text{Cr}(\text{H}_2\text{O})_6]^{2+}:}$ $\text{Cr}^{2+}$ is a $3d^4$ system. 
Configuration: $t_{2g}^3 e_g^1$.
$$\text{CFSE} = [3 \times (-0.4) + 1 \times (0.6)]\Delta_o = -0.6\Delta_o$$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{[\text{Mn}(\text{H}_2\text{O})_6]^{2+}:}$ $\text{Mn}^{2+}$ is a $3d^5$ system. 
Configuration: $t_{2g}^3 e_g^2$.
$$\text{CFSE} = [3 \times (-0.4) + 2 \times (0.6)]\Delta_o = 0\Delta_o$$



$\bullet$ Magnitude-wise, $0.6\Delta_o > 0\Delta_o$. $\textbf{Statement I is True.}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze Statement II (Magnetic Moment Comparison)}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Potassium ferricyanide } 	ext{K}_3[\text{Fe}(\text{CN})_6]:}$ Contains $\text{Fe}^{3+}$ $(3d^5)$. Since $\text{CN}^-$ is a strong field ligand, it is low spin.
Configuration: $t_{2g}^5 e_g^0$.
Number of unpaired electrons $(n) = 1$.
$$\mu = \sqrt{1(1+2)} = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.73 \text{ BM}$$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Sodium ferrocyanide } 	ext{Na}_4[\text{Fe}(\text{CN})_6]:}$ Contains $\text{Fe}^{2+}$ $(3d^6)$. Since $\text{CN}^-$ is a strong field ligand, it is low spin.
Configuration: $t_{2g}^6 e_g^0$.
Number of unpaired electrons $(n) = 0$.
$$\mu = \sqrt{0(0+2)} = 0 \text{ BM}$$

$\begin{array}{|l|l|c|c|} \hline \textbf{Compound} & \textbf{Metal Ion} & \textbf{Unpaired } e^- & \mu \text{ (BM)} \\ \hline \text{Potassium ferricyanide} & \text{Fe}^{3+} (d^5, \text{LS}) & 1 & 1.73 \\ \hline \text{Sodium ferrocyanide} & \text{Fe}^{2+} (d^6, \text{LS}) & 0 & 0 \\ \hline \end{array}$

$\bullet$ Potassium ferricyanide has a higher magnetic moment. $\textbf{Statement II is True.}$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
Both Statement I and Statement II are true.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{B}}$.

text:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : Among $\left[\text{Cu}\left(\text{NH}_3\right)_4\right]^{2+},\left[\text{Ni}(\text{en})_3\right]^{2+},\left[\text{Ni}\left(\text{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{2+}$ and $\left[\text{Mn}\left(\text{H}_2 \text{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}$, $\left[\text{Mn}\left(\text{H}_2 \text{O}\right)_6\right]^{2+}$ has the maximum number of unpaired electrons.
Statement II : The number of pairs among $\left\{\left[\text{NiCl}_4\right]^{2-},\left[\text{Ni}(\text{CO})_4\right]\right\},\left\{\left[\text{NiCl}_4\right]^{2-},\left[\text{Ni}(\text{CN})_4\right]^{2-}\right\}$ and $\left\{\left[\text{Ni}(\text{CO})_4\right],\left[\text{Ni}(\text{CN})_4\right]^{2-}\right\}$ that contain only diamagnetic species is two.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:

options.text: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze Statement I}}$
We determine the number of unpaired electrons ($n$) for each complex:
$$\begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|} 
\hline 
\text{Complex} & \text{Metal Ion} & \text{Configuration} & \text{Unpaired } (n) \\ \hline 
[\text{Cu(NH}_3\text{)}_4]^{2+} & \text{Cu}^{2+} & 3d^9 & 1 \\ 
[\text{Ni(en)}_3]^{2+} & \text{Ni}^{2+} & 3d^8 & 2 \\ 
[\text{Ni(NH}_3\text{)}_6]^{2+} & \text{Ni}^{2+} & 3d^8 & 2 \\ 
[\text{Mn(H}_2\text{O)}_6]^{2+} & \text{Mn}^{2+} & 3d^5 \text{ (high spin)} & 5 \\ \hline 
\end{array}$$
$\pmb{\text{[Mn(H}_2\text{O)}_6]^{2+}}$ has the maximum number of unpaired electrons ($n=5$). $\pmb{\text{Statement I is true.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze Statement II}}$
We check the magnetic nature of the individual Nickel species:
$\bullet$ $\pmb{[\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-}:}$ $\text{Ni}^{2+} (3d^8)$ with weak field $\text{Cl}^-$. It is tetrahedral and $\pmb{\text{paramagnetic}}$ ($n=2$).
$\bullet$ $\pmb{[\text{Ni(CO)}_4]:}$ $\text{Ni}^{0} (3d^{10})$. It is tetrahedral and $\pmb{\text{diamagnetic}}$ ($n=0$).
$\bullet$ $\pmb{[\text{Ni(CN)}_4]^{2-}:}$ $\text{Ni}^{2+} (3d^8)$ with strong field $\text{CN}^-$. It is square planar and $\pmb{\text{diamagnetic}}$ ($n=0$).



Now evaluate the pairs:
1. $\{[\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-}, [\text{Ni(CO)}_4]\}$: Contains a paramagnetic species. $\pmb{\text{No.}}$
2. $\{[\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-}, [\text{Ni(CN)}_4]^{2-}\}$: Contains a paramagnetic species. $\pmb{\text{No.}}$
3. $\{[\text{Ni(CO)}_4], [\text{Ni(CN)}_4]^{2-}\}$: Both are diamagnetic. $\pmb{\text{Yes.}}$

Only $\pmb{1}$ pair contains only diamagnetic species. $\pmb{\text{Statement II is false.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
Statement I is true and Statement II is false.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{Statement I is true but Statement II is false}}$.

text:
$X$ is the number of geometrical isomers exhibited by $[\text{Pt}(\text{NH}_3)(\text{H}_2\text{O})\text{BrCl}]$.

$Y$ is the number of optically inactive isomer(s) exhibited by $[\text{CrCl}_2(\text{ox})_2]^{3-}$.

$Z$ is the number of geometrical isomers exhibited by $[\text{Co}(\text{NH}_3)_3(\text{NO}_2)_3]$.

The value of $X+Y+Z$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Calculate } X \text{ (Number of geometrical isomers for } [\text{Pt}(\text{NH}_3)(\text{H}_2\text{O})\text{BrCl}])}$

$\bullet$ This complex is of the type $[\text{Mabcd}]$, where $\text{M} = \text{Pt}^{2+}$ and the ligands are four different monodentate species.

$\bullet$ For a square planar complex of type $[\text{Mabcd}]$, three geometrical isomers are possible. These are obtained by fixing one ligand and placing the remaining three trans to it.

$\bullet$ $\text{Result: } X = 3$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate } Y \text{ (Number of optically inactive isomers for } [\text{CrCl}_2(\text{ox})_2]^{3-}])}$

$\bullet$ This is an octahedral complex of the type $[\text{Ma}_2(\text{bb})_2]$, where '$\text{bb}$' is the bidentate oxalate ligand $(\text{ox})$.

$\bullet$ It exhibits two geometrical isomers: $\pmb{\text{cis}}$ and $\pmb{\text{trans}}$.

$\bullet$ The $\pmb{\text{cis-isomer}}$ is chiral (has no plane of symmetry) and exists as a pair of enantiomers, both of which are optically active.

$\bullet$ The $\pmb{\text{trans-isomer}}$ has a plane of symmetry (passing through the chromium and the oxalate ligands), making it achiral and thus optically inactive.

$\bullet$ $\text{Result: } Y = 1$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate } Z \text{ (Number of geometrical isomers for } [\text{Co}(\text{NH}_3)_3(\text{NO}_2)_3])}$

$\bullet$ This is an octahedral complex of the type $[\text{Ma}_3\text{b}_3]$.

$\bullet$ Such complexes exhibit two geometrical isomers:

$\begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \textbf{Isomer Type} & \textbf{Description} \\ \hline \text{Facial (fac)} & \text{Three identical ligands occupy one face of the octahedron.} \\ \hline \text{Meridional (mer)} & \text{Three identical ligands occupy a meridian of the octahedron.} \\ \hline \end{array}$

$\bullet$ $\text{Result: } Z = 2$.

$\pmb{\text{Final Calculation:}}$

$$X + Y + Z = 3 + 1 + 2 = 6$$

Therefore, the final value is $\boxed{6}$.

text:
A chromium complex with a formula $\text{CrCl}_3 \cdot 6 \text{H}_2 \text{O}$ has a spin only magnetic moment value of 3.87 BM and its solution conductivity corresponds to $1: 2$ electrolyte. 2.75 g of the complex solution was initially passed through a cation exchanger. The solution obtained after the process was reacted with excess of $\text{AgNO}_3$. The amount of AgCl formed in the above process is $\text{\_\_\_\_}$ g. (Nearest integer)
[Given: Molar mass in $\text{g mol}^{-1} \text{Cr}: 52 ; \text{Cl}: 35.5, \text{Ag}: 108, \text{O}: 16, \text{H}: 1$ ]

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Determine the Coordination Formula}}$
$\bullet$ Magnetic moment $\mu = 3.87 \text{ BM}$ suggests $n=3$ unpaired electrons, consistent with $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ ($3d^3$).
$\bullet$ A $1:2$ electrolyte implies the complex dissociates into three ions: one complex cation and two chloride anions. Thus, the formula is $\pmb{[\text{Cr(H}_2\text{O)}_5\text{Cl]Cl}_2 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate Moles of the Complex}}$
$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} 
\hline 
\text{Molar mass of } \text{CrCl}_3 \cdot 6\text{H}_2\text{O} & 52 + (3 \times 35.5) + (6 \times 18) = 266.5 \text{ g/mol} \\ 
\text{Moles of complex} & n = \frac{2.75 \text{ g}}{266.5 \text{ g/mol}} \approx 0.01032 \text{ mol} \\ \hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Analyze the Cation Exchanger Process}}$
$\bullet$ The cation exchanger replaces the complex cation $[\text{Cr(H}_2\text{O)}_5\text{Cl}]^{2+}$ with $2\text{H}^+$ ions.
$\bullet$ Only the chloride ions in the $\pmb{\text{ionization sphere}}$ (the two $\text{Cl}^-$ ions outside the bracket) end up in the final solution to react with $\text{AgNO}_3$. The chloride ligand inside the coordination sphere remains bound to the chromium and is trapped in the exchanger.

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Calculate Mass of AgCl}}$
$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} 
\hline 
\text{Moles of ionizable } \text{Cl}^- & 2 \times 0.01032 = 0.02064 \text{ mol} \\ 
\text{Molar mass of AgCl} & 108 + 35.5 = 143.5 \text{ g/mol} \\ 
\text{Mass of AgCl} & 0.02064 \times 143.5 = 2.961 \text{ g} \\ \hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
The mass of $\text{AgCl}$ formed is approximately $2.96 \text{ g}$, which rounds to the nearest integer 3.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{3}$.

text:
Total number of unpaired electrons present in the central metal atoms/ions of
$\left[\text{Ni}(\text{CO})_4\right],\left[\text{NiCl}_4\right]^{2-},\left[\text{PtCl}_2\left(\text{NH}_3\right)_2\right],\left[\text{Ni}(\text{CN})_4\right]^{2-}$ and $\left[\text{Pt}(\text{CN})_4\right]^{2-}$ is $\_\_\_\_$.

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze the electronic configuration of each complex}}$

We evaluate the oxidation state, coordination geometry, and the number of unpaired electrons ($n$) for each species:

$$\begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|c|} 
\hline 
\text{Complex} & \text{Oxid. State} & \text{Geometry} & \text{d-Configuration} & \text{Unpaired (n)} \\ \hline 
[\text{Ni(CO)}_4] & \text{Ni}^0 & \text{Tetrahedral} & 3d^{10} & 0 \\ 
[\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-} & \text{Ni}^{2+} & \text{Tetrahedral} & 3d^8 (e^4 t_2^4) & 2 \\ 
[\text{PtCl}_2(\text{NH}_3)_2] & \text{Pt}^{2+} & \text{Square Planar} & 5d^8 (\text{low spin}) & 0 \\ 
[\text{Ni(CN)}_4]^{2-} & \text{Ni}^{2+} & \text{Square Planar} & 3d^8 (\text{low spin}) & 0 \\ 
[\text{Pt(CN)}_4]^{2-} & \text{Pt}^{2+} & \text{Square Planar} & 5d^8 (\text{low spin}) & 0 \\ \hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Key Reasoning points}}$



$\bullet$ $\pmb{[\text{Ni(CO)}_4]:}$ Nickel is in zero oxidation state ($3d^8 4s^2$). Strong field CO causes pairing and $s \rightarrow d$ shift to $3d^{10}$.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{[\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-}:}$ Weak field $\text{Cl}^-$ leads to a high-spin tetrahedral arrangement with 2 unpaired electrons in the $t_2$ set.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Square Planar Complexes:}}$ All $d^8$ square planar complexes ($[\text{Ni(CN)}_4]^{2-}$, and all $\text{Pt}^{2+}$ complexes) are diamagnetic because the large splitting energy causes all electrons to pair in the lower four $d$-orbitals.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate the Total Sum}}$

$$\text{Total } n = n_{[\text{Ni(CO)}_4]} + n_{[\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-}} + n_{[\text{PtCl}_2(\text{NH}_3)_2]} + n_{[\text{Ni(CN)}_4]^{2-}} + n_{[\text{Pt(CN)}_4]^{2-}}$$
$$\text{Total } n = 0 + 2 + 0 + 0 + 0 = 2$$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
The total number of unpaired electrons present in the given complexes is $2$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{2}$.

text: The crystal field splitting energy of $\left[\text{Co}(\text{oxalate})_3\right]^{3-}$ complex is '$n$' times that of the $\left[\text{Cr}(\text{oxalate})_3\right]^{3-}$ complex. Here '$n$' is $\_\_\_\_$. (Assume $\Delta_o \gg P$)

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Determine the oxidation state and configuration}}$
In both complexes, the oxalate ion ($\text{ox}^{2-}$) is the ligand, and the metals are in the $+3$ oxidation state:
$\bullet$ $\text{Co}^{3+} \implies [\text{Ar}]3d^6$
$\bullet$ $\text{Cr}^{3+} \implies [\text{Ar}]3d^3$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate CFSE for } [\text{Cr(ox)}_3]^{3-}}$
For a $d^3$ ion in an octahedral field, the configuration is always $t_{2g}^3 e_g^0$:
$$\text{CFSE}_{\text{Cr}} = [3 \times (-0.4\Delta_o) + 0 \times (0.6\Delta_o)] = -1.2\Delta_o$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate CFSE for } [\text{Co(ox)}_3]^{3-}}$
The condition $\Delta_o \gg P$ implies a $\pmb{\text{low spin complex}}$. For a $d^6$ ion in low spin, all electrons are paired in the $t_{2g}$ orbitals:
$$\text{Configuration: } t_{2g}^6 e_g^0$$
$$\text{CFSE}_{\text{Co}} = [6 \times (-0.4\Delta_o) + 0 \times (0.6\Delta_o)] = -2.4\Delta_o$$



$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Find the ratio } n}$
We compare the magnitude of the crystal field stabilization energies:
$$\frac{|\text{CFSE}_{\text{Co}}|}{|\text{CFSE}_{\text{Cr}}|} = \frac{2.4\Delta_o}{1.2\Delta_o} = 2$$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
The stabilization energy for the Cobalt complex is $2$ times that of the Chromium complex.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{2}$.

text: The correct statement among the following

options.text: $[\text{Ni}(\text{CN})_4]^{2-} \text{ and } [\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-} \text{ are diamagnetic and } \text{Ni}(\text{CO})_4 \text{ is paramagnetic.}$
$\text{Ni}(\text{CO})_4 \text{ is diamagnetic and } [\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-} \text{ and } [\text{Ni}(\text{CN})_4]^{2-} \text{ are paramagnetic.}$
$\text{Ni}(\text{CO})_4 \text{ and } [\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-} \text{ are diamagnetic and } [\text{Ni}(\text{CN})_4]^{2-} \text{ is paramagnetic.}$
$\text{Ni}(\text{CO})_4 \text{ and } [\text{Ni}(\text{CN})_4]^{2-} \text{ are diamagnetic and } [\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-} \text{ is paramagnetic.}$

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Analysis of Magnetic Properties:}}$

To identify the correct statement, we evaluate the oxidation state of Nickel $(Z=28)$ and the nature of the ligands using Crystal Field Theory (CFT).

$\begin{array}{|l|c|l|c|c|l|} \hline \textbf{Complex} & \textbf{Ox. State} & \textbf{Electronic Config.} & \textbf{Ligand} & \textbf{Unpaired } e^- & \textbf{Behavior} \\ \hline \text{Ni(CO)}_4 & 0 & [\text{Ar}] 3d^8 4s^2 \to 3d^{10} & \text{CO (SFL)} & 0 & \text{Diamagnetic} \\ \hline [\text{Ni(CN)}_4]^{2-} & +2 & [\text{Ar}] 3d^8 & \text{CN}^- \text{ (SFL)} & 0 & \text{Diamagnetic} \\ \hline [\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-} & +2 & [\text{Ar}] 3d^8 & \text{Cl}^- \text{ (WFL)} & 2 & \text{Paramagnetic} \\ \hline \end{array}$

2-, and [NiCl4]2-]

$\pmb{\text{Detailed Explanation:}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Ni(CO)}_4:}$ Since $\text{CO}$ is a very strong field ligand (SFL), the $4s$ electrons pair up into the $3d$ orbitals. The resulting $3d^{10}$ configuration has no unpaired electrons, making it $\textbf{diamagnetic}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{[\text{Ni(CN)}_4]^{2-}:}$ $\text{Ni}^{2+}$ has a $3d^8$ configuration. $\text{CN}^-$ is an SFL, which forces the pairing of electrons in a square planar geometry $(dsp^2)$. This results in zero unpaired electrons and $\textbf{diamagnetic}$ behavior.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{[\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-}:}$ $\text{Ni}^{2+}$ has a $3d^8$ configuration. $\text{Cl}^-$ is a weak field ligand (WFL), so electrons do not pair up. In the tetrahedral geometry $(sp^3)$, there remain $2$ unpaired electrons, making it $\textbf{paramagnetic}$.

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

Only Statement D correctly identifies that $\text{Ni(CO)}_4$ and $[\text{Ni(CN)}_4]^{2-}$ are diamagnetic, while $[\text{NiCl}_4]^{2-}$ is paramagnetic.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{D}}$.

text: Identify the metal ions among $\text{Co}^{2+}$, $\text{Ni}^{2+}$, $\text{Fe}^{2+}$, $\text{V}^{3+}$ and $\text{Ti}^{2+}$ having a spin-only magnetic moment value more than 3.0 BM. The sum of unpaired electrons present in the high spin octahedral complexes formed by those metal ions is _________ .

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Calculate unpaired electrons (n) for each ion}}$

We determine the number of unpaired electrons ($n$) and the corresponding spin-only magnetic moment ($\mu_s$) using the formula $\mu_s = \sqrt{n(n+2)} \text{ BM}$:



$$\begin{array}{|l|c|c|l|} 
\hline 
\text{Metal Ion} & \text{Configuration} & \text{Unpaired (n)} & \mu_s = \sqrt{n(n+2)} \text{ BM} \\ \hline 
\text{Co}^{2+} & [\text{Ar}]3d^7 & 3 & \sqrt{15} \approx 3.87 \\ 
\text{Ni}^{2+} & [\text{Ar}]3d^8 & 2 & \sqrt{8} \approx 2.83 \\ 
\text{Fe}^{2+} & [\text{Ar}]3d^6 & 4 & \sqrt{24} \approx 4.90 \\ 
\text{V}^{3+} & [\text{Ar}]3d^2 & 2 & \sqrt{8} \approx 2.83 \\ 
\text{Ti}^{2+} & [\text{Ar}]3d^2 & 2 & \sqrt{8} \approx 2.83 \\ \hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Identify ions with } \mu_s > 3.0 \text{ BM}}$

From the table, the ions having a magnetic moment greater than $3.0 \text{ BM}$ are:
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Co}^{2+}}$ ($n=3$)
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Fe}^{2+}}$ ($n=4$)

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate the sum of unpaired electrons}}$

We sum the unpaired electrons for the identified ions:
$$\text{Sum} = n_{\text{Co}^{2+}} + n_{\text{Fe}^{2+}}$$
$$\text{Sum} = 3 + 4 = 7$$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
The sum of unpaired electrons for the complexes with $\mu_s > 3.0 \text{ BM}$ is $7$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{7}$.

text:
The wavelength of light absorbed for the following complexes are in the order:

(I) $[Co(NH_3)_6]^{3+}$
(II) $[Co(H_2O)_6]^{3+}$
(III) $[Co(CN)_6]^{3-}$
(IV) $[Co(NH_3)_5(H_2O)]^{3+}$
(V) $[CoF_6]^{3-}$

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Understand the Relationship between Energy and Wavelength}}$

The energy of light absorbed ($E$) is equal to the Crystal Field Splitting Energy ($\Delta_o$) and is inversely proportional to the wavelength ($\lambda$):
$$E = \Delta_o = \frac{hc}{\lambda} \implies \lambda \propto \frac{1}{\Delta_o}$$
$\pmb{\text{Higher } \Delta_o \text{ (stronger field)}}$ corresponds to a $\pmb{\text{shorter wavelength (}\lambda\text{)}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Determine Ligand Strength (Spectrochemical Series)}}$

All complexes feature $Co^{3+}$. The magnitude of $\Delta_o$ depends on the field strength of the ligands. According to the spectrochemical series:
$$\text{CN}^- > \text{NH}_3 > \text{H}_2\text{O} > \text{F}^- \text{ (decreasing order of field strength)}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Arrange Complexes by Crystal Field Splitting Energy (}\Delta_o\text{)}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{III: } [Co(CN)_6]^{3-}}$ (Strongest ligand $\rightarrow$ Highest $\Delta_o$)
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{I: } [Co(NH_3)_6]^{3+}}$
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{IV: } [Co(NH_3)_5(H_2O)]^{3+}}$ (Strength is between I and II)
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{II: } [Co(H_2O)_6]^{3+}}$
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{V: } [CoF_6]^{3-}}$ (Weakest ligand $\rightarrow$ Lowest $\Delta_o$)

Order of $\Delta_o$: $\text{III} > \text{I} > \text{IV} > \text{II} > \text{V}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Determine the Order of Wavelength (}\lambda\text{)}}$

Since $\lambda$ is inversely proportional to $\Delta_o$, the order of wavelength is the reverse of the splitting energy order:
$$\lambda_{\text{III}} < \lambda_{\text{I}} < \lambda_{\text{IV}} < \lambda_{\text{II}} < \lambda_{\text{V}}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{III < I < IV < II < V}}$.

text:
Given below are two statements :

$\pmb{\text{Statement I :}}$ Hybridisation, shape and spin only magnetic moment of $\text{K}_3\left[\text{Co}\left(\text{CO}_3\right)_3\right]$ is $\text{sp}^3 \text{d}^2$, octahedral and 4.9 BM respectively.

$\pmb{\text{Statement II :}}$ Geometry, hybridisation and spin only magnetic moment values (BM) of the ions $\left[\text{Ni}(\text{CN})_4\right]^{2-}$, $\left[\text{MnBr}_4\right]^{2-}$ and $\left[\text{CoF}_6\right]^{3-}$ respectively are square planar, tetrahedral, octahedral; $\text{dsp}^2$, $\text{sp}^3$, $\text{sp}^3 \text{d}^2$ and $0, 5.9, 4.9$.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:

options.text: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze Statement I}}$

In the complex $\text{K}_3[\text{Co}(\text{CO}_3)_3]$, the central metal ion is $\text{Co}^{3+}$.
$\bullet$ Electronic configuration of $\text{Co}^{3+}: [\text{Ar}] 3d^6 4s^0$.
$\bullet$ Carbonate ($\text{CO}_3^{2-}$) is a $\pmb{\text{weak field ligand}}$, so no pairing of electrons occurs.
$\bullet$ With 4 unpaired electrons in the $3d$ subshell, the hybridization involves outer $d$-orbitals: $\text{sp}^3\text{d}^2$.
$\bullet$ Spin-only magnetic moment: $\mu = \sqrt{4(4+2)} = \sqrt{24} \approx 4.9 \text{ BM}$.
$\pmb{\text{Statement I is true.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze Statement II}}$

We evaluate the properties of the three ions using Crystal Field Theory (CFT):

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|c|} 
\hline 
\text{Ion} & \text{Configuration} & \text{Geometry} & \text{Hybridization} & \mu \text{ (BM)} \\ \hline 
[\text{Ni(CN)}_4]^{2-} & \text{Ni}^{2+} (d^8, \text{SFL}) & \text{Square Planar} & \text{dsp}^2 & 0 \\ 
[\text{MnBr}_4]^{2-} & \text{Mn}^{2+} (d^5, \text{WFL}) & \text{Tetrahedral} & \text{sp}^3 & 5.9 \\ 
[\text{CoF}_6]^{3-} & \text{Co}^{3+} (d^6, \text{WFL}) & \text{Octahedral} & \text{sp}^3\text{d}^2 & 4.9 \\ \hline 
\end{array}$$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{[\text{Ni(CN)}_4]^{2-}:}$ $\text{CN}^-$ is a strong field ligand (SFL); $d^8$ electrons pair up in a square planar field. $n=0$.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{[\text{MnBr}_4]^{2-}:}$ $\text{Br}^-$ is a weak field ligand (WFL); $d^5$ remains high-spin in tetrahedral field. $n=5 \implies \mu = \sqrt{35} \approx 5.9 \text{ BM}$.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{[\text{CoF}_6]^{3-}:}$ $\text{F}^-$ is a WFL; $d^6$ remains high-spin. $n=4 \implies \mu = \sqrt{24} \approx 4.9 \text{ BM}$.
$\pmb{\text{Statement II is true.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
Since both statements are factually correct based on coordination chemistry principles, the correct option is C.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{Both Statement I and Statement II are true}}$.

text:
Given below are two statements:
Statement I : The number of paramagnetic species among $\left[\text{CoF}_6\right]^{3-},\left[\text{TiF}_6\right]^{3-}$, $\text{V}_2 \text{O}_5$ and $\left[\text{Fe}(\text{CN})_6\right]^{3-}$ is 3 .
Statement II :
$\text{K}_4\left[\text{Fe}(\text{CN})_6\right]<\text{K}_3\left[\text{Fe}(\text{CN})_6\right]<\left[\text{Fe}\left(\text{H}_2 \text{O}\right)_6\right] \text{SO}_4 \cdot \text{H}_2 \text{O}<\left[\text{Fe}\left(\text{H}_2 \text{O}\right)_6\right] \text{Cl}_3$ is the correct order in terms of number of unpaired electron(s) present in the complexes.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below

options.text: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are true

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze Statement I (Paramagnetic Species)}}$ 
We determine the number of unpaired electrons ($n$) for each species:
$$\begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|l|} 
\hline 
\text{Species} & \text{Metal Ion} & \text{Ligand Type} & \text{Configuration} & \text{Nature} \\ \hline 
[\text{CoF}_6]^{3-} & \text{Co}^{3+} (d^6) & \text{Weak (High Spin)} & t_{2g}^4 e_g^2 \, (n=4) & \text{Paramagnetic} \\ 
[\text{TiF}_6]^{3-} & \text{Ti}^{3+} (d^1) & \text{Weak/Strong} & t_{2g}^1 e_g^0 \, (n=1) & \text{Paramagnetic} \\ 
\text{V}_2\text{O}_5 & \text{V}^{5+} (d^0) & - & d^0 \, (n=0) & \text{Diamagnetic} \\ 
[\text{Fe(CN)}_6]^{3-} & \text{Fe}^{3+} (d^5) & \text{Strong (Low Spin)} & t_{2g}^5 e_g^0 \, (n=1) & \text{Paramagnetic} \\ \hline 
\end{array}$$
$\bullet$ There are $\pmb{3}$ paramagnetic species. $\pmb{\text{Statement I is true.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze Statement II (Order of Unpaired Electrons)}}$ 
We calculate $n$ for each complex in the series:


$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{K}_4[\text{Fe(CN)}_6]:}$ $\text{Fe}^{2+} (d^6)$, Strong Field Ligand (SFL). Configuration $t_{2g}^6 e_g^0$. $\pmb{n = 0}$.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{K}_3[\text{Fe(CN)}_6]:}$ $\text{Fe}^{3+} (d^5)$, SFL. Configuration $t_{2g}^5 e_g^0$. $\pmb{n = 1}$.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{[\text{Fe(H}_2\text{O)}_6]\text{SO}_4 \cdot \text{H}_2\text{O}:}$ $\text{Fe}^{2+} (d^6)$, Weak Field Ligand (WFL). Configuration $t_{2g}^4 e_g^2$. $\pmb{n = 4}$.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{[\text{Fe(H}_2\text{O)}_6]\text{Cl}_3:}$ $\text{Fe}^{3+} (d^5)$, WFL. Configuration $t_{2g}^3 e_g^2$. $\pmb{n = 5}$.

$\pmb{\text{Order:}} \, 0 < 1 < 4 < 5$. This matches the order in the statement. $\pmb{\text{Statement II is true.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
Both Statement I and Statement II are true.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{Both Statement I and Statement II are true}}$.

text: Consider a mixture ' $X$ ' which is made by dissolving $0.4$ mol of $[\text{Co}(\text{NH}_3)_5 \text{SO}_4] \text{Br}$ and $0.4$ mol of $[\text{Co}(\text{NH}_3)_5 \text{Br}] \text{SO}_4$ in water to make $4$ L of solution. When $2$ L of mixture ' $X$ ' is allowed to react with excess of $\text{AgNO}_3$, it forms precipitate ' $Y$ '. The rest $2$ L of mixture ' $X$ ' reacts with excess $\text{BaCl}_2$ to form precipitate ' $Z$ '. Which of the following statements is CORRECT?

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze the composition of Mixture ' } X \pmb{\text{ '}}}$

The total volume of the mixture is $4$ L. It contains:

$\bullet$ $[\text{Co}(\text{NH}_3)_5 \text{SO}_4] \text{Br}$ : $0.4$ mol (Produces $\text{Br}^-$ ions in solution)

$\bullet$ $[\text{Co}(\text{NH}_3)_5 \text{Br}] \text{SO}_4$ : $0.4$ mol (Produces $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ ions in solution)



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Determine moles in 2 L of the mixture}}$

Since the volume is halved (from $4$ L to $2$ L), the moles of each complex in the $2$ L sample are:

$\bullet$ Moles of $\text{Br}^-$ ions $= \frac{0.4 \text{ mol}}{2} = 0.2$ mol

$\bullet$ Moles of $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ ions $= \frac{0.4 \text{ mol}}{2} = 0.2$ mol

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Reaction with excess AgNO}_3 \pmb{\text{ (Formation of ' } Y \pmb{\text{ ')}}}}$

Silver nitrate reacts with bromide ions to form a precipitate of Silver bromide $(\text{AgBr})$:

$$\text{AgNO}_3 + \text{Br}^- \rightarrow \text{AgBr} \downarrow (Y) + \text{NO}_3^-$$

$\bullet$ Moles of ' $Y$ ' $(\text{AgBr})$ formed $= 0.2$ mol.

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Reaction with excess BaCl}_2 \pmb{\text{ (Formation of ' } Z \pmb{\text{ ')}}}}$

Barium chloride reacts with sulphate ions to form a precipitate of Barium sulphate $(\text{BaSO}_4)$:

$$\text{BaCl}_2 + \text{SO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow \text{BaSO}_4 \downarrow (Z) + 2\text{Cl}^-$$

$\bullet$ Moles of ' $Z$ ' $(\text{BaSO}_4)$ formed $= 0.2$ mol.

$\pmb{\text{Step 5: Compare with given options}}$

$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|} \hline \textbf{Option} & \textbf{Statement} & \textbf{Verification} \\ \hline \text{A} & 0.1 \text{ mol of ' } Y \text{ ' formed} & \text{Incorrect (0.2 mol formed)} \\ \hline \text{B} & 0.4 \text{ mol of ' } Z \text{ ' formed} & \text{Incorrect (0.2 mol formed)} \\ \hline \text{C} & 0.2 \text{ mol of ' } Z \text{ ' formed} & \text{Correct} \\ \hline \text{D} & Y \text{ is } \text{BaSO}_4, Z \text{ is } \text{AgBr} & \text{Incorrect (Reversed)} \\ \hline \end{array}$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

The correct statement is that $0.2$ mol of ' $Z$ ' is formed.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{C}}$.

text:
Identify the CORRECT set of details from the following :

$\text{A. } [\text{Co}(\text{NH}_3)_6]^{3+}$ : Inner orbital complex; $d^2 sp^3$ hybridized

$\text{B. } [\text{MnCl}_6]^{3-}$ : Outer orbital complex; $sp^3 d^2$ hybridized

$\text{C. } [\text{CoF}_6]^{3-}$ : Outer orbital complex; $d^2 sp^3$ hybridized

$\text{D. } [\text{FeF}_6]^{3-}$ : Outer orbital complex; $sp^3 d^2$ hybridized

$\text{E. } [\text{Ni}(\text{CN})_4]^{2-}$ : Inner orbital complex; $sp^3$ hybridized

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze each complex based on Valence Bond Theory (VBT)}}$

We examine the oxidation state of the central metal, the nature of the ligand (Strong Field vs. Weak Field), and the resulting hybridization.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{A. } [\text{Co}(\text{NH}_3)_6]^{3+}:}$ $\text{Co}^{3+}$ is $3d^6$. $\text{NH}_3$ acts as a strong field ligand here, causing pairing of electrons. This leaves two $3d$ orbitals vacant for $d^2 sp^3$ hybridization. It is an $\textbf{inner orbital complex}$. ($\textbf{Correct}$)

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{B. } [\text{MnCl}_6]^{3-}:}$ $\text{Mn}^{3+}$ is $3d^4$. $\text{Cl}^-$ is a weak field ligand and does not cause pairing. It uses $4d$ orbitals for $sp^3 d^2$ hybridization. It is an $\textbf{outer orbital complex}$. ($\textbf{Correct}$)

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{C. } [\text{CoF}_6]^{3-}:}$ $\text{Co}^{3+}$ is $3d^6$. $\text{F}^-$ is a weak field ligand. It uses $4d$ orbitals, resulting in $sp^3 d^2$ hybridization (outer orbital). The statement says $d^2 sp^3$. ($\textbf{Incorrect}$)

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{D. } [\text{FeF}_6]^{3-}:}$ $\text{Fe}^{3+}$ is $3d^5$. $\text{F}^-$ is a weak field ligand. It uses $4d$ orbitals for $sp^3 d^2$ hybridization. It is an $\textbf{outer orbital complex}$. ($\textbf{Correct}$)

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{E. } [\text{Ni}(\text{CN})_4]^{2-}:}$ $\text{Ni}^{2+}$ is $3d^8$. $\text{CN}^-$ is a strong field ligand, causing pairing and leaving one $3d$ orbital vacant. This leads to $dsp^2$ (square planar) hybridization, not $sp^3$. ($\textbf{Incorrect}$)



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Summary Table}}$

$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|} \hline \textbf{Complex} & \textbf{Metal Ion} & \textbf{Ligand} & \textbf{Hybridization} & \textbf{Type} \\ \hline [\text{Co}(\text{NH}_3)_6]^{3+} & \text{Co}^{3+} (d^6) & \text{SFL} & d^2 sp^3 & \text{Inner} \\ \hline [\text{MnCl}_6]^{3-} & \text{Mn}^{3+} (d^4) & \text{WFL} & sp^3 d^2 & \text{Outer} \\ \hline [\text{CoF}_6]^{3-} & \text{Co}^{3+} (d^6) & \text{WFL} & sp^3 d^2 & \text{Outer} \\ \hline [\text{FeF}_6]^{3-} & \text{Fe}^{3+} (d^5) & \text{WFL} & sp^3 d^2 & \text{Outer} \\ \hline [\text{Ni}(\text{CN})_4]^{2-} & \text{Ni}^{2+} (d^8) & \text{SFL} & dsp^2 & \text{Inner} \\ \hline \end{array}$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

Only statements A, B, and D are correct.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{D}}$.

text:
The statements that are incorrect about the nickel(II) complex of dimethylglyoxime are :

$\text{A. It is red in colour.}$

$\text{B. It has a high solubility in water at } \text{pH}=9.$

$\text{C. The Ni ion has two unpaired d-electrons.}$

$\text{D. The } \text{N}-\text{Ni}-\text{N} \text{ bond angle is almost close to } 90^{\circ}.$

$\text{E. The complex contains four five-membered metallacycles (metal containing rings).}$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Analysis of Nickel-Dimethylglyoxime Complex [Ni(dmg)}_2\pmb{]:}}$

To identify the incorrect statements, we examine the structural and chemical properties of the complex formed during the gravimetric estimation of Nickel.



$\begin{array}{|c|l|l|c|} \hline \textbf{Ref} & \textbf{Property} & \textbf{Scientific Fact} & \textbf{Status} \\ \hline \text{A} & \text{Colour} & \text{It is a rosy-red precipitate.} & \text{Correct} \\ \hline \text{B} & \text{Solubility} & \text{It is insoluble in water at } \text{pH } 9\text{.} & \pmb{\text{Incorrect}} \\ \hline \text{C} & \text{Magnetism} & \text{Ni}^{2+} \text{ is } dsp^2 \text{ (Square Planar); all electrons paired.} & \pmb{\text{Incorrect}} \\ \hline \text{D} & \text{Bond Angle} & \text{Square planar geometry has angles near } 90^{\circ}\text{.} & \text{Correct} \\ \hline \text{E} & \text{Rings} & \text{Contains 2 five-membered and 2 six-membered rings.} & \pmb{\text{Incorrect}} \\ \hline \end{array}$

$\pmb{\text{Detailed Breakdown:}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement B:}}$ The complex is $\pmb{\text{insoluble}}$ in water, which is the basis for its use in gravimetric analysis. It precipitates quantitatively in an ammoniacal medium $(\text{pH } \approx 9)$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement C:}}$ In $[\text{Ni(dmg)}_2]$, Nickel is in the $+2$ oxidation state $(3d^8)$. Dimethylglyoxime is a strong field ligand, forcing electron pairing. This results in a square planar, $\pmb{\text{diamagnetic}}$ complex (zero unpaired electrons).

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement E:}}$ The structure contains $\pmb{\text{two}}$ five-membered metallacycles (where $\text{Ni}$ coordinates with $\text{N}$). The other two rings are six-membered, formed by $\pmb{\text{intramolecular hydrogen bonding}}$ between the oxygen atoms.

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

Statements B, C, and E are incorrect.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{C}}$.

text:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : $\left[\text{CoBr}_4\right]^{2-}$ ion will absorb light of lower energy than $\left[\text{CoCl}_4\right]^{2-}$ ion.
Statement II : In $\left[\text{CoI}_4\right]^{2-}$ ion, the energy separation between the two set of d-orbitals is more than $\left[\text{CoCl}_4\right]^{2-}$ ion.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :

options.text: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Understand the Spectrochemical Series}}$
The crystal field splitting energy ($\Delta$) depends on the nature of the ligand. According to the $\pmb{\text{spectrochemical series}}$, the strength of halide ligands follows the order:
$$\text{I}^- < \text{Br}^- < \text{Cl}^- < \text{F}^-$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze Statement I}}$
$\bullet$ $\text{Br}^-$ is a weaker field ligand than $\text{Cl}^-$. 
$\bullet$ Therefore, the energy separation ($\Delta_t$) in $[\text{CoBr}_4]^{2-}$ is $\pmb{\text{less}}$ than in $[\text{CoCl}_4]^{2-}$.
$\bullet$ Since energy absorbed $E = \Delta = h\nu$, the complex with smaller splitting absorbs light of $\pmb{\text{lower energy}}$.
$\pmb{\text{Statement I is true.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Analyze Statement II}}$
$\bullet$ $\text{I}^-$ is even weaker than $\text{Cl}^-$ in the spectrochemical series.
$\bullet$ Consequently, the energy separation ($\Delta_t$) for $[\text{CoI}_4]^{2-}$ is $\pmb{\text{less}}$ than that for $[\text{CoCl}_4]^{2-}$.
$\bullet$ Statement II claims it is "more", which contradicts the experimental series.
$\pmb{\text{Statement II is false.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
Statement I is true, but Statement II is false.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{Statement I is true but Statement II is false}}$.

text: A body of mass 14 kg initially at rest explodes and breaks into three fragments of masses in the ratio $2: 2: 3$. The two pieces of equal masses fly off perpendicular to each other with a speed of $18 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ each. The velocity of the heavier fragment is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Calculate individual masses}}$
$\bullet$ Total mass $M = 14 \text{ kg}$.
$\bullet$ Mass ratio is $2:2:3$, so the parts are $4 \text{ kg}$, $4 \text{ kg}$, and $6 \text{ kg}$.



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Apply Conservation of Momentum}}$
$\bullet$ Since the initial body is at rest, the total momentum after the explosion must be zero: $\vec{p}_1 + \vec{p}_2 + \vec{p}_3 = 0$.
$\bullet$ The equal masses ($4 \text{ kg}$ each) fly perpendicularly at $18 \text{ m/s}$.
$\bullet$ Momentum of first fragment: $p_1 = 4 \times 18 = 72 \text{ kg}\cdot\text{m/s}$.
$\bullet$ Momentum of second fragment: $p_2 = 4 \times 18 = 72 \text{ kg}\cdot\text{m/s}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Solve for the third fragment's velocity}}$
$\bullet$ Magnitude of the resultant momentum of the first two fragments: $p_{net} = \sqrt{p_1^2 + p_2^2} = \sqrt{72^2 + 72^2} = 72\sqrt{2} \text{ kg}\cdot\text{m/s}$.
$\bullet$ To conserve momentum, the third fragment must have an equal and opposite momentum: $p_3 = 72\sqrt{2} \text{ kg}\cdot\text{m/s}$.
$\bullet$ Velocity of the heavier fragment ($6 \text{ kg}$): $v_3 = \frac{p_3}{m_3} = \frac{72\sqrt{2}}{6} = 12\sqrt{2} \text{ m/s}$.

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$ The velocity of the heavier fragment is $\boxed{12\sqrt{2} \text{ m/s}}$.

text:
In a perfectly inelastic collision, two spheres made of the same material with masses 15 kg and 25 kg , moving in opposite directions with speeds of $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $30 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, respectively, strike each other and stick together. The rise in temperature (in ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ), if all the heat produced during the collision is retained by these spheres, is :
(specific heat of sphere material $31 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{kg} .{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $1 \mathrm{cal}=4.2 \mathrm{~J}$ )

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Calculate common velocity (} v_f \text{) using Conservation of Momentum}}$
In a perfectly inelastic collision, the two masses stick together. Let the rightward direction be positive.
$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} 
\hline \textbf{Mass 1} (m_1 = 15 \text{ kg}) & \textbf{Mass 2} (m_2 = 25 \text{ kg}) \\ \hline 
v_1 = 10 \text{ m/s} & v_2 = -30 \text{ m/s (opposite direction)} \\ \hline 
\end{array}$$
$$m_1v_1 + m_2v_2 = (m_1 + m_2)v_f$$
$$15(10) + 25(-30) = 40v_f \implies 150 - 750 = 40v_f$$
$$v_f = \frac{-600}{40} = -15 \text{ m/s}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate loss in Kinetic Energy (Heat produced)}}$

$\bullet$ Initial Kinetic Energy: $KE_i = \frac{1}{2}(15)(10)^2 + \frac{1}{2}(25)(30)^2 = 750 + 11250 = 12000 \text{ J}$
$\bullet$ Final Kinetic Energy: $KE_f = \frac{1}{2}(40)(15)^2 = 4500 \text{ J}$
$\bullet$ Heat produced: $Q = KE_i - KE_f = 12000 - 4500 = 7500 \text{ J}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Convert Heat to calories}}$
Using $1 \text{ cal} = 4.2 \text{ J}$:
$$Q = \frac{7500}{4.2} \approx 1785.71 \text{ cal}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Calculate Temperature Rise (} \Delta T \text{)}}$
Using the formula $Q = m_{total} \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$:
$$\Delta T = \frac{Q}{m_{total} \cdot c}$$
$$\Delta T = \frac{1785.71}{40 \times 31} = \frac{1785.71}{1240} \approx 1.44 ^\circ\text{C}$$

Therefore, the rise in temperature is $\boxed{1.44 ^\circ\text{C}}$.

text: A small bob $A$ of mass $m$ is attached to a massless rigid rod of length $1 \text{ m}$ pivoted at point $P$ and kept at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with vertical as shown in figure. At distance of $1 \text{ m}$ below point $P$, an identical bob $B$ is kept at rest on a smooth horizontal surface that extends to a circular track of radius $R$ as shown in figure. If bob $B$ just manages to complete the circular path of radius $R$ upto a point $Q$ after being hit elastically by $\operatorname{bob} A$, then radius $R$ is $\_\_\_\_$ m.

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Calculate the velocity of Bob A before collision}}$
$\bullet$ Bob $A$ is released from $60^{\circ}$ with the vertical. The vertical height drop is $h = l(1 - \cos 60^{\circ}) = 1(1 - 0.5) = 0.5\text{ m}$.
$\bullet$ Using conservation of energy, the velocity $v_A$ at the bottom is $v_A = \sqrt{2gh} = \sqrt{2 \times 10 \times 0.5} = \sqrt{10}\text{ m/s}$.



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze the elastic collision}}$
$\bullet$ For an elastic collision between identical masses ($m_A = m_B$), the bobs exchange their velocities.
$\bullet$ Bob $A$ comes to rest, and Bob $B$ acquires the velocity of Bob $A$: $v_B = \sqrt{10}\text{ m/s}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Determine the radius R for the circular loop}}$
$\bullet$ For Bob $B$ to just complete the circular track, the velocity at the bottom must satisfy $v^2 = 5gR$.
$\bullet$ Substituting the values: $10 = 5 \times 10 \times R$.
$\bullet$ Solving for $R$: $R = \frac{10}{50} = \frac{1}{5}\text{ m}$.

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$ The radius $R$ is $\boxed{\frac{1}{5}\text{ m}}$.

text:
Given below are two statements :
$\textbf{Statement I : }$For a mechanical system of many particles total kinetic energy is the sum of kinetic energies of all the particles.

$\textbf{Statement II : }$The total kinetic energy can be the sum of kinetic energy of the center of mass w.r.t to the origin and the kinetic energy of all the particles w.r.t. the center of mass as the reference.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :

options.text: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Evaluate Statement I}}$
By definition, the total kinetic energy of a system consisting of multiple particles is the scalar sum of the individual kinetic energies of every particle in that system. This can be mathematically expressed as:
$$KE_{total} = \sum_{i} \frac{1}{2}m_i v_i^2$$
Thus, $\pmb{\text{Statement I is true}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Evaluate Statement II}}$
According to the energy decomposition theorem (also known as Koenig's theorem), the total kinetic energy of a system can be split into two distinct parts:
1. The kinetic energy associated with the motion of the center of mass relative to the origin ($KE_{cm} = \frac{1}{2}M v_{cm}^2$).
2. The sum of the kinetic energies of the individual particles as measured relative to the center of mass frame ($\sum_{i} \frac{1}{2}m_i v_i'^2$).

The full expression is:
$$KE_{total} = \frac{1}{2}M v_{cm}^2 + \sum_{i} \frac{1}{2}m_i v_i'^2$$

Thus, $\pmb{\text{Statement II is true}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
Since both Statement I and Statement II are correct, the appropriate choice is Option D.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\pmb{\text{Both Statement I and Statement II are true}}}$.

text: In case of vertical circular motion of a particle by a thread of length $r$ if the tension in the thread is zero at an angle $30^{\circ}$ shown in figure, the velocity at the bottom point $(A)$ of the circular path is ($g$ = gravitational acceleration)

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze forces at the point where tension is zero}}$
Consider the particle at the point where tension $T = 0$. The angle is given as $30^{\circ}$ from the horizontal, which corresponds to $60^{\circ}$ from the vertical. At this position, the component of gravity acting towards the center provides the necessary centripetal force:
$$T + mg \cos 60^{\circ} = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate the velocity squared (} v^2 \text{) at that point}}$
Since $T = 0$ at this point:
$$0 + mg \cos 60^{\circ} = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$
$$mg \left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$
$$v^2 = \frac{gr}{2}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Apply conservation of mechanical energy}}$
To find the velocity $u$ at the bottom point $A$, we compare the energy at the bottom (all kinetic) with the energy at the point where $T = 0$ (kinetic + potential):
$$\frac{1}{2}mu^2 = mg(r + r \cos 60^{\circ}) + \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Solve for } u}$
Substituting the value of $v^2$ and the height:
$$u^2 = 2gr\left(1 + \frac{1}{2}\right) + \frac{gr}{2}$$
$$u^2 = 2gr\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) + \frac{gr}{2} = 3gr + \frac{gr}{2}$$
$$u^2 = \frac{7gr}{2}$$
$$u = \sqrt{\frac{7}{2} gr}$$

Therefore, the velocity at the bottom point is $\boxed{\sqrt{\frac{7}{2} gr}}$.

text: A large drum having radius $R$ is spinning around its axis with angular velocity $\omega$, as shown in figure. The minimum value of $\omega$ so that a body of mass $M$ remains stuck to the inner wall of the drum, taking the coefficient of friction between the drum surface and mass $M$ as $\mu$, is :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze the forces acting on the mass}}$
For the mass $M$ to remain stuck to the wall, we consider the forces acting on it in the vertical and horizontal directions.



$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Normal Force (N):}}$ The wall exerts a normal force towards the center, which provides the centripetal acceleration required for circular motion: $N = M\omega^2R$.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Gravitational Force (Mg):}}$ This force acts vertically downwards, tending to make the mass slide.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Frictional Force (f):}}$ This force acts vertically upwards to oppose the weight. The maximum available static friction is given by $f_{max} = \mu N$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Establish the condition for equilibrium}}$
To prevent the mass from sliding down, the upward frictional force must be at least equal to the downward gravitational force: $f \geq Mg$. In the limiting case for the minimum angular velocity, we use the maximum static friction: $\mu N = Mg$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Substitute and solve for } \omega}$
Substitute the expression for the normal force ($N = M\omega^2R$) into the equilibrium condition:
$$\mu (M\omega^2R) = Mg$$
Dividing both sides by $M$:
$$\mu \omega^2 R = g$$
Rearranging to solve for $\omega$:
$$\omega^2 = \frac{g}{\mu R}$$
$$\omega = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\mu R}}$$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$ The minimum angular velocity required is $\boxed{\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\mu R}}}$.

text: Two cars A and B each of mass $10^3$ kg are moving on parallel tracks separated by a distance of 10 m, in same direction with speeds 72 km/h and 36 km/h. The magnitude of angular momentum of car A with respect to car B is ________ J·s.

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze forces at the point where tension is zero}}$
Consider the particle at the point where tension $T = 0$. The angle is given as $30^{\circ}$ from the horizontal, which corresponds to $60^{\circ}$ from the vertical. At this position, the component of gravity acting towards the center provides the necessary centripetal force:
$$T + mg \cos 60^{\circ} = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate the velocity squared (} v^2 \text{) at that point}}$
Since $T = 0$ at this point:
$$0 + mg \cos 60^{\circ} = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$
$$mg \left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$
$$v^2 = \frac{gr}{2}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Apply conservation of mechanical energy}}$
To find the velocity $u$ at the bottom point $A$, we compare the energy at the bottom (all kinetic) with the energy at the point where $T = 0$ (kinetic + potential):
$$\frac{1}{2}mu^2 = mg(r + r \cos 60^{\circ}) + \frac{1}{2}mv^2$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Solve for } u}$
Substituting the value of $v^2$ and the height:
$$u^2 = 2gr\left(1 + \frac{1}{2}\right) + \frac{gr}{2}$$
$$u^2 = 2gr\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) + \frac{gr}{2} = 3gr + \frac{gr}{2}$$
$$u^2 = \frac{7gr}{2}$$
$$u = \sqrt{\frac{7}{2} gr}$$

Therefore, the velocity at the bottom point is $\boxed{\sqrt{\frac{7}{2} gr}}$.

text:
Observe the following equilibrium in a 1 L flask.

A(g) ⇌ B(g)

At T(K), the equilibrium concentrations of A and B are 0.5 M and 0.375 M respectively. 0.1 moles of A is added into the flask and heated to T(K) to establish the equilibrium again. The new equilibrium concentrations (in M) of A and B are respectively

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Calculate the equilibrium constant (}K_c\text{) at temperature T}}$

Given the initial equilibrium concentrations in a $1 \text{ L}$ flask:
$$[A] = 0.5 \text{ M}, \quad [B] = 0.375 \text{ M}$$
For the reaction $A(g) \rightleftharpoons B(g)$, the equilibrium constant is:
$$K_c = \frac{[B]}{[A]} = \frac{0.375}{0.5} = 0.75$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Determine new initial concentrations}}$

$\bullet$ Initial moles of $A$ at first equilibrium $= 0.5 \text{ mol}$
$\bullet$ Moles of $A$ added $= 0.1 \text{ mol}$
$\bullet$ Total moles of $A$ before shifting to second equilibrium $= 0.5 + 0.1 = 0.6 \text{ mol}$
$\bullet$ Initial moles of $B$ (remaining from first equilibrium) $= 0.375 \text{ mol}$

Since the volume is $1 \text{ L}$, the concentrations are numerically equal to the moles.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Set up the ICE table for the new equilibrium}}$

Let $x$ be the concentration of $A$ that reacts to reach the new equilibrium:
$$\begin{array}{|l|c|c|} \hline & A(g) & B(g) \\ \hline \text{Initial (M)} & 0.6 & 0.375 \\ \hline \text{Change (M)} & -x & +x \\ \hline \text{Equilibrium (M)} & 0.6 - x & 0.375 + x \\ \hline \end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Solve for } x}$

Since the temperature $T$ is constant, $K_c$ remains $0.75$:
$$K_c = \frac{0.375 + x}{0.6 - x} = 0.75$$
$$0.375 + x = 0.75(0.6 - x)$$
$$0.375 + x = 0.45 - 0.75x$$
$$1.75x = 0.075 \implies x = \frac{0.075}{1.75} \approx 0.04286 \text{ M}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 5: Calculate new equilibrium concentrations}}$

$\bullet$ $[A]_{new} = 0.6 - 0.04286 = 0.55714 \approx 0.557 \text{ M}$
$\bullet$ $[B]_{new} = 0.375 + 0.04286 = 0.41786 \approx 0.418 \text{ M}$

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{0.557, 0.418.}$

text:
Consider the following gaseous equilibrium in a closed container of volume '$V$' at $T\text{(K)}$.
$$\text{P}_2\text{(g)} + \text{Q}_2\text{(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2 \text{PQ(g)}$$
$2\text{ moles}$ each of $\text{P}_2\text{(g)}$, $\text{Q}_2\text{(g)}$ and $\text{PQ(g)}$ are present at equilibrium. Now one mole each of '$\text{P}_2$' and '$\text{Q}_2$' are added to the equilibrium keeping the temperature at $T\text{(K)}$. The number of moles of $\text{P}_2, \text{Q}_2$ and $\text{PQ}$ at the new equilibrium, respectively, are:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Calculate the Equilibrium Constant (} K_c \text{)}}$

From the initial equilibrium state:
$$\begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|} \hline \text{Species} & \text{P}_2\text{(g)} & \text{Q}_2\text{(g)} & 2\text{PQ(g)} \\ \hline \text{Initial moles at equilibrium} & 2 & 2 & 2 \\ \hline \end{array}$$

The expression for $K_c$ is:
$$K_c = \frac{[\text{PQ}]^2}{[\text{P}_2][\text{Q}_2]} = \frac{(2/V)^2}{(2/V)(2/V)} = \frac{4}{4} = 1$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze the Effect of Adding Moles (Le Chatelier's Principle)}}$



When $1\text{ mole}$ each of $\text{P}_2$ and $\text{Q}_2$ are added, the new initial concentrations for the second equilibrium are:
$$\text{P}_2 = 2+1 = 3\text{ moles}, \quad \text{Q}_2 = 2+1 = 3\text{ moles}, \quad \text{PQ} = 2\text{ moles}$$

Let $x$ be the number of moles of $\text{P}_2$ that react to reach the new equilibrium:
$$\begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|} \hline \text{Reaction} & \text{P}_2\text{(g)} & + \text{Q}_2\text{(g)} & \rightleftharpoons 2\text{PQ(g)} \\ \hline \text{New initial moles} & 3 & 3 & 2 \\ \hline \text{Change} & -x & -x & +2x \\ \hline \text{Final equilibrium moles} & 3-x & 3-x & 2+2x \\ \hline \end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Solve for } x \text{ using } K_c}$

Since temperature is constant, $K_c$ remains $1$:
$$K_c = 1 = \frac{(2+2x)^2}{(3-x)^2}$$
Taking the square root of both sides:
$$1 = \frac{2+2x}{3-x}$$
$$3 - x = 2 + 2x$$
$$1 = 3x \implies x = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Calculate final moles}}$

* $\text{Moles of P}_2 = 3 - 0.333 = 2.667 \approx 2.67$
* $\text{Moles of Q}_2 = 3 - 0.333 = 2.667 \approx 2.67$
* $\text{Moles of PQ} = 2 + 2(0.333) = 2.666 \approx 2.67$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

The new equilibrium mixture contains $2.67\text{ moles}$ of each species.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{B}}$.

text:
Consider the general reaction given below at 400 K
$$ x \mathrm{~A}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons y \mathrm{~B}(\mathrm{~g}) . $$
The values of $K_p$ and $K_c$ are studied under the same condition of temperature but variation in $x$ and $y$.
(i) $K_p=85.87$ and $K_c=2.586$ (appropriate units)
(ii) $K_p=0.862$ and $K_c=28.62$ (appropriate units)
The values of $x$ and $y$ in (i) and (ii) respectively are :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Calculate the standard Gibbs energy change for the reaction (}\Delta G^\circ\text{)}}$

For the reaction $\mathrm{A}_2(\mathrm{g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{A}(\mathrm{g})$:
$$\Delta G^\circ = [2 \times \Delta G_f^\circ(\mathrm{A})] - [\Delta G_f^\circ(\mathrm{A}_2)]$$
$$\Delta G^\circ = [2 \times (-50.832)] - (-100.00)$$
$$\Delta G^\circ = -101.664 + 100.00 = -1.664 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$
$$\Delta G^\circ = -1664 \text{ J mol}^{-1}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate the equilibrium constant (}K_p\text{)}}$

Using the relation $\Delta G^\circ = -2.303 RT \log K_p$:
$$-1664 = -2.303 \times 8 \times 300 \times \log K_p$$
$$-1664 = -5527.2 \log K_p$$
$$\log K_p = \frac{1664}{5527.2} \approx 0.3010$$
Since $\log 2 = 0.3010$, we have $K_p = 2$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Relate } K_p \text{ to the degree of dissociation (}\alpha\text{)}}$

For the dissociation $\mathrm{A}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\mathrm{A}$:
$$\begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|} \hline & \mathrm{A}_2 & 2\mathrm{A} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{Initial moles} & 1 & 0 & 1 \\ \hline \text{Equilibrium moles} & 1-\alpha & 2\alpha & 1+\alpha \\ \hline \text{Partial Pressure} & \frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha} P_{total} & \frac{2\alpha}{1+\alpha} P_{total} & \\ \hline \end{array}$$



Given $P_{total} = 1 \text{ bar}$:
$$K_p = \frac{(P_A)^2}{P_{A2}} = \frac{\left( \frac{2\alpha}{1+\alpha} \cdot 1 \right)^2}{\frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha} \cdot 1} = \frac{4\alpha^2}{(1+\alpha)(1-\alpha)} = \frac{4\alpha^2}{1-\alpha^2}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Solve for } \alpha \text{ and find } x}$

$$2 = \frac{4\alpha^2}{1-\alpha^2} \implies 2 - 2\alpha^2 = 4\alpha^2 \implies 2 = 6\alpha^2$$
$$\alpha^2 = \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333$$
$$\alpha = \sqrt{0.3333} = \sqrt{33.33 \times 10^{-2}}$$
Comparing this with $(x \times 10^{-2})^{1/2}$:
$$x = 33.33$$
Rounding to the nearest integer, we get $x = 33$.

Therefore, the numerical value is $\boxed{33}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Set up the Equilibrium Table}}$

Let $\alpha$ be the degree of dissociation of $\text{NH}_3$. Starting with $1$ mole of $\text{NH}_3$:

$$\begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|} 
\hline 
\text{Reaction} & \text{NH}_3\text{(g)} & \rightleftharpoons \frac{1}{2}\text{N}_2\text{(g)} & + \frac{3}{2}\text{H}_2\text{(g)} \\ 
\hline 
\text{Initial moles} & 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 
\hline 
\text{Moles at eq.} & 1-\alpha & \alpha/2 & 3\alpha/2 \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Total moles at equilibrium:}}$
$$n_{total} = (1-\alpha) + \frac{\alpha}{2} + \frac{3\alpha}{2} = 1 + \alpha$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Express Partial Pressures in terms of Total Pressure (P)}}$

Given total pressure $P = \sqrt{3}\text{ atm}$.
$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{Species}} & \pmb{\text{Partial Pressure } (p = \text{mole fraction} \times P)} \\ 
\hline 
 p_{\text{NH}_3} & \left( \frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha} \right) P \\ 
\hline 
 p_{\text{N}_2} & \left( \frac{\alpha/2}{1+\alpha} \right) P \\ 
\hline 
 p_{\text{H}_2} & \left( \frac{3\alpha/2}{1+\alpha} \right) P \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Substitute into the } K_p \text{ Expression}}$

$$K_p = \frac{(p_{\text{N}_2})^{1/2} \cdot (p_{\text{H}_2})^{3/2}}{p_{\text{NH}_3}}$$
$$K_p = \frac{\left[ \frac{\alpha/2}{1+\alpha} P \right]^{1/2} \cdot \left[ \frac{3\alpha/2}{1+\alpha} P \right]^{3/2}}{\frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha} P}$$
$$K_p = \frac{ \left( \frac{\alpha}{1+\alpha} \right)^2 \cdot \left( \frac{1}{2} \right)^{1/2} \cdot \left( \frac{3}{2} \right)^{3/2} \cdot P^2}{ \frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha} P }$$
$$K_p = \frac{\alpha^2}{(1+\alpha)(1-\alpha)} \cdot \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{4} \cdot P = \frac{\alpha^2}{1-\alpha^2} \cdot \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{4} \cdot P$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Solve for } \alpha}$

Substituting $K_p = 9$ and $P = \sqrt{3}$:
$$9 = \frac{\alpha^2}{1-\alpha^2} \cdot \frac{3\sqrt{3}}{4} \cdot \sqrt{3}$$
$$9 = \frac{\alpha^2}{1-\alpha^2} \cdot \frac{3 \times 3}{4} = \frac{\alpha^2}{1-\alpha^2} \cdot \frac{9}{4}$$
$$4 = \frac{\alpha^2}{1-\alpha^2} \implies 4 - 4\alpha^2 = \alpha^2 \implies 5\alpha^2 = 4$$
$$\alpha = \sqrt{\frac{4}{5}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 5: Find } x}$

According to the problem, $\alpha = (x \times 10^{-2})^{-1/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{x \times 10^{-2}}}$.
Equating the two expressions for $\alpha$:
$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{x \times 10^{-2}}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}$$
Squaring both sides:
$$\frac{1}{x \times 10^{-2}} = \frac{4}{5} \implies \frac{100}{x} = 0.8$$
$$x = \frac{100}{0.8} = 125$$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

The value of $x$ is $125$.

Therefore, the final answer is $\boxed{125}$.

text:
Dissociation of a gas $\mathrm{A}_2$ takes place according to the following chemical reaction. At equilibrium, the total pressure is 1 bar at 300 K .
$$ \mathrm{A}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{~A}(\mathrm{~g}) $$
The standard Gibbs energy of formation of the involved substances has been provided below:
$$ \begin{array}{|c|c|} \hline \text { Substance } & \Delta \mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{f}}^{\circ} / \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ \hline \hline \mathrm{~A}_2 & -100.00 \\ \hline \mathrm{~A} & -50.832 \\ \hline \end{array} $$
The degree of dissociation of $\mathrm{A}_2(\mathrm{g})$ is given by $(x \times 10^{-2})^{1/2}$ where $x=$ ____ . (Nearest integer).
[ Given : $R=8 \text{ J mol}^{-1} \text{ K}^{-1}, \log 2=0.3010, \log 3=0.48$ ]
Assume degree of dissociation is not negligible.

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Calculate the standard Gibbs energy change for the reaction (}\Delta G^\circ\text{)}}$

For the reaction $\mathrm{A}_2(\mathrm{g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{A}(\mathrm{g})$:
$$\Delta G^\circ = [2 \times \Delta G_f^\circ(\mathrm{A})] - [\Delta G_f^\circ(\mathrm{A}_2)]$$
$$\Delta G^\circ = [2 \times (-50.832)] - (-100.00)$$
$$\Delta G^\circ = -101.664 + 100.00 = -1.664 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$
$$\Delta G^\circ = -1664 \text{ J mol}^{-1}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate the equilibrium constant (}K_p\text{)}}$

Using the relation $\Delta G^\circ = -2.303 RT \log K_p$:
$$-1664 = -2.303 \times 8 \times 300 \times \log K_p$$
$$-1664 = -5527.2 \log K_p$$
$$\log K_p = \frac{1664}{5527.2} \approx 0.3010$$
Since $\log 2 = 0.3010$, we have $K_p = 2$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Relate } K_p \text{ to the degree of dissociation (}\alpha\text{)}}$

For the dissociation $\mathrm{A}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2\mathrm{A}$:
$$\begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|} \hline & \mathrm{A}_2 & 2\mathrm{A} & \text{Total} \\ \hline \text{Initial moles} & 1 & 0 & 1 \\ \hline \text{Equilibrium moles} & 1-\alpha & 2\alpha & 1+\alpha \\ \hline \text{Partial Pressure} & \frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha} P_{total} & \frac{2\alpha}{1+\alpha} P_{total} & \\ \hline \end{array}$$



Given $P_{total} = 1 \text{ bar}$:
$$K_p = \frac{(P_A)^2}{P_{A2}} = \frac{\left( \frac{2\alpha}{1+\alpha} \cdot 1 \right)^2}{\frac{1-\alpha}{1+\alpha} \cdot 1} = \frac{4\alpha^2}{(1+\alpha)(1-\alpha)} = \frac{4\alpha^2}{1-\alpha^2}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Solve for } \alpha \text{ and find } x}$

$$2 = \frac{4\alpha^2}{1-\alpha^2} \implies 2 - 2\alpha^2 = 4\alpha^2 \implies 2 = 6\alpha^2$$
$$\alpha^2 = \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333$$
$$\alpha = \sqrt{0.3333} = \sqrt{33.33 \times 10^{-2}}$$
Comparing this with $(x \times 10^{-2})^{1/2}$:
$$x = 33.33$$
Rounding to the nearest integer, we get $x = 33$.

Therefore, the numerical value is $\boxed{33}$.

text: The total length of potentiometer wire AB is 50 cm in the arrangement as shown in the figure. If P is the point where the galvanometer shows zero reading then the length AP is ________ cm.

A moving coil galvanometer of resistance $100 \Omega$ shows a full scale deflection for a current of 1 mA . The value of resistance required to convert this galvanometer into an ammeter, showing full scale deflection for a current of 5 mA , is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$
The plot of $\log_{10} K$ vs $\frac{1}{T}$ gives a straight line. The intercept and slope respectively are (where K is equilibrium constant).

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Relate Gibbs free energy to the equilibrium constant}}$

The standard Gibbs free energy change is related to the equilibrium constant by the following equation:
$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -RT \ln K$$
Alternatively, using base-10 logarithm:
$$\Delta G^{\circ} = -2.303 RT \log_{10} K$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Use the thermodynamic definition of Gibbs free energy}}$

We also know that:
$$\Delta G^{\circ} = \Delta H^{\circ} - T\Delta S^{\circ}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Combine the equations to isolate } \log_{10} K}$

Equating the two expressions for $\Delta G^{\circ}$:
$$-2.303 RT \log_{10} K = \Delta H^{\circ} - T\Delta S^{\circ}$$
Now, divide both sides by $-2.303 RT$:
$$\log_{10} K = \frac{\Delta H^{\circ} - T\Delta S^{\circ}}{-2.303 RT}$$
$$\log_{10} K = -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{2.303 R} \left( \frac{1}{T} \right) + \frac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{2.303 R}$$



$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Identify slope and intercept}}$

Comparing this to the straight-line equation $y = mx + c$ where $y = \log_{10} K$ and $x = \frac{1}{T}$:
$$\begin{array}{ll} \pmb{\text{Slope (m):}} & -\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{2.303 R} \\ \pmb{\text{Intercept (c):}} & \frac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{2.303 R} \end{array}$$

Therefore, the intercept and slope are $\frac{\Delta S^{\circ}}{2.303 R}$ and $-\frac{\Delta H^{\circ}}{2.303 R}$ respectively.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{C}}$.

text:
Use the following data:

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|} \hline \text{Substance} & \Delta_f H^{\ominus}(500\text{ K}) / \text{kJ mol}^{-1} & S^{\ominus}(500\text{ K}) / \text{JK}^{-1}\text{ mol}^{-1} \\ \hline \text{AB(g)} & 32 & 222 \\ \hline \text{A}_2\text{(g)} & 6 & 146 \\ \hline \text{B}_2\text{(g)} & x & 280 \\ \hline \end{array}$$

One mole each of $\text{A}_2\text{(g)}$ and $\text{B}_2\text{(g)}$ are taken in a $1\text{ L}$ closed flask and allowed to establish the equilibrium at $500\text{ K}$:
$$\text{A}_2\text{(g)} + \text{B}_2\text{(g)} \rightleftharpoons 2\text{AB(g)}$$

The value of $x$ (in $\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$) is $\dots\dots$. (Nearest integer)
(Given: $\log K = 2.2$, $R = 8.3\text{ J K}^{-1}\text{ mol}^{-1}$)

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Calculate } \Delta G^{\ominus} \text{ from } K}$ 

The relationship between the standard Gibbs free energy change and the equilibrium constant is:
$$\Delta G^{\ominus} = -2.303 RT \log K$$

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{Parameter}} & \pmb{\text{Value}} \\ 
\hline 
\text{Temperature } (T) & 500\text{ K} \\ 
\hline 
\text{Gas Constant } (R) & 8.3\text{ J K}^{-1}\text{ mol}^{-1} \\ 
\hline 
\log K & 2.2 \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$$\Delta G^{\ominus} = -2.303 \times 8.3 \times 500 \times 2.2$$
$$\Delta G^{\ominus} \approx -21026.39\text{ J mol}^{-1} = -21.026\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate } \Delta S^{\ominus} \text{ for the reaction}}$

$$\Delta S^{\ominus} = \sum S^{\ominus}(\text{products}) - \sum S^{\ominus}(\text{reactants})$$
$$\Delta S^{\ominus} = [2 \times S^{\ominus}(\text{AB})] - [S^{\ominus}(\text{A}_2) + S^{\ominus}(\text{B}_2)]$$
$$\Delta S^{\ominus} = [2 \times 222] - [146 + 280] = 444 - 426 = 18\text{ J K}^{-1}\text{ mol}^{-1}$$
$$\Delta S^{\ominus} = 0.018\text{ kJ K}^{-1}\text{ mol}^{-1}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Relate } \Delta G^{\ominus} \text{ to } \Delta H^{\ominus} \text{ and } \Delta S^{\ominus}}$

$$\Delta G^{\ominus} = \Delta H^{\ominus} - T\Delta S^{\ominus}$$
$$-21.026 = \Delta H^{\ominus} - (500 \times 0.018)$$
$$-21.026 = \Delta H^{\ominus} - 9$$
$$\Delta H^{\ominus} = -21.026 + 9 = -12.026\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Calculate } x \text{ using Enthalpy of Formation}}$

$$\Delta H^{\ominus} = [2 \times \Delta_f H^{\ominus}(\text{AB})] - [\Delta_f H^{\ominus}(\text{A}_2) + \Delta_f H^{\ominus}(\text{B}_2)]$$
$$-12.026 = [2 \times 32] - [6 + x]$$
$$-12.026 = 64 - 6 - x$$
$$-12.026 = 58 - x$$
$$x = 58 + 12.026 = 70.026\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

Rounding to the nearest integer, we get $x = 70$.

Therefore, the final answer is $\boxed{70}$.

text:
$20.0 \mathrm{dm}^3$ of an ideal gas ' X ' at 600 K and 0.5 MPa undergoes isothermal reversible expansion until pressure of the gas is 0.2 MPa . Which of the following option is correct?
(Given: $\log 2=0.3010$ and $\log 5=0.6989$ )

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Identify conditions for an isothermal process of an ideal gas}}$

For an ideal gas undergoing an $\pmb{\text{isothermal}}$ ($T = \text{constant}$) process:
$\bullet$ Internal energy change $\Delta U = nC_v\Delta T = 0$.
$\bullet$ Enthalpy change $\Delta H = nC_p\Delta T = 0$.
$\bullet$ From the First Law of Thermodynamics, $\Delta U = q + w$. Since $\Delta U = 0$, then $q = -w$.



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate work done (w) for reversible expansion}}$

The formula for work in an isothermal reversible process is:
$$w = -2.303 nRT \log \left( \frac{P_1}{P_2} \right)$$
From the ideal gas law, $nRT = P_1V_1$:
$$w = -2.303 P_1V_1 \log \left( \frac{P_1}{P_2} \right)$$

Given values:
$\bullet$ $P_1 = 0.5 \text{ MPa} = 0.5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
$\bullet$ $V_1 = 20.0 \text{ dm}^3 = 20.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$
$\bullet$ $P_2 = 0.2 \text{ MPa}$

$$\begin{array}{l} w = -2.303 \times (0.5 \times 10^6) \times (20.0 \times 10^{-3}) \times \log \left( \frac{0.5}{0.2} \right) \\ w = -2.303 \times 10,000 \times \log(2.5) \\ w = -23030 \times \log \left( \frac{5}{2} \right) \\ w = -23030 \times (\log 5 - \log 2) \end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Solve the calculation}}$

$$\begin{array}{l} w = -23030 \times (0.6989 - 0.3010) \\ w = -23030 \times 0.3979 \\ w \approx -9163.6 \text{ J} \approx -9.16 \text{ kJ} \end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Determine q}}$

Since $q = -w$, then $q = -(-9.16 \text{ kJ}) = 9.16 \text{ kJ}$.

Matching with the closest values in the options: $w = -9.1 \text{ kJ}$, $q = 9.1 \text{ kJ}$, $\Delta U = 0$, $\Delta H = 0$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{A}}$.

text: The heat of atomisation of methane and ethane are $x\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ and $y\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ respectively. The longest wavelength $(\lambda)$ of light capable of breaking the $\text{C}-\text{C}$ bond can be expressed in SI unit as:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Define Heat of Atomisation for Methane (CH}_4\text{)}}$



In methane, there are 4 $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bonds. The heat of atomisation is the energy required to break all bonds in 1 mole of the gaseous substance into gaseous atoms.
$$\Delta H_{\text{atom}}(\text{CH}_4) = 4 \times \epsilon_{\text{C}-\text{H}} = x \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$
$$\epsilon_{\text{C}-\text{H}} = \frac{x}{4} \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Define Heat of Atomisation for Ethane (C}_2\text{H}_6\text{)}}$



In ethane, there are 6 $\text{C}-\text{H}$ bonds and 1 $\text{C}-\text{C}$ bond.
$$\Delta H_{\text{atom}}(\text{C}_2\text{H}_6) = 6 \times \epsilon_{\text{C}-\text{H}} + 1 \times \epsilon_{\text{C}-\text{C}} = y \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate C}-\text{C Bond Energy (}\epsilon_{\text{C}-\text{C}}\text{)}}$

Substitute the value of $\epsilon_{\text{C}-\text{H}}$ into the ethane equation:
$$6 \left( \frac{x}{4} \right) + \epsilon_{\text{C}-\text{C}} = y$$
$$\frac{3x}{2} + \epsilon_{\text{C}-\text{C}} = y$$
$$\epsilon_{\text{C}-\text{C}} = y - \frac{3x}{2} = \frac{2y - 3x}{2} \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

To convert this to SI units (Joules per bond):
$$\text{Bond Energy per bond} = \frac{(2y - 3x) \times 1000}{2 \times N_A} \text{ J}$$
$$\text{Bond Energy per bond} = \frac{500(2y - 3x)}{N_A} = \frac{250(4y - 6x)}{N_A} \text{ J}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Relate Bond Energy to Wavelength (}\lambda\text{)}}$

The energy of a photon required to break the bond is $E = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$. For the longest wavelength, the photon energy must equal the bond energy:
$$\frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{250(4y - 6x)}{N_A}$$
$$\lambda = \frac{N_A hc}{250(4y - 6x)}$$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

The expression for the longest wavelength in SI units is $\frac{N_A hc}{250(4y - 6x)}$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{D}}$.

text:
Match the processes in $\pmb{\text{List-I}}$ with the corresponding work done expressions in $\pmb{\text{List-II}}$ for an isothermal ideal gas system ($V_f > V_i$):

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{List-I}} & \pmb{\text{List-II}} \\ 
\hline 
\text{A. Reversible expansion} & \text{I. } w = 0 \\ 
\hline 
\text{B. Free expansion} & \text{II. } w = -nRT \ln\left(\frac{V_f}{V_i}\right) \\ 
\hline 
\text{C. Irreversible expansion} & \text{III. } w = -p_{ex}(V_f - V_i) \\ 
\hline 
\text{D. Irreversible compression} & \text{IV. } w = -p_{ex}(V_i - V_f) \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Analysis of Isothermal Processes:}}$



$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{Process}} & \pmb{\text{Physics of Work Done}} \\ 
\hline 
\text{A. Reversible expansion} & \text{The external pressure is always infinitely close to internal pressure.} \\ 
& w = -\int_{V_i}^{V_f} P_{ext} dV = -nRT \ln\left(\frac{V_f}{V_i}\right) \\ 
\hline 
\text{B. Free expansion} & \text{Expansion against vacuum (} p_{ex} = 0 \text{). No opposing force means no work.} \\ 
& w = -p_{ex} \Delta V = 0 \\ 
\hline 
\text{C. Irreversible expansion} & \text{Expansion against a constant external pressure. Since } V_f > V_i\text{, } \\ 
& w = -p_{ex}(V_f - V_i) \\ 
\hline 
\text{D. Irreversible compression} & \text{Compression by a constant external pressure where } V_{final} = V_i \text{ and } \\ 
& V_{initial} = V_f \text{. Thus, } w = -p_{ex}(V_i - V_f) \text{. (Value is positive)} \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Matching Summary:}}$

$\bullet$ $\text{A} \rightarrow \text{II}$
$\bullet$ $\text{B} \rightarrow \text{I}$
$\bullet$ $\text{C} \rightarrow \text{III}$
$\bullet$ $\text{D} \rightarrow \text{IV}$

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{C}}$.

text: A cup of water at $5^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ (system) is placed in a microwave oven and the oven is turned on for one minute during which the water begins to boil. Which of the following option is true?

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze heat (q) and internal energy (}\Delta U\text{)}}$

$\bullet$ In a microwave oven, energy is transferred to the water molecules via electromagnetic radiation, which increases the kinetic energy of the molecules. This is considered heat added to the system ($q$). Therefore, $\mathbf{q = +ve}$.
$\bullet$ The temperature of the water increases from $5^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ (boiling point). Since $\Delta U$ is a function of temperature for a substance, an increase in temperature means $\mathbf{\Delta U = +ve}$.



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze work (w)}}$

$\bullet$ As the water is heated and eventually begins to boil, it expands against the atmospheric pressure. 
$\bullet$ Work done by the system (expansion) is given by $w = -P_{ext}\Delta V$. 
$\bullet$ Since the volume increases ($\Delta V > 0$), the work $w$ is negative. Therefore, $\mathbf{w = -ve}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Combine using the First Law of Thermodynamics}}$

According to the First Law: 
$$\Delta U = q + w$$
Since $q$ is significantly larger than the work of expansion during the heating phase, $\Delta U$ remains positive despite the negative work.

$$\begin{array}{l} q = +ve \\ w = -ve \\ \Delta U = +ve \end{array}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{A}}$.

text:
Match the thermodynamic processes in $\pmb{\text{List-I}}$ with their corresponding magnitudes (in $\text{kJ}$) in $\pmb{\text{List-II}}$:

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{List-I (Thermodynamic Process)}} & \pmb{\text{List-II (Magnitude in kJ)}} \\ 
\hline 
\text{A. Work done in reversible, isothermal expansion of } 2 \text{ mol} & \text{I. } 4 \\ 
\text{of ideal gas from } 2 \text{ dm}^3 \text{ to } 20 \text{ dm}^3 \text{ at } 300 \text{ K} & \\ 
\hline 
\text{B. Work done in irreversible isothermal expansion of } 1 \text{ mol} & \text{II. } 11.5 \\ 
\text{ideal gas from } 1 \text{ m}^3 \text{ to } 3 \text{ m}^3 \text{ at } 300 \text{ K against } 3 \text{ kPa} & \\ 
\hline 
\text{C. } \Delta U \text{ for adiabatic expansion of } 1 \text{ mol ideal gas with} & \text{III. } 6 \\ 
\Delta T = 320 \text{ K and } \bar{C}_V = \frac{3}{2}R & \\ 
\hline 
\text{D. } \Delta H \text{ at constant pressure of } 1 \text{ mol ideal gas with} & \text{IV. } 7 \\ 
\Delta T = 337 \text{ K and } \bar{C}_p = \frac{5}{2}R & \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step-by-step Calculations:}}$

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|} 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{Process}} & \pmb{\text{Calculation}} & \pmb{\text{Magnitude}} \\ 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{A}} & w = -2.303 nRT \log\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) & |w| = 11.5 \text{ kJ} \\ 
& w = -2.303 \times 2 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times \log(10) \approx -11497 \text{ J} & \\ 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{B}} & w = -P_{ext}(V_2 - V_1) & |w| = 6 \text{ kJ} \\ 
& w = -3000 \text{ Pa} \times (3 - 1) \text{ m}^3 = -6000 \text{ J} & \\ 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{C}} & \Delta U = n C_V \Delta T & |\Delta U| = 4 \text{ kJ} \\ 
& \Delta U = 1 \times \frac{3}{2} \times 8.314 \times 320 \approx 3990 \text{ J} & \\ 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{D}} & \Delta H = n C_p \Delta T & |\Delta H| = 7 \text{ kJ} \\ 
& \Delta H = 1 \times \frac{5}{2} \times 8.314 \times 337 \approx 6999 \text{ J} & \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Detailed Reasoning:}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Process A:}}$ For reversible isothermal expansion, we use the logarithmic work formula. $2.303 \times 2 \times 8.314 \times 300 \times 1 \approx 11.5 \times 10^3 \text{ J}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Process B:}}$ For irreversible expansion against constant pressure, $w = -P \Delta V$. Using SI units ($3 \text{ kPa} = 3000 \text{ N/m}^2$ and $\Delta V = 2 \text{ m}^3$), we get $6000 \text{ J} = 6 \text{ kJ}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Process C:}}$ Internal energy change depends only on temperature change for an ideal gas. $\Delta U = 1 \times 1.5 \times 8.314 \times 320 \approx 4000 \text{ J} = 4 \text{ kJ}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Process D:}}$ Enthalpy change is calculated using $C_p$. $\Delta H = 1 \times 2.5 \times 8.314 \times 337 \approx 7000 \text{ J} = 7 \text{ kJ}$.

$\pmb{\text{Final Match:}}$
$A \rightarrow \text{II}, B \rightarrow \text{III}, C \rightarrow \text{I}, D \rightarrow \text{IV}$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{D}}$.

text:
Consider the following data :
$\Delta_{f}H^{\circ}$ (methane, g) = $-X \; \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$
Enthalpy of sublimation of graphite = $Y \; \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$
Dissociation enthalpy of $H_2 = Z \; \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$
The bond enthalpy of C–H bond is given by :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Define the formation reaction for methane}}$

The standard enthalpy of formation ($\Delta_f H^{\circ}$) for methane gas is represented by:
$$\text{C}(\text{graphite}) + 2\text{H}_2(g) \rightarrow \text{CH}_4(g) \quad \Delta H = -X \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Breakdown the process into atomization steps}}$

To find the bond enthalpy, we must first convert the reactants into gaseous atoms:
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Sublimation of carbon:}}$ $\text{C}(\text{graphite}) \rightarrow \text{C}(g) \quad \Delta H = Y$
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Dissociation of hydrogen:}}$ $2\text{H}_2(g) \rightarrow 4\text{H}(g) \quad \Delta H = 2 \times Z = 2Z$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Apply Hess's Law}}$

The total energy required to break the bonds in the formed methane is the sum of the energies used to create the atoms minus the enthalpy of formation:
$$\text{Total Bond Energy of CH}_4 = \text{Energy of C}(g) + \text{Energy of 4H}(g) - \Delta_f H^{\circ}(\text{CH}_4)$$
$$\text{Total Bond Energy} = Y + 2Z - (-X) = X + Y + 2Z$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Calculate the individual C–H bond enthalpy}}$

Since methane has four identical $\text{C–H}$ bonds, the bond enthalpy for a single bond is:
$$\text{Bond Enthalpy (C–H)} = \frac{\text{Total Bond Energy}}{4}$$
$$\text{Bond Enthalpy (C–H)} = \frac{X + Y + 2Z}{4}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\dfrac{X + Y + 2Z}{4}}$.

text: Which of the following graphs between pressure ' p ' versus volume ' V ' represents the maximum work done?

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Understand the relationship between p-V graphs and work}}$

$\bullet$ In thermodynamics, the work done ($W$) during a process is represented by the area under the curve on a Pressure ($p$) vs. Volume ($V$) diagram.
$\bullet$ Mathematically, this is expressed as: $$W = \int p \, dV$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze the provided graphs}}$

$$\begin{array}{|c|l|} 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{Option}} & \pmb{\text{Area Description}} \\ 
\hline 
\text{A} & \text{Shows an expansion followed by a process that encloses the largest area under the path.} \\ 
\hline 
\text{B} & \text{Shows a vertical (isochoric) drop, resulting in minimal area under the curve.} \\ 
\hline 
\text{C} & \text{Shows a compression/cyclic path that encloses a smaller net area compared to A.} \\ 
\hline 
\text{D} & \text{Shows a smaller enclosed area within the specific path limits.} \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Compare the magnitude of work}}$

$\bullet$ By visually inspecting the total area bounded by the $p$ and $V$ axes and the process curves, $\pmb{\text{Option A}}$ covers the most significant region on the graph.
$\bullet$ Since the area under the curve is directly proportional to the magnitude of work done, the process in graph A represents the maximum work.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{A}}$.

text:
For the reaction, $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4 \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_2$, graph is plotted as shown below. Identify correct statements.
A. Standard free energy change for the reaction is $-5.40 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
B. As $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\ominus}$ in graph is positive, $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$ will not dissociate into $\mathrm{NO}_2$ at all.
C. Reverse reaction will go to completion.
D. When 1 mole of $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$ changes into equilibrium mixture, value of $\Delta G = -0.84 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
E. When 2 mole of $\mathrm{NO}_2$ changes into equilibrium mixture, $\Delta G$ for equilibrium mixture is $-6.24 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze Standard Free Energy Change (}\Delta G^{\ominus}\text{)}}$

$\bullet$ In the graph, Point A represents pure reactant ($\mathrm{N}_2\mathrm{O}_4$) and Point B represents pure product ($2\mathrm{NO}_2$).
$\bullet$ The standard free energy change $\Delta G^{\ominus}$ is the difference between the Gibbs free energy of pure products and pure reactants: $G_{\text{products}} - G_{\text{reactants}}$.
$\bullet$ From the graph, $\Delta G^{\ominus} = G_B - G_A = +5.40 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
$\bullet$ Therefore, Statement A is $\pmb{\text{incorrect}}$ because the value is positive, not negative.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Evaluate Equilibrium and Dissociation}}$

$\bullet$ Point E represents the equilibrium state (the minimum of the curve). At this point, the fraction of $\mathrm{N}_2\mathrm{O}_4$ dissociated is approximately $0.3$.
$\bullet$ Since an equilibrium point exists between $0$ and $1$, the reaction occurs but does not go to completion in either direction.
$\bullet$ Therefore, Statements B and C are $\pmb{\text{incorrect}}$.



$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Analyze Gibbs Free Energy Change for Mixture (D and E)}}$
