$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement D:}}$ When $1 \text{ mole of } \mathrm{N}_2\mathrm{O}_4$ (Point A) moves to the equilibrium mixture (Point E), the change in free energy is $\Delta G = G_E - G_A$.
$\bullet$ From the graph, this vertical drop is indicated as $-0.84 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. So, Statement D is $\pmb{\text{correct}}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement E:}}$ When $2 \text{ moles of } \mathrm{NO}_2$ (Point B) move to the equilibrium mixture (Point E), the change in free energy is $\Delta G = G_E - G_B$.
$\bullet$ We can calculate this as: $(G_E - G_A) - (G_B - G_A) = -0.84 - 5.40 = -6.24 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.
$\bullet$ Thus, Statement E is $\pmb{\text{correct}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

Statements D and E are the only correct statements.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{B}}$.

text:
Consider the following statements about manganate and permanganate ions. Identify the correct statements.

$\pmb{\text{A.}}$ The geometry of both manganate and permanganate ions is tetrahedral.
$\pmb{\text{B.}}$ The oxidation states of Mn in manganate and permanganate are +7 and +6, respectively.
$\pmb{\text{C.}}$ Oxidation of Mn(II) salt by peroxodisulphate gives manganate ion as the final product.
$\pmb{\text{D.}}$ Manganate ion is paramagnetic and permanganate ion is diamagnetic.
$\pmb{\text{E.}}$ Acidified permanganate ion reduces oxalate, nitrite and iodide ions.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Evaluation of Statements:}}$

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|} 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{Stmt}} & \pmb{\text{Analysis}} & \pmb{\text{Status}} \\ 
\hline 
\text{A} & \text{Both } \text{MnO}_4^{2-} \text{ and } \text{MnO}_4^- \text{ have } sp^3 \text{ hybridized Mn and are tetrahedral.} & \pmb{\text{Correct}} \\ 
\hline 
\text{B} & \text{Manganate is +6; Permanganate is +7. The statement reverses them.} & \pmb{\text{Incorrect}} \\ 
\hline 
\text{C} & \text{Peroxodisulphate oxidizes Mn(II) to } \pmb{\text{permanganate}} \text{ (not manganate).} & \pmb{\text{Incorrect}} \\ 
\hline 
\text{D} & \text{Manganate (} d^1 \text{) is paramagnetic; Permanganate (} d^0 \text{) is diamagnetic.} & \pmb{\text{Correct}} \\ 
\hline 
\text{E} & \text{Permanganate is an oxidant; it } \pmb{\text{oxidizes}} \text{ (not reduces) these ions.} & \pmb{\text{Incorrect}} \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement A:}}$ In both $\text{MnO}_4^{2-}$ (green) and $\text{MnO}_4^-$ (purple), the manganese atom is at the center of a tetrahedron formed by four oxygen atoms. Pi bonding occurs through the overlap of oxygen $p$-orbitals with manganese $d$-orbitals.


$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement C:}}$ The reaction is: $2\text{Mn}^{2+} + 5\text{S}_2\text{O}_8^{2-} + 8\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{MnO}_4^- + 10\text{SO}_4^{2-} + 16\text{H}^+$. The product is the purple permanganate ion.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement D:}}$ Manganate $(\text{MnO}_4^{2-})$ has Mn in the $+6$ state $([Ar]3d^1)$, making it paramagnetic due to one unpaired electron. Permanganate $(\text{MnO}_4^-)$ has Mn in the $+7$ state $([Ar]3d^0)$, making it diamagnetic.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement E:}}$ Acidified $\text{KMnO}_4$ is a strong $\pmb{\text{oxidizing agent}}$. It oxidizes oxalate $(\text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-})$ to $\text{CO}_2$, nitrite $(\text{NO}_2^-)$ to $\text{NO}_3^-$, and iodide $(\text{I}^-)$ to $\text{I}_2$.

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
Statements A and D are the only correct statements.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{A}}$.

text:
Given below are two statements:

$\textbf{Statement I :}$ The number of pairs, from the following, in which both the ions are coloured in aqueous solution is $3$.

$$\left[\text{Sc}^{3+}, \text{Ti}^{3+}\right], \left[\text{Mn}^{2+}, \text{Cr}^{2+}\right], \left[\text{Cu}^{2+}, \text{Zn}^{2+}\right] \text{ and } \left[\text{Ni}^{2+}, \text{Ti}^{4+}\right]$$

$\textbf{Statement II :}$ $\text{Th}^{4+}$ is the strongest reducing agent among $\text{Th}^{4+}$, $\text{Ce}^{4+}$, $\text{Gd}^{3+}$ and $\text{Eu}^{2+}$.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:

options.text: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze Statement I (Color in aqueous solution)}}$

Color in transition metal ions typically arises from $d\text{-}d$ transitions, which occur in ions with partially filled $d$-orbitals (electronic configuration $d^1$ to $d^9$). Ions with $d^0$ or $d^{10}$ configurations are generally colorless.

$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|} \hline \textbf{Pair} & \textbf{Ion 1 (Config.)} & \textbf{Ion 2 (Config.)} & \textbf{Both Coloured?} \\ \hline [\text{Sc}^{3+}, \text{Ti}^{3+}] & \text{Sc}^{3+} (d^0) \text{ - Colourless} & \text{Ti}^{3+} (d^1) \text{ - Coloured} & \text{No} \\ \hline [\text{Mn}^{2+}, \text{Cr}^{2+}] & \text{Mn}^{2+} (d^5) \text{ - Coloured} & \text{Cr}^{2+} (d^4) \text{ - Coloured} & \text{Yes} \\ \hline [\text{Cu}^{2+}, \text{Zn}^{2+}] & \text{Cu}^{2+} (d^9) \text{ - Coloured} & \text{Zn}^{2+} (d^{10}) \text{ - Colourless} & \text{No} \\ \hline [\text{Ni}^{2+}, \text{Ti}^{4+}] & \text{Ni}^{2+} (d^8) \text{ - Coloured} & \text{Ti}^{4+} (d^0) \text{ - Colourless} & \text{No} \\ \hline \end{array}$

 

$\bullet$ Only $\pm b{1}$ pair has both ions coloured.

$\bullet$ $\textbf{Statement I is False.}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze Statement II (Reducing agents)}}$

$\bullet$ $\text{Th}^{4+}$ and $\text{Gd}^{3+}$ are in their most stable oxidation states; they do not readily act as reducing agents.

$\bullet$ $\text{Ce}^{4+}$ is a strong $\textbf{oxidizing agent}$ as it tends to gain an electron to reach the stable $+3$ state.

$\bullet$ $\text{Eu}^{2+}$ is a strong $\textbf{reducing agent}$ because it tends to lose an electron to reach the stable $+3$ state (attaining a stable $f^7$ configuration is a common driving force).

$\bullet$ $\text{Th}^{4+}$ is not a reducing agent.

$\bullet$ $\textbf{Statement II is False.}$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

Both Statement I and Statement II are false.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{B}}$.

text:
Given below are two statements:

$\textbf{Statement I :}$ The first ionization enthalpy of $\text{Cr}$ is lower than that of $\text{Mn}$.

$\textbf{Statement II :}$ The second and third ionization enthalpies of $\text{Cr}$ are higher than those of $\text{Mn}$.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:

options.text: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Write electronic configurations}}$

To compare ionization enthalpies, we first look at the ground state electronic configurations of Chromium $(\text{Cr, } Z=24)$ and Manganese $(\text{Mn, } Z=25)$:

$\bullet$ $\text{Cr: } [\text{Ar}] 3d^5 4s^1$

$\bullet$ $\text{Mn: } [\text{Ar}] 3d^5 4s^2$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze Statement I (First Ionization Enthalpy, IE}_1\text{)}}$

$\bullet$ $\textbf{Cr to Cr}^+ \textbf{:}$ Electron is removed from the $4s^1$ orbital.

$\bullet$ $\textbf{Mn to Mn}^+ \textbf{:}$ Electron is removed from the $4s^2$ orbital.

$\bullet$ $\text{Mn}$ has a higher nuclear charge $(Z=25)$ than $\text{Cr}$ $(Z=24)$. Furthermore, the $4s$ orbital in $\text{Mn}$ is completely filled, providing more stability than the half-filled $4s$ in $\text{Cr}$. Thus, removing the first electron from $\text{Mn}$ requires more energy.

$\bullet$ $\text{Result: IE}_1(\text{Cr}) < \text{IE}_1(\text{Mn})$. $\textbf{Statement I is True.}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Analyze Statement II (IE}_2 \text{ and IE}_3\text{)}}$

$\textbf{For Second Ionization Enthalpy (IE}_2\textbf{):}$

$\bullet$ $\text{Cr}^+ \text{: } [\text{Ar}] 3d^5$ (Extra stability due to half-filled $d$-subshell).

$\bullet$ $\text{Mn}^+ \text{: } [\text{Ar}] 3d^5 4s^1$.

$\bullet$ Removing an electron from the stable $3d^5$ configuration of $\text{Cr}^+$ is much harder than removing the $4s^1$ electron from $\text{Mn}^+$. Thus, $\text{IE}_2(\text{Cr}) > \text{IE}_2(\text{Mn})$.

$\textbf{For Third Ionization Enthalpy (IE}_3\textbf{):}$

$\bullet$ $\text{Cr}^{2+} \text{: } [\text{Ar}] 3d^4$.

$\bullet$ $\text{Mn}^{2+} \text{: } [\text{Ar}] 3d^5$ (Extra stability due to half-filled $d$-subshell).

$\bullet$ Removing an electron from the stable $3d^5$ configuration of $\text{Mn}^{2+}$ requires significantly more energy than from the $3d^4$ configuration of $\text{Cr}^{2+}$. Thus, $\text{IE}_3(\text{Mn}) > \text{IE}_3(\text{Cr})$.

$\bullet$ $\text{Result:}$ While $\text{IE}_2$ is higher for $\text{Cr}$, $\text{IE}_3$ is higher for $\text{Mn}$. $\textbf{Statement II is False.}$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
Statement I is true, but Statement II is false.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{A}}$.

text: On heating a mixture of common salt and $K_2Cr_2O_7$ in equal amount along with concentrated $H_2SO_4$ in a test tube, a gas is evolved. Formula of the gas evolved and oxidation state of the central metal atom in the gas respectively are:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze Statement I (Color in aqueous solution)}}$

Color in transition metal ions typically arises from $d\text{-}d$ transitions, which occur in ions with partially filled $d$-orbitals (electronic configuration $d^1$ to $d^9$). Ions with $d^0$ or $d^{10}$ configurations are generally colorless.

$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|} \hline \textbf{Pair} & \textbf{Ion 1 (Config.)} & \textbf{Ion 2 (Config.)} & \textbf{Both Coloured?} \\ \hline [\text{Sc}^{3+}, \text{Ti}^{3+}] & \text{Sc}^{3+} (d^0) \text{ - Colourless} & \text{Ti}^{3+} (d^1) \text{ - Coloured} & \text{No} \\ \hline [\text{Mn}^{2+}, \text{Cr}^{2+}] & \text{Mn}^{2+} (d^5) \text{ - Coloured} & \text{Cr}^{2+} (d^4) \text{ - Coloured} & \text{Yes} \\ \hline [\text{Cu}^{2+}, \text{Zn}^{2+}] & \text{Cu}^{2+} (d^9) \text{ - Coloured} & \text{Zn}^{2+} (d^{10}) \text{ - Colourless} & \text{No} \\ \hline [\text{Ni}^{2+}, \text{Ti}^{4+}] & \text{Ni}^{2+} (d^8) \text{ - Coloured} & \text{Ti}^{4+} (d^0) \text{ - Colourless} & \text{No} \\ \hline \end{array}$

 

$\bullet$ Only $\pm b{1}$ pair has both ions coloured.

$\bullet$ $\textbf{Statement I is False.}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze Statement II (Reducing agents)}}$

$\bullet$ $\text{Th}^{4+}$ and $\text{Gd}^{3+}$ are in their most stable oxidation states; they do not readily act as reducing agents.

$\bullet$ $\text{Ce}^{4+}$ is a strong $\textbf{oxidizing agent}$ as it tends to gain an electron to reach the stable $+3$ state.

$\bullet$ $\text{Eu}^{2+}$ is a strong $\textbf{reducing agent}$ because it tends to lose an electron to reach the stable $+3$ state (attaining a stable $f^7$ configuration is a common driving force).

$\bullet$ $\text{Th}^{4+}$ is not a reducing agent.

$\bullet$ $\textbf{Statement II is False.}$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

Both Statement I and Statement II are false.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{B}}$.

text:
Given below are some of the statements about Mn and $\text{Mn}_2\text{O}_7$. Identify the correct statements.
A. Mn forms the oxide $\text{Mn}_2\text{O}_7$, in which Mn is in its highest oxidation state.
B. Oxygen stabilizes the Mn in higher oxidation states by forming multiple bonds with Mn.
C. $\text{Mn}_2\text{O}_7$ is an ionic oxide.
D. The structure of $\text{Mn}_2\text{O}_7$ consists of one bridged oxygen.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze Statement A}}$
In $\text{Mn}_2\text{O}_7$, let the oxidation state of Mn be $x$:
$$2(x) + 7(-2) = 0 \implies 2x = 14 \implies x = +7$$
Since the electronic configuration of Mn is $[\text{Ar}] 3d^5 4s^2$, $+7$ is indeed its highest oxidation state. $\pmb{\text{Statement A is correct.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze Statement B}}$
Oxygen has a high ability to form $p\pi-d\pi$ multiple bonds with transition metals, which effectively stabilizes high oxidation states like $\text{Mn}^{7+}$. $\pmb{\text{Statement B is correct.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Analyze Statement C}}$
$\text{Mn}_2\text{O}_7$ is a green oil at room temperature. High oxidation state oxides (typically $>+4$) of transition metals exhibit covalent character due to high polarizing power of the metal cation. $\pmb{\text{Statement C is incorrect.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Analyze Statement D}}$

The structure of $\text{Mn}_2\text{O}_7$ consists of two $\text{MnO}_4$ tetrahedra sharing a common vertex. It contains a single $\text{Mn-O-Mn}$ bridge. $\pmb{\text{Statement D is correct.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
Statements A, B, and D are correct.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{A, B and D Only}}$.

text: $\mathrm{MnO}_4^{2-}$, in acidic medium, disproportionates to :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Understand Disproportionation}}$

$\bullet$ A $\pmb{\text{disproportionation reaction}}$ is a specific type of redox reaction where the same element undergoes both oxidation and reduction simultaneously.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze the Oxidation States of Manganese}}$

$\bullet$ In the manganate ion ($\mathrm{MnO}_4^{2-}$), the oxidation state of $\mathrm{Mn}$ is $\pmb{+6}$.
$\bullet$ Manganate ions are stable only in strongly alkaline solutions. In acidic or neutral solutions, they are unstable and disproportionate.



$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Determine the Products}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Oxidation:}}$ The $\mathrm{Mn(+6)}$ in $\mathrm{MnO}_4^{2-}$ is oxidized to $\mathrm{Mn(+7)}$ in the permanganate ion ($\mathrm{MnO}_4^-$).
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Reduction:}}$ The $\mathrm{Mn(+6)}$ in $\mathrm{MnO}_4^{2-}$ is reduced to $\mathrm{Mn(+4)}$ in manganese dioxide ($\mathrm{MnO}_2$).

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Write the Balanced Equation}}$

The balanced chemical equation in an acidic medium is:
$$3\mathrm{MnO}_4^{2-} + 4\mathrm{H}^+ \rightarrow 2\mathrm{MnO}_4^- + \mathrm{MnO}_2 + 2\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

In an acidic medium, $\mathrm{MnO}_4^{2-}$ disproportionates to give $\pmb{\mathrm{MnO}_4^-}$ and $\pmb{\mathrm{MnO}_2}$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{B}}$.

text:
Consider the following reactions :
$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \rightarrow \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{KHSO}_4+\mathrm{NaHSO}_4+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\ & \mathrm{A}+\mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow \mathrm{B}+\mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\ & \mathrm{B}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2 \rightarrow \mathrm{C}+\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \end{aligned} $$
In the product 'C', 'X' is the number of $\mathrm{O}_2^{2-}$ units, 'Y' is the total number of oxygen atoms present and 'Z' is the oxidation state of $\mathrm{Cr}$. The value of $X+Y+Z$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Identify the chemical species A, B, and C}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Reaction 1 (Chromyl Chloride Test):}}$ The reaction of a chloride salt with potassium dichromate and concentrated sulfuric acid produces $\pmb{\text{Chromyl Chloride}}$, which is characterized by its deep red vapors.
  $$\mathrm{4NaCl + K_2Cr_2O_7 + 6H_2SO_4 \rightarrow 2CrO_2Cl_2 (A) + 2KHSO_4 + 4NaHSO_4 + 3H_2O}$$
  Therefore, $\pmb{A = CrO_2Cl_2}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Reaction 2:}}$ Chromyl chloride reacts with $\mathrm{NaOH}$ to form a yellow solution of $\pmb{\text{Sodium Chromate}}$.
  $$\mathrm{CrO_2Cl_2 + 4NaOH \rightarrow Na_2CrO_4 (B) + 2NaCl + 2H_2O}$$
  Therefore, $\pmb{B = Na_2CrO_4}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Reaction 3:}}$ Sodium chromate reacts with $\mathrm{H_2O_2}$ in an acidic medium to form a deep blue complex, $\pmb{\text{Chromium Pentoxide}}$ (also known as Chromium(VI) peroxide).
  $$\mathrm{Na_2CrO_4 + H_2SO_4 + 2H_2O_2 \rightarrow CrO_5 (C) + Na_2SO_4 + 3H_2O}$$
  Therefore, $\pmb{C = CrO_5}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze the structure of product C (CrO}_5\text{)}}$



$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{X (Number of }} \mathrm{O}_2^{2-} \pmb{\text{ units):}}$ $\mathrm{CrO_5}$ has a characteristic "butterfly" structure with $\pmb{2}$ peroxide ($\mathrm{O}_2^{2-}$) linkages and one oxo group ($\mathrm{Cr=O}$).
  So, $\pmb{X = 2}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Y (Total number of oxygen atoms):}}$ There are $\pmb{5}$ oxygen atoms in the molecular formula $\mathrm{CrO_5}$.
  So, $\pmb{Y = 5}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Z (Oxidation state of Cr):}}$ In $\mathrm{CrO_5}$, four oxygen atoms are in the peroxide state ($-1$) and one is in the oxide state ($-2$). 
  $$Z + 4(-1) + 1(-2) = 0 \implies Z = +6$$
  So, $\pmb{Z = 6}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate the final sum}}$

$$X + Y + Z = 2 + 5 + 6 = \pmb{13}$$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

The sum of the peroxide units, total oxygen atoms, and the oxidation state of Chromium in the final product is $\pmb{13}$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{13}}$.

text:
Number of photons of equal energy emitted per second by a 6 mW laser source operating at 663 nm is ________.
(Given : $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34}$ J.s and $c=3\times10^{8}$ m/s)

A volume of $x \text{ mL}$ of $5 \text{ M } \mathrm{NaHCO}_3$ solution was mixed with $10 \text{ mL}$ of $2 \text{ M } \mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{CO}_3$ solution to make an electrolytic buffer. If the same buffer was used in the following electrochemical cell to record a cell potential of $235.3 \text{ mV}$, then the value of $x = \dots \dots \text{ mL}$ (nearest integer).

$$\mathrm{Sn(s) \mid Sn(OH)_6^{2-} (0.5 \text{ M}) \mid HSnO_2^- (0.05 \text{ M}) \mid OH^- \mid Bi_2O_3(s) \mid Bi(s)}$$

Consider up to one place of decimal for intermediate calculations

$$\left[\begin{array}{ll}\text { Given: } & E_{\mathrm{Sn(OH)_6^{2-} \mid HSnO_2^-}}^o = - 0.9 \text{ V} \\ & E_{\mathrm{Bi_2O_3 \mid Bi}}^o = -0.44 \text{ V} \\ & \mathrm{pKa}_{(\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{CO}_3)} = 6.11 \\ & \frac{2.303RT}{F} = 0.059 \text{ V} \\ & \text{Antilog}(1.29) = 19.5\end{array}\right]$$

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Identify the Anode and Cathode half-reactions}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Anode (Oxidation):}}$ The Tin (Sn) electrode undergoes oxidation from $\mathrm{Sn(OH)_6^{2-}}$ to $\mathrm{HSnO_2^-}$.
  Reaction: $\mathrm{HSnO_2^- + 3OH^- + H_2O \rightarrow Sn(OH)_6^{2-} + 2e^-}$
  $\mathrm{E}_{ox}^o = -(-0.9 \text{ V}) = +0.9 \text{ V}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Cathode (Reduction):}}$ The Bismuth (Bi) electrode.
  Reaction: $\mathrm{Bi_2O_3 + 3H_2O + 6e^- \rightarrow 2Bi + 6OH^-}$
  $\mathrm{E}_{red}^o = -0.44 \text{ V}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate the Standard Cell Potential (E}_{cell}^o\pmb{\text{)}}}$

$\bullet$ To balance electrons, multiply the anode reaction by 3:
  $\mathrm{3HSnO_2^- + 9OH^- + 3H_2O \rightarrow 3Sn(OH)_6^{2-} + 6e^-}$
$\bullet$ Net Cell Reaction: $\mathrm{3HSnO_2^- + 3OH^- + Bi_2O_3 + 6H_2O \rightarrow 3Sn(OH)_6^{2-} + 2Bi + 6OH^-}$
  Simplified: $\mathrm{3HSnO_2^- + Bi_2O_3 + 6H_2O \rightarrow 3Sn(OH)_6^{2-} + 2Bi + 3OH^-}$
$\bullet$ $\mathrm{E}_{cell}^o = \mathrm{E}_{cathode}^o - \mathrm{E}_{anode}^o = -0.44 - (-0.9) = \pmb{0.46 \text{ V}}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Apply the Nernst Equation to find [OH}^-\pmb{\text{]}}}$

$\bullet$ Given: $\mathrm{E}_{cell} = 235.3 \text{ mV} = 0.2353 \text{ V}$, $n = 6$.
$$\mathrm{E}_{cell} = \mathrm{E}_{cell}^o - \frac{0.059}{6} \log \frac{\mathrm{[Sn(OH)_6^{2-}]^3 [OH^-]^3}}{\mathrm{[HSnO_2^-]^3}}$$
$$0.2353 = 0.46 - \frac{0.059}{6} \log \left( \frac{0.5}{0.05} \times \mathrm{[OH^-]} \right)^3$$
$$0.2353 - 0.46 = -\frac{0.059}{2} \log (10 \times \mathrm{[OH^-]})$$
$$-0.2247 = -0.0295 \log (10 \times \mathrm{[OH^-]})$$
$$\log (10 \times \mathrm{[OH^-]}) = \frac{0.2247}{0.0295} \approx 7.617$$
$$\log [\mathrm{OH}^-] + 1 = 7.617 \implies \log [\mathrm{OH}^-] = 6.617$$
$\bullet$ Since $\mathrm{pOH} = -\log [\mathrm{OH}^-]$, $\mathrm{pOH} = -6.617$. 
$\bullet$ $\mathrm{pH} = 14 - \mathrm{pOH} = 14 - (-6.617) = \pmb{7.4}$ (approximately).



$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Use Henderson-Hasselbalch Equation for the buffer}}$

$\bullet$ The buffer consists of $\mathrm{NaHCO_3}$ (salt) and $\mathrm{H_2CO_3}$ (acid).
$$\mathrm{pH = pKa + \log \frac{[salt]}{[acid]}}$$
$$7.4 = 6.11 + \log \frac{5 \times x / (x+10)}{2 \times 10 / (x+10)}$$
$$1.29 = \log \frac{5x}{20}$$
$\bullet$ Given $\text{Antilog}(1.29) = 19.5$:
$$19.5 = \frac{5x}{20}$$
$$19.5 = \frac{x}{4}$$
$$x = 19.5 \times 4 = \pmb{78 \text{ mL}}$$

Therefore, the correct value of $x$ is $\boxed{78}$.

text: Consider the above electrochemical cell where a metal electrode (M) is undergoing redox reaction by forming $\text{M}^+ (\text{M} \rightarrow \text{M}^+ + \text{e}^-)$. The cation $\text{M}^+$ is present in two different concentrations $c_1$ and $c_2$ as shown above. Which of the following statements is correct for generating a positive cell potential?

options.text: If $c_1$ is present at anode, then $c_1 > c_2$.
If $c_1$ is present at cathode, then $c_1 > c_2$.
If $c_1$ is present at cathode, then $c_1 < c_2$.
If $c_1$ is present at anode, then $c_1 = c_2$.

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Identify the Type of Cell}}$

This is a $\pmb{\text{concentration cell}}$ because the same metal and the same ions are used in both half-cells, differing only in their concentrations. For such a cell, the standard cell potential ($E^\circ_{\text{cell}}$) is $\pmb{0}$.



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Set up the Cell Reactions}}$

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{Anode (Oxidation)}} & \text{M(s)} \rightarrow \text{M}^+(c_{\text{anode}}) + \text{e}^- \\ 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{Cathode (Reduction)}} & \text{M}^+(c_{\text{cathode}}) + \text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{M(s)} \\ 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{Net Reaction}} & \text{M}^+(c_{\text{cathode}}) \rightleftharpoons \text{M}^+(c_{\text{anode}}) \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Apply the Nernst Equation}}$

The cell potential ($E_{\text{cell}}$) is given by:
$$E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cell}} - \frac{0.0591}{n} \log Q$$
Since $E^\circ_{\text{cell}} = 0$ and $n = 1$:
$$E_{\text{cell}} = -0.0591 \log \left( \frac{c_{\text{anode}}}{c_{\text{cathode}}} \right) = 0.0591 \log \left( \frac{c_{\text{cathode}}}{c_{\text{anode}}} \right)$$



$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Condition for Positive Cell Potential}}$

For $E_{\text{cell}} > 0$:
$$\log \left( \frac{c_{\text{cathode}}}{c_{\text{anode}}} \right) > 0 \implies \frac{c_{\text{cathode}}}{c_{\text{anode}}} > 1$$
$$\pmb{c_{\text{cathode}} > c_{\text{anode}}}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 5: Evaluate the Options}}$

$\bullet$ If $c_1$ is at the $\pmb{\text{cathode}}$, then for the potential to be positive, $c_{\text{cathode}} (c_1)$ must be greater than $c_{\text{anode}} (c_2)$. Thus, $\pmb{c_1 > c_2}$.
$\bullet$ If $c_1$ is at the $\pmb{\text{anode}}$, then for the potential to be positive, $c_{\text{anode}} (c_1)$ must be less than $c_{\text{cathode}} (c_2)$. Thus, $\pmb{c_1 < c_2}$.

Comparing this with the options, Statement B matches our requirement.

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
For a positive cell potential, if $c_1$ is at the cathode, then $c_1$ must be greater than $c_2$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{B}}$.

text:
In the given electrochemical cell, $\mathrm{Ag(s) | AgCl(s) | FeCl_2(aq), FeCl_3(aq) | Pt(s)}$ at $298 \text{ K}$, the cell potential ($E_{cell}$) will increase when :

A. Concentration of $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ is increased.
B. Concentration of $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ is decreased.
C. Concentration of $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$ is decreased.
D. Concentration of $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$ is increased.
E. Concentration of $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ is increased.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Write the half-cell reactions}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Anode (Oxidation):}}$ The Silver/Silver chloride electrode acts as the anode.
$$\mathrm{Ag(s) + Cl^-(aq) \rightarrow AgCl(s) + e^-}$$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Cathode (Reduction):}}$ The Iron system at the Platinum electrode acts as the cathode.
$$\mathrm{Fe^{3+}(aq) + e^- \rightarrow Fe^{2+}(aq)}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Derive the overall cell reaction}}$

By adding the two half-reactions, we get the net cell reaction:
$$\mathrm{Ag(s) + Cl^-(aq) + Fe^{3+}(aq) \rightarrow AgCl(s) + Fe^{2+}(aq)}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Apply the Nernst Equation}}$

The cell potential is given by:
$$E_{cell} = E_{cell}^o - \frac{0.059}{1} \log Q$$
Where $Q$ (reaction quotient) is defined as:
$$Q = \frac{[\mathrm{Fe^{2+}}]}{[\mathrm{Cl^-}] [\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}]}$$ 
Note: Solids like $\mathrm{Ag}$ and $\mathrm{AgCl}$ have an activity of 1.

Substituting $Q$ into the Nernst equation:
$$E_{cell} = E_{cell}^o - 0.059 \log \left( \frac{[\mathrm{Fe^{2+}}]}{[\mathrm{Cl^-}] [\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}]} \right)$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Analyze the conditions for increasing } E_{cell}}$

To increase $E_{cell}$, the value of the logarithmic term must $\pmb{\text{decrease}}$. This happens when the ratio $\frac{[\mathrm{Fe^{2+}}]}{[\mathrm{Cl^-}] [\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}]}$ becomes smaller:
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Decrease } [\mathrm{Fe^{2+}}]\pmb{\text{:}}}$ Shifting the equilibrium forward (Condition C).
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Increase } [\mathrm{Fe^{3+}}]\pmb{\text{:}}}$ Providing more reactant (Condition D).
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Increase } [\mathrm{Cl^-}]\pmb{\text{:}}}$ Providing more reactant at the anode (Condition E).

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

The cell potential increases under conditions $\pmb{\text{C, D, and E}}$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{A}}$.

text:
Consider the following reduction processes :
$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Al}^{3+}+3 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{s}), \mathrm{E}^0=-1.66 \mathrm{~V} \\ & \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}, \mathrm{E}^0=+0.77 \mathrm{~V} \\ & \mathrm{Co}^{3+}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Co}^{2+}, \mathrm{E}^0=+1.81 \mathrm{~V} \\ & \mathrm{Cr}^{3+}+3 \mathrm{e}^{-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{s}), \mathrm{E}^0=-0.74 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$
The tendency to act as reducing agent decreases in the order :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Calculate the Specific Conductance (}\kappa\text{)}}$

$\bullet$ Conductance ($G$) = $4 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~S}$.
$\bullet$ Area ($A$) = $1 \mathrm{~cm}^2 = 1 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2$.
$\bullet$ Distance ($l$) = $15 \mathrm{~cm} = 0.15 \mathrm{~m}$.
$\bullet$ $\kappa = G \times \frac{l}{A} = (4 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~S}) \times \frac{0.15 \mathrm{~m}}{1 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2} = 0.06 \mathrm{~S \, m}^{-1}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Determine Concentration (} C \text{) from pH}}$

$\bullet$ Given $\mathrm{pH} = 5$, then $[\mathrm{H}^+] = 10^{-5} \mathrm{~mol \, L}^{-1}$.
$\bullet$ For a weak acid, $[\mathrm{H}^+] = C\alpha$. Since $\alpha \ll 1$, we can relate $\mathrm{H}^+$ and molar conductivity later, but first we need molar conductivity $\Lambda_m = \frac{\kappa}{C}$.
$\bullet$ To find $C$, we use the concentration in SI units: $C = \frac{[\mathrm{H}^+]}{\alpha}$. 
$\bullet$ $\Lambda_m = \frac{\kappa}{C} = \frac{\kappa \alpha}{[\mathrm{H}^+]}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Relate Molar Conductivity to Limiting Molar Conductivity (}\Lambda_m^\circ\text{)}}$

$\bullet$ We know $\alpha = \frac{\Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m^\circ}$. Substituting $\Lambda_m$ from Step 2:
$\bullet$ $\Lambda_m = \frac{\kappa \alpha}{10^{-5}}$ (using $C$ in $\mathrm{mol \, L}^{-1}$ adjusted to $\mathrm{mol \, m}^{-3}$).
$\bullet$ $C \text{ (in } \mathrm{mol \, m}^{-3}\text{)} = [\mathrm{H}^+] \times 1000 / \alpha = 10^{-2} / \alpha$.
$\bullet$ $\Lambda_m = \frac{\kappa}{C} = \frac{0.06}{10^{-2} / \alpha} = 6\alpha$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Solve for } \Lambda_m^\circ}$

$\bullet$ Using $\Lambda_m = \Lambda_m^\circ \alpha$ and $\Lambda_m = 6\alpha$:
$\bullet$ $6\alpha = \Lambda_m^\circ \alpha$.
$\bullet$ $\Lambda_m^\circ = 6 \mathrm{~S \, m}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

The value of the limiting molar conductivity is $\pmb{6} \mathrm{~S \, m}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{6}$.

text:
For a strong electrolyte, $\Lambda_m$ increases slowly with dilution and can be represented by the equation:
$$\Lambda_m = \Lambda_m^\circ - A c^{1/2}$$
Molar conductivity values of the solutions of strong electrolyte AB at $18^\circ\text{C}$ are given below:
$$\begin{array}{|l|c|c|c|c|} 
\hline 
c \text{ [mol L}^{-1}] & 0.04 & 0.09 & 0.16 & 0.25 \\ 
\hline 
\Lambda_m \text{ [S cm}^2 \text{ mol}^{-1}] & 96.1 & 95.7 & 95.3 & 94.9 \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$
The value of constant $A$ based on the above data [in $\text{S cm}^2 \text{ mol}^{-1} / (\text{mol/L})^{1/2}$] unit is $\dots\dots$.

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Understand the Debye-Hückel-Onsager Equation}}$

For strong electrolytes, the relationship between molar conductivity $(\Lambda_m)$ and concentration $(c)$ is linear when plotted against the square root of concentration $(\sqrt{c})$. The equation is:
$$\Lambda_m = \Lambda_m^\circ - A\sqrt{c}$$
This equation is in the form of a straight line, $y = mx + c$, where:
$\bullet$ $y = \Lambda_m$
$\bullet$ $x = \sqrt{c}$
$\bullet$ $\text{Slope } (m) = -A$
$\bullet$ $y\text{-intercept} = \Lambda_m^\circ$



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Prepare the data for calculation}}$

We calculate $\sqrt{c}$ for the given concentrations:
$$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|} 
\hline 
c \text{ (mol L}^{-1}) & \sqrt{c} \text{ (mol L}^{-1})^{1/2} & \Lambda_m \text{ (S cm}^2 \text{ mol}^{-1}) \\ 
\hline 
0.04 & \sqrt{0.04} = 0.2 & 96.1 \\ 
0.09 & \sqrt{0.09} = 0.3 & 95.7 \\ 
0.16 & \sqrt{0.16} = 0.4 & 95.3 \\ 
0.25 & \sqrt{0.25} = 0.5 & 94.9 \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate the constant A}}$

The constant $A$ is the negative of the slope of the line. We can pick any two points from the table. Let's use the first and last points:
$$\text{Slope } (m) = \frac{\Delta \Lambda_m}{\Delta \sqrt{c}} = \frac{\Lambda_{m2} - \Lambda_{m1}}{\sqrt{c_2} - \sqrt{c_1}}$$
$$m = \frac{94.9 - 96.1}{0.5 - 0.2} = \frac{-1.2}{0.3} = -4$$

Since $m = -A$:
$$-A = -4 \implies A = 4$$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
The value of constant $A$ for the strong electrolyte AB is $4$.

Therefore, the final answer is $\boxed{4}$.

text:
Consider the following redox reaction taking place in acidic medium
$$ \text{BH}_4^{-}(aq) + \text{ClO}_3^{-}(aq) \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{BO}_3^{-}(aq) + \text{Cl}^{-}(aq) $$
If the Nernst equation for the above balanced reaction is
$$ E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cell}}^{\circ} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q, $$
then the value of $n$ is $\dots\dots$. (Nearest integer)

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Assign Oxidation States to Central Atoms}}$

To find the value of $n$ (the total number of electrons transferred in the balanced redox reaction), we calculate the change in oxidation states for the elements being oxidized and reduced.

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|} 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{Species}} & \pmb{\text{Element}} & \pmb{\text{Oxidation State Calculation}} \\ 
\hline 
\text{BH}_4^{-} & \text{B} & x + 4(+1) = -1 \implies x = -5 \\ 
\hline 
\text{H}_2\text{BO}_3^{-} & \text{B} & 2(+1) + x + 3(-2) = -1 \implies x = +3 \\ 
\hline 
\text{ClO}_3^{-} & \text{Cl} & x + 3(-2) = -1 \implies x = +5 \\ 
\hline 
\text{Cl}^{-} & \text{Cl} & x = -1 \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Determine Electrons Transferred per Half-Reaction}}$



$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Oxidation (Boron):}}$
$$\text{B}^{-5} \rightarrow \text{B}^{+3} + 8e^-$$
Change in oxidation state = $8$ electrons per Boron atom.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Reduction (Chlorine):}}$
$$\text{Cl}^{+5} + 6e^- \rightarrow \text{Cl}^{-1}$$
Change in oxidation state = $6$ electrons per Chlorine atom.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Balance the Total Electrons (n)}}$

To balance the overall redox equation, the number of electrons lost must equal the number of electrons gained. We find the $\pmb{\text{Least Common Multiple (LCM)}}$ of $8$ and $6$.

$$\text{LCM}(8, 6) = 24$$

$\pmb{\text{Verification via Balanced Equations:}}$

$\bullet$ Multiply oxidation half-reaction by $3$: $3 \times 8e^- = 24e^-$
$\bullet$ Multiply reduction half-reaction by $4$: $4 \times 6e^- = 24e^-$



$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
The total number of electrons exchanged in the balanced chemical equation is $24$. Therefore, $n = 24$.

Therefore, the final answer is $\boxed{24}$.

text:
Molar conductivity of a weak acid HQ of concentration 0.18 M was found to be $1 / 30$ of the molar conductivity of another weak acid HZ with concentration of 0.02 M . If $\lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{Q}^{-}$ happened to be equal with $\lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{Z}^{-}$, then the difference of the $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}$ values of the two weak acids $\left(\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HQ})-\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{a}}(\mathrm{HZ})\right)$ is $\_\_\_\_$ (Nearest integer).
[Given: degree of dissociation $(\alpha) \ll 1$ for both weak acids, $\lambda^{\circ}$ : limiting molar conductivity of ions]

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Relate Molar Conductivity to Degree of Dissociation (}\alpha\text{)}}$

$\bullet$ The degree of dissociation is given by $\alpha = \frac{\Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m^\circ}$.
$\bullet$ Since $\lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{Q}^{-} = \lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{Z}^{-}$ and $\lambda^{\circ} \mathrm{H}^{+}$ is common, the limiting molar conductivity $\Lambda_m^\circ$ is the same for both acids (HQ and HZ).
$\bullet$ Given $\Lambda_m(\text{HQ}) = \frac{1}{30} \Lambda_m(\text{HZ})$, it follows that $\alpha_{\text{HQ}} = \frac{1}{30} \alpha_{\text{HZ}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Express the Acid Dissociation Constant (K}_a\pmb{\text{)}}}$

$\bullet$ For a weak acid where $\alpha \ll 1$, the dissociation constant is $K_a = C \alpha^2$.
$\bullet$ For HQ: $K_a(\text{HQ}) = C_{\text{HQ}} \cdot \alpha_{\text{HQ}}^2 = 0.18 \cdot \alpha_{\text{HQ}}^2$.
$\bullet$ For HZ: $K_a(\text{HZ}) = C_{\text{HZ}} \cdot \alpha_{\text{HZ}}^2 = 0.02 \cdot \alpha_{\text{HZ}}^2$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Find the ratio of the K}_a\pmb{\text{ values}}}$

$$\frac{K_a(\text{HQ})}{K_a(\text{HZ})} = \frac{0.18 \cdot \alpha_{\text{HQ}}^2}{0.02 \cdot \alpha_{\text{HZ}}^2} = \frac{0.18}{0.02} \left( \frac{\alpha_{\text{HQ}}}{\alpha_{\text{HZ}}} \right)^2$$
Substituting $\frac{\alpha_{\text{HQ}}}{\alpha_{\text{HZ}}} = \frac{1}{30}$:
$$\frac{K_a(\text{HQ})}{K_a(\text{HZ})} = 9 \cdot \left( \frac{1}{30} \right)^2 = 9 \cdot \frac{1}{900} = \frac{1}{100} = 10^{-2}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Calculate the difference in pK}_a\pmb{\text{ values}}}$

$\bullet$ Taking negative logarithm on both sides:
$$-\log \left( \frac{K_a(\text{HQ})}{K_a(\text{HZ})} \right) = -\log(10^{-2})$$
$$\text{pK}_a(\text{HQ}) - \text{pK}_a(\text{HZ}) = 2$$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

The difference in the $\mathrm{pK}_a$ values of the two weak acids is $\pmb{2}$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\pmb{2}$.

text:
Electricity is passed through an acidic solution of $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ till all the $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ was exhausted, leading to the deposition of $300 \text{ mg}$ of $\text{Cu}$ metal. However, a current of $600 \text{ mA}$ was continued to pass through the same solution for another $28$ minutes by keeping the total volume of the solution fixed at $200 \text{ mL}$. The total volume of oxygen evolved at STP during the entire process is $\dots\dots \text{ mL}$. (Nearest integer)
[Given:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \text{Cu}^{2+}(\text{aq}) + 2\text{e}^- \rightarrow \text{Cu(s)} \quad E^o_{\text{red}} = +0.34 \text{ V} \\ & \text{O}_2(\text{g}) + 4\text{H}^+ + 4\text{e}^- \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \quad E^o_{\text{red}} = +1.23 \text{ V} \end{aligned} $$
Molar mass of $\text{Cu} = 63.54 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$
Molar mass of $\text{O}_2 = 32 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$
Faraday Constant $= 96500 \text{ C mol}^{-1}$
Molar volume at $\text{STP} = 22.4 \text{ L}$]

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Understand the Electrolytic Process}}$

In an acidic solution of $\text{CuSO}_4$, the following reactions occur:
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{At Cathode:}}$ $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ is reduced to $\text{Cu(s)}$. Once all $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ is exhausted, $\text{H}^+$ ions are reduced to $\text{H}_2(\text{g})$.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{At Anode:}}$ Water is oxidized to produce $\text{O}_2(\text{g})$.



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate Moles of Oxygen from Cu Deposition}}$

According to Faraday's Laws of Electrolysis, the number of equivalents of substance reacting at each electrode is the same.
$$\text{Equivalents of Cu deposited} = \text{Equivalents of O}_2 \text{ evolved (Phase 1)}$$

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{Parameter}} & \pmb{\text{Calculation}} \\ 
\hline 
\text{Mass of Cu} & 300 \text{ mg} = 0.3 \text{ g} \\ 
\hline 
\text{Moles of Cu} & 0.3 / 63.54 \approx 0.004721 \text{ mol} \\ 
\hline 
\text{n-factor of Cu} & 2 \text{ (from Cu}^{2+} \rightarrow \text{Cu)} \\ 
\hline 
\text{Equivalents of Cu} & 0.004721 \times 2 = 0.009442 \text{ eq} \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

Since $4$ equivalents of $\text{O}_2$ occupy $22.4 \text{ L}$ at STP ($n$-factor for $\text{O}_2$ is $4$):
$$\text{Moles of O}_2 \text{ (Phase 1)} = \frac{0.009442}{4} = 0.0023605 \text{ mol}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate Moles of Oxygen from Continued Electrolysis}}$

After $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ is exhausted, the current continues to evolve $\text{O}_2$ at the anode.
$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{Parameter}} & \pmb{\text{Value}} \\ 
\hline 
\text{Current (I)} & 600 \text{ mA} = 0.6 \text{ A} \\ 
\hline 
\text{Time (t)} & 28 \text{ min} = 28 \times 60 = 1680 \text{ s} \\ 
\hline 
\text{Charge (Q)} & I \times t = 0.6 \times 1680 = 1008 \text{ C} \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$$\text{Moles of O}_2 \text{ (Phase 2)} = \frac{Q}{n \cdot F} = \frac{1008}{4 \times 96500} \approx 0.0026114 \text{ mol}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Calculate Total Volume of Oxygen at STP}}$

$$\text{Total Moles of O}_2 = 0.0023605 + 0.0026114 = 0.0049719 \text{ mol}$$
$$\text{Total Volume (mL)} = 0.0049719 \times 22400 \text{ mL/mol}$$
$$\text{Total Volume} \approx 111.37 \text{ mL}$$

Rounding to the nearest integer, we get $111 \text{ mL}$.

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
The total volume of oxygen evolved is $111 \text{ mL}$.

Therefore, the final answer is $\boxed{111}$.

text:
Consider the following electrochemical cell :
$$ \mathrm{Pt}\left|\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{g})(1 \mathrm{bar})\right| \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \| \mathrm{M}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq}, 1.0 \mathrm{M}) \mid \mathrm{M}(\mathrm{s}) $$
The pH above which, oxygen gas would start to evolve at anode is $\_\_\_\_$ (nearest integer).
$$ \left.\left[\begin{array}{ll} \text { Given : } & E_{\mathrm{M}^{2+} / \mathrm{M}}^{\circ} = 0.994 \mathrm{~V} \\ & E_{\mathrm{O}_2 / \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}}^{\circ} = 1.23 \mathrm{~V} \end{array}\right\} \text { standard reduction potential }
\\ \text {and} \frac{RT}{F}(2.303) = 0.059 \mathrm{~V} \text { at the given condition}\right] $$

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Assign Oxidation States to Central Atoms}}$

To find the value of $n$ (the total number of electrons transferred in the balanced redox reaction), we calculate the change in oxidation states for the elements being oxidized and reduced.

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|} 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{Species}} & \pmb{\text{Element}} & \pmb{\text{Oxidation State Calculation}} \\ 
\hline 
\text{BH}_4^{-} & \text{B} & x + 4(+1) = -1 \implies x = -5 \\ 
\hline 
\text{H}_2\text{BO}_3^{-} & \text{B} & 2(+1) + x + 3(-2) = -1 \implies x = +3 \\ 
\hline 
\text{ClO}_3^{-} & \text{Cl} & x + 3(-2) = -1 \implies x = +5 \\ 
\hline 
\text{Cl}^{-} & \text{Cl} & x = -1 \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Determine Electrons Transferred per Half-Reaction}}$



$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Oxidation (Boron):}}$
$$\text{B}^{-5} \rightarrow \text{B}^{+3} + 8e^-$$
Change in oxidation state = $8$ electrons per Boron atom.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Reduction (Chlorine):}}$
$$\text{Cl}^{+5} + 6e^- \rightarrow \text{Cl}^{-1}$$
Change in oxidation state = $6$ electrons per Chlorine atom.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Balance the Total Electrons (n)}}$

To balance the overall redox equation, the number of electrons lost must equal the number of electrons gained. We find the $\pmb{\text{Least Common Multiple (LCM)}}$ of $8$ and $6$.

$$\text{LCM}(8, 6) = 24$$

$\pmb{\text{Verification via Balanced Equations:}}$

$\bullet$ Multiply oxidation half-reaction by $3$: $3 \times 8e^- = 24e^-$
$\bullet$ Multiply reduction half-reaction by $4$: $4 \times 6e^- = 24e^-$



$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
The total number of electrons exchanged in the balanced chemical equation is $24$. Therefore, $n = 24$.

Therefore, the final answer is $\boxed{24}$.

text:
Consider the following electrochemical cell at $298 \text{ K}$:
$$\mathrm{Pt \mid HSnO_2^-(aq) \mid Sn(OH)_6^{2-}(aq) \mid OH^-(aq) \mid Bi_2O_3(s) \mid Bi(s)}$$
If the reaction quotient at a given time is $10^6$, then the cell EMF $(\mathrm{E}_{cell})$ is $\_\_\_\_$ $\times 10^{-1} \text{ V}$ (Nearest integer).
Given the standard half-cell reduction potentials as:
$$E_{\mathrm{Bi_2O_3 / Bi, OH^-}}^o = -0.44 \text{ V} \quad \text{and} \quad E_{\mathrm{Sn(OH)_6^{2-} / HSnO_2^-, OH^-}}^o = -0.90 \text{ V}$$

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Identify Anode and Cathode half-reactions}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Anode (Oxidation):}}$ Based on the cell notation, Tin (Sn) species are at the anode. 
  Reaction: $\mathrm{HSnO_2^- + 3OH^- + H_2O \rightarrow Sn(OH)_6^{2-} + 2e^-}$
  $\mathrm{E}_{anode(red)}^o = -0.90 \text{ V}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Cathode (Reduction):}}$ Bismuth (Bi) species are at the cathode.
  Reaction: $\mathrm{Bi_2O_3 + 3H_2O + 6e^- \rightarrow 2Bi + 6OH^-}$
  $\mathrm{E}_{cathode(red)}^o = -0.44 \text{ V}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Balance the electrons and find the overall reaction}}$

To balance electrons ($n=6$), multiply the anode reaction by 3:
$\bullet$ $3 \times [\mathrm{HSnO_2^- + 3OH^- + H_2O \rightarrow Sn(OH)_6^{2-} + 2e^-}]$
$\bullet$ $\mathrm{3HSnO_2^- + 9OH^- + 3H_2O \rightarrow 3Sn(OH)_6^{2-} + 6e^-}$

Overall cell reaction:
$$\mathrm{3HSnO_2^-(aq) + Bi_2O_3(s) + 3H_2O(l) \rightarrow 3Sn(OH)_6^{2-}(aq) + 2Bi(s) + 3OH^-(aq)}$$
Total electrons transferred, $\pmb{n = 6}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate Standard Cell Potential (E}_{cell}^o\pmb{\text{)}}}$

$$\mathrm{E}_{cell}^o = \mathrm{E}_{cathode}^o - \mathrm{E}_{anode}^o$$
$$\mathrm{E}_{cell}^o = -0.44 - (-0.90) = \pmb{0.46 \text{ V}}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Apply the Nernst Equation}}$

Given the reaction quotient $\pmb{Q = 10^6}$ at $298 \text{ K}$:
$$\mathrm{E}_{cell} = \mathrm{E}_{cell}^o - \frac{0.059}{n} \log Q$$
$$\mathrm{E}_{cell} = 0.46 - \frac{0.059}{6} \log(10^6)$$
$$\mathrm{E}_{cell} = 0.46 - \frac{0.059}{6} \times 6$$
$$\mathrm{E}_{cell} = 0.46 - 0.059 = \pmb{0.401 \text{ V}}$$

$\pmb{\text{Final Conversion:}}$

The value is requested in the form $\_\_\_\_ \times 10^{-1} \text{ V}$.
$$0.401 \text{ V} = 4.01 \times 10^{-1} \text{ V}$$
Rounding to the nearest integer, we get $\pmb{4}$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\pmb{4}$.

text:
MX is a sparingly soluble salt that follows the given solubility equilibrium at $298 \text{ K}$.
$\text{MX(s)} \rightleftharpoons \text{M}^+(\text{aq}) + \text{X}^-(\text{aq}); \quad K_{sp} = 10^{-10}$
If the standard reduction potential for $\text{M}^+(\text{aq}) + e^- \rightarrow \text{M(s)}$ is $(E^\ominus_{\text{M}^+/\text{M}}) = 0.79 \text{ V}$, then the value of the standard reduction potential for the metal/metal insoluble salt electrode $E^\ominus_{\text{X}^-/\text{MX(s)}/\text{M}}$ is $\dots\dots \text{ mV}$. (nearest integer)
[Given: $\frac{2.303 RT}{F} = 0.059 \text{ V}$]

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Define the half-cell reactions}}$

We are dealing with two related electrode processes:

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|} 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{Electrode}} & \pmb{\text{Half-reaction}} & \pmb{\text{Standard Potential}} \\ 
\hline 
\text{Metal/Metal ion} & \text{M}^+(\text{aq}) + e^- \rightarrow \text{M(s)} & E^\ominus_{\text{M}^+/\text{M}} = 0.79 \text{ V} \\ 
\hline 
\text{Metal/Insoluble salt} & \text{MX(s)} + e^- \rightarrow \text{M(s)} + \text{X}^-(\text{aq}) & E^\ominus_{\text{X}^-/\text{MX}/\text{M}} = ? \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Relate the potentials using the solubility equilibrium}}$

The reduction potential of the metal electrode immersed in a solution saturated with its insoluble salt is given by the Nernst equation at standard conditions (where $[\text{X}^-] = 1 \text{ M}$):
$$E_{\text{MX}/\text{M}} = E^\ominus_{\text{M}^+/\text{M}} + \frac{0.059}{n} \log[\text{M}^+]$$

From the solubility product $K_{sp} = [\text{M}^+][\text{X}^-]$. Since $[\text{X}^-] = 1 \text{ M}$ at standard state for the $\text{X}^-/\text{MX}/\text{M}$ electrode:
$$[\text{M}^+] = \frac{K_{sp}}{1} = K_{sp}$$

Substituting this into the Nernst equation, we get the relationship:
$$E^\ominus_{\text{X}^-/\text{MX}/\text{M}} = E^\ominus_{\text{M}^+/\text{M}} + 0.059 \log(K_{sp})$$



$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate the value}}$

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{Parameter}} & \pmb{\text{Value}} \\ 
\hline 
E^\ominus_{\text{M}^+/\text{M}} & 0.79 \text{ V} \\ 
\hline 
\log(K_{sp}) & \log(10^{-10}) = -10 \\ 
\hline 
\text{Correction term} & 0.059 \times (-10) = -0.59 \text{ V} \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$$E^\ominus_{\text{X}^-/\text{MX}/\text{M}} = 0.79 - 0.59 = 0.20 \text{ V}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Convert to mV}}$

$$0.20 \text{ V} = 0.20 \times 1000 = 200 \text{ mV}$$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
The standard reduction potential for the metal/metal insoluble salt electrode is $200 \text{ mV}$.

Therefore, the final answer is $\boxed{200}$.

text:
The pH and conductance of a weak acid $(\mathrm{HX})$ was found to be 5 and $4 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~S}$, respectively. The conductance was measured under standard condition using a cell where the electrode plates having a surface area of $1 \mathrm{~cm}^2$ were at a distance of 15 cm apart. The value of the limiting molar conductivity is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mathrm{S} \mathrm{m}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. (nearest integer)
(Given : degree of dissociation of the weak acid $(\alpha) \ll 1$ )

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Calculate the Specific Conductance (}\kappa\text{)}}$

$\bullet$ Conductance ($G$) = $4 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~S}$.
$\bullet$ Area ($A$) = $1 \mathrm{~cm}^2 = 1 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2$.
$\bullet$ Distance ($l$) = $15 \mathrm{~cm} = 0.15 \mathrm{~m}$.
$\bullet$ $\kappa = G \times \frac{l}{A} = (4 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~S}) \times \frac{0.15 \mathrm{~m}}{1 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2} = 0.06 \mathrm{~S \, m}^{-1}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Determine Concentration (} C \text{) from pH}}$

$\bullet$ Given $\mathrm{pH} = 5$, then $[\mathrm{H}^+] = 10^{-5} \mathrm{~mol \, L}^{-1}$.
$\bullet$ For a weak acid, $[\mathrm{H}^+] = C\alpha$. Since $\alpha \ll 1$, we can relate $\mathrm{H}^+$ and molar conductivity later, but first we need molar conductivity $\Lambda_m = \frac{\kappa}{C}$.
$\bullet$ To find $C$, we use the concentration in SI units: $C = \frac{[\mathrm{H}^+]}{\alpha}$. 
$\bullet$ $\Lambda_m = \frac{\kappa}{C} = \frac{\kappa \alpha}{[\mathrm{H}^+]}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Relate Molar Conductivity to Limiting Molar Conductivity (}\Lambda_m^\circ\text{)}}$

$\bullet$ We know $\alpha = \frac{\Lambda_m}{\Lambda_m^\circ}$. Substituting $\Lambda_m$ from Step 2:
$\bullet$ $\Lambda_m = \frac{\kappa \alpha}{10^{-5}}$ (using $C$ in $\mathrm{mol \, L}^{-1}$ adjusted to $\mathrm{mol \, m}^{-3}$).
$\bullet$ $C \text{ (in } \mathrm{mol \, m}^{-3}\text{)} = [\mathrm{H}^+] \times 1000 / \alpha = 10^{-2} / \alpha$.
$\bullet$ $\Lambda_m = \frac{\kappa}{C} = \frac{0.06}{10^{-2} / \alpha} = 6\alpha$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Solve for } \Lambda_m^\circ}$

$\bullet$ Using $\Lambda_m = \Lambda_m^\circ \alpha$ and $\Lambda_m = 6\alpha$:
$\bullet$ $6\alpha = \Lambda_m^\circ \alpha$.
$\bullet$ $\Lambda_m^\circ = 6 \mathrm{~S \, m}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

The value of the limiting molar conductivity is $\pmb{6} \mathrm{~S \, m}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{6}$.

text:
$$ \text{Match the LIST-I with LIST-II:} $$

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \text{LIST-I} & \text{LIST-II} \\ \hline \text{A. Magnetic induction} & \text{I. } [\mathrm{M L T^{-2} A^{-2}}] \\ \hline \text{B. Magnetic flux} & \text{II. } [\mathrm{M L^{2} T^{-2} A^{-2}}] \\ \hline \text{C. Magnetic permeability} & \text{III. } [\mathrm{M L^{0} T^{-2} A^{-1}}] \\ \hline \text{D. Self inductance} & \text{IV. } [\mathrm{M L^{2} T^{-2} A^{-1}}] \\ \hline \end{array}$$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Determine the Dimensions for each Physical Quantity}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{A. Magnetic Induction (B):}}$ Using the Lorentz force formula $F = qvB$, we have $B = \frac{F}{qv}$. Dimensions: $[B] = \frac{[M L T^{-2}]}{[A T][L T^{-1}]} = [M L^{0} T^{-2} A^{-1}]$. $\rightarrow$ $\pmb{\text{Matches with III.}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{B. Magnetic Flux (}\phi\text{):}}$ Using $\phi = B \times \text{Area}$. Dimensions: $[\phi] = [M L^0 T^{-2} A^{-1}] \times [L^2] = [M L^2 T^{-2} A^{-1}]$. $\rightarrow$ $\pmb{\text{Matches with IV.}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{C. Magnetic Permeability (}\mu_0\text{):}}$ From Ampere's Law for a wire, $B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$, so $\mu_0 = \frac{B \times r}{I}$. Dimensions: $[\mu_0] = \frac{[M T^{-2} A^{-1}][L]}{[A]} = [M L T^{-2} A^{-2}]$. $\rightarrow$ $\pmb{\text{Matches with I.}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{D. Self Inductance (L):}}$ From energy $U = \frac{1}{2}LI^2$, $L = \frac{2U}{I^2}$. Dimensions: $[L] = \frac{[M L^2 T^{-2}]}{[A^2]} = [M L^2 T^{-2} A^{-2}]$. $\rightarrow$ $\pmb{\text{Matches with II.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Conclusion}}$

$\bullet$ The correct mapping is $\pmb{\text{A-III, B-IV, C-I, D-II}}$.

Therefore, the correct option is $\boxed{\text{D}}$.

text:
An electromagnetic wave of frequency $100\text{ MHz}$ propagates through a medium of conductivity, $\sigma = 10\text{ mho/m}$. The ratio of maximum conduction current density to maximum displacement current density is $\_\_\_\_$. 

$$ \left[\text{Take } \frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} = 9 \times 10^9\text{ Nm}^2 / \text{C}^2\right] $$

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Define conduction and displacement current densities}}$
For an electromagnetic wave in a conducting medium with an electric field $E = E_0 \sin(\omega t)$, the current densities are:
$\bullet$  Maximum conduction current density: $J_{c,max} = \sigma E_0$
$\bullet$  Maximum displacement current density: $J_{d,max} = \epsilon_0 \omega E_0$



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Determine the ratio formula}}$
The ratio of the maximum conduction current density to the maximum displacement current density is:
$$\frac{J_{c,max}}{J_{d,max}} = \frac{\sigma E_0}{\epsilon_0 \omega E_0} = \frac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0 \omega}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate the angular frequency } \omega}$
Given frequency $f = 100\text{ MHz} = 10^8\text{ Hz}$:
$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 10^8\text{ rad/s}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Determine the permittivity } \epsilon_0}$
From the given value $\frac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} = 9 \times 10^9$:
$$\epsilon_0 = \frac{1}{4\pi \times 9 \times 10^9} = \frac{1}{36\pi \times 10^9} = \frac{10^{-9}}{36\pi}\text{ F/m}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 5: Compute the final ratio}}$
Substitute the values into the ratio formula:
$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{10}{\left(\frac{10^{-9}}{36\pi}\right) \times (2\pi \times 10^8)}$$
$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{10 \times 36\pi}{2\pi \times 10^8 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{360\pi}{2\pi \times 10^{-1}}$$
$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{180}{10^{-1}} = 1800$$

Therefore, the ratio is $\boxed{1800}$.

text:
The electric field of an electromagnetic wave travelling through a medium is given by $\vec{E}(x, t)=25 \sin \left(2.0 \times 10^{15} t-10^7 x\right) \hat{n}$ then the refractive index of the medium is ________ .
(All given measurement are in SI units)

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Identify wave parameters from the electric field equation}}$
The given electric field is $E(x, t) = 25 \sin(2.0 \times 10^{15}t - 10^7x)$ V/m. This follows the standard wave form:
$$E = E_0 \sin(\omega t - k x)$$
By comparison, we find:
$\bullet$ Angular frequency $\omega = 2.0 \times 10^{15} \text{ rad/s}$
$\bullet$ Wave number $k = 10^7 \text{ rad/m}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate the speed of the wave in the medium (} v \text{)}}$
The speed of an electromagnetic wave in a medium is given by the ratio of angular frequency to wave number:
$$v = \frac{\omega}{k} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{15}}{10^7} = 2.0 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate the refractive index (} n \text{)}}$
The refractive index is defined as the ratio of the speed of light in vacuum ($c$) to the speed of light in the medium ($v$):
$$n = \frac{c}{v}$$
Using $c = 3.0 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$:
$$n = \frac{3.0 \times 10^8}{2.0 \times 10^8} = 1.5$$

Therefore, the refractive index of the medium is $\boxed{1.5}$.

text:
Match the LIST-I with LIST-II:

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} 
\hline 
\textbf{List-I} & \textbf{List-II} \\ 
\hline 
\text{A. Radio-wave} & \text{I. is produced by Magnetron valve} \\ 
\hline 
\text{B. Micro-wave} & \text{II. due to change in the vibrational modes of atoms} \\ 
\hline 
\text{C. Infrared-wave} & \text{III. due to inner shell electrons moving from higher to lower energy level} \\ 
\hline 
\text{D. X-ray} & \text{IV. due to rapid acceleration of electrons} \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Analysis of Electromagnetic Wave Sources:}}$

$\bullet$  $\textbf{A. Radio-wave:}$ These waves are typically produced by the rapid acceleration and deceleration of electrons within aerials, antennas, and transmitters. 
$\pmb{A \rightarrow \text{IV}}$



$\bullet$  $\textbf{B. Micro-wave:}$ Microwaves are generated by special vacuum tubes, such as a Magnetron valve, klystrons, or Gunn diodes. 
$\pmb{B \rightarrow \text{I}}$

$\bullet$  $\textbf{C. Infrared-wave:}$ Infrared radiation is often referred to as heat waves and is produced due to changes in the vibrational and rotational modes of atoms and molecules. 
$\pmb{C \rightarrow \text{II}}$

$\bullet$  $\textbf{D. X-ray:}$ X-rays are produced when high-energy electrons strike a metal target or due to inner shell electrons in an atom moving from higher energy levels to lower energy levels. 
$\pmb{D \rightarrow \text{III}}$

$\pmb{\text{Correct Matching:}}$
$$(A)\text{-}(\text{IV}), \quad (B)\text{-}(\text{I}), \quad (C)\text{-}(\text{II}), \quad (D)\text{-}(\text{III})$$

Therefore, the correct option is $\boxed{(2)}$.

text: The ratio of speeds of electromagnetic waves in vacuum and a medium, having dielectric constant $k=3$ and permeability of $\mu=2 \mu_0$, is ( $\mu_0=$ permeability of vacuum)

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Identify the formula for speed of light in vacuum}}$
The speed of an electromagnetic wave in vacuum ($c$) is related to its permeability ($\mu_0$) and permittivity ($\epsilon_0$) by:
$$c = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \epsilon_0}}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Identify the formula for speed in a medium}}$
The speed of light in a medium ($v$) is given by:
$$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu \epsilon}}$$
Given that the permeability $\mu = 2\mu_0$ and dielectric constant $k = 3$ (where $\epsilon = k\epsilon_0$), we substitute these values:
$$v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(2\mu_0) \times (3\epsilon_0)}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{6\mu_0 \epsilon_0}}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate the ratio of speeds}}$
To find the ratio of speed in vacuum to speed in the medium ($c/v$):
$$\frac{c}{v} = \frac{\frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \epsilon_0}}}{\frac{1}{\sqrt{6\mu_0 \epsilon_0}}} = \frac{\sqrt{6\mu_0 \epsilon_0}}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \epsilon_0}}$$
$$\frac{c}{v} = \sqrt{6}$$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
The ratio of speeds $c:v$ is $\sqrt{6}:1$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\sqrt{6}: 1}$.

text:
Match List - I with List - II.

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} 
\hline 
\textbf{List - I (Relation)} & \textbf{List - II (Law)} \\ 
\hline 
\text{A. } \oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = -\frac{d}{dt} \oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{a} & \text{I. Ampere's circuital law} \\ 
\hline 
\text{B. } \oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 \left( I + \epsilon_0 \frac{d\phi_E}{dt} \right) & \text{II. Faraday's laws of electromagnetic induction} \\ 
\hline 
\text{C. } \oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{a} = \frac{1}{\epsilon_0} \int_V \rho dv & \text{III. Ampere - Maxwell law} \\ 
\hline 
\text{D. } \oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I & \text{IV. Gauss's law of electrostatics} \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Analysis of Maxwell's Equations and Electromagnetic Laws:}}$

$\bullet$ $\textbf{A. Faraday's Law:}$ The equation $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{l} = -\frac{d}{dt} \oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{a}$ represents the line integral of the electric field around a closed loop, which equals the negative rate of change of magnetic flux through the surface enclosed by the loop. This is the integral form of Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction.
$\pmb{A \rightarrow \text{II}}$



$\bullet$ $\textbf{B. Ampere-Maxwell Law:}$ The relation $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 \left( I + \epsilon_0 \frac{d\phi_E}{dt} \right)$ shows that magnetic fields are produced by both conduction currents ($I$) and changing electric fields (displacement current $\epsilon_0 \frac{d\phi_E}{dt}$). This is known as the Ampere-Maxwell law.
$\pmb{B \rightarrow \text{III}}$

$\bullet$ $\textbf{C. Gauss's Law for Electrostatics:}$ The relation $\oint \vec{E} \cdot d\vec{a} = \frac{1}{\epsilon_0} \int_V \rho dv$ states that the net electric flux through any closed surface is proportional to the total enclosed electric charge. This is Gauss's law of electrostatics.
$\pmb{C \rightarrow \text{IV}}$

$\bullet$ $\textbf{D. Ampere's Circuital Law:}$ The equation $\oint \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{l} = \mu_0 I$ relates the integrated magnetic field around a closed loop to the electric current passing through the loop. This is the original form of Ampere's circuital law.
$\pmb{D \rightarrow \text{I}}$

$\pmb{\text{Correct Matching:}}$
$$(A)\text{-}(\text{II}), \quad (B)\text{-}(\text{III}), \quad (C)\text{-}(\text{IV}), \quad (D)\text{-}(\text{I})$$

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{(2)}$.

text:
A laser beam has intensity of $4.0 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}^2$. The amplitude of magnetic field associated with beam is ________ T.
(Take $\epsilon_{\mathrm{o}}=8.85 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^2 / \mathrm{Nm}^2$ and $\mathrm{c}=3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ )

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Identify the intensity formula for an EM wave}}$
The average intensity $I$ of an electromagnetic wave is related to the electric field amplitude $E_0$ by:
$$I = \frac{1}{2}\epsilon_0 c E_0^2$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Relate Electric and Magnetic field amplitudes}}$
The amplitudes of the electric and magnetic fields are related by the speed of light $c$:
$$E_0 = cB_0$$
Substituting this into the intensity formula gives:
$$I = \frac{1}{2}\epsilon_0 c (cB_0)^2 = \frac{1}{2}\epsilon_0 c^3 B_0^2$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Solve for the magnetic field amplitude } B_0}$
Rearranging the formula to solve for $B_0$:
$$B_0 = \sqrt{\frac{2I}{\epsilon_0 c^3}}$$


$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Substitute the given values}}$
$\bullet$ $I = 4.0 \times 10^{14} \text{ W/m}^2$
$\bullet$ $\epsilon_0 = 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C}^2/\text{Nm}^2$
$\bullet$ $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$

$$B_0 = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 4.0 \times 10^{14}}{8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times (3 \times 10^8)^3}}$$
$$B_0 = \sqrt{\frac{8.0 \times 10^{14}}{2.39 \times 10^{14}}} = \sqrt{3.347} \approx 1.83 \text{ T}$$

Therefore, the amplitude of the magnetic field is $\boxed{1.83 \text{ T}}$.

text:
The electric field in a plane electromagnetic wave is given by :
$$ E_y=69 \sin \left[0.6 \times 10^3 x-1.8 \times 10^{11} t\right] \mathrm{V} / \mathrm{m} . $$

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Determine the direction of propagation}}$ 
The given electric field equation is in the form $E_y = E_0 \sin(kx - \omega t)$. 
$\bullet$  Since the argument is $(kx - \omega t)$, the wave propagates along the $+x$ direction. 
$\bullet$  The electric field $\vec{E}$ is along the $y$-direction (given as $E_y$). 



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Determine the direction of the magnetic field}}$ 
For an electromagnetic wave, the direction of propagation is given by the vector product $\vec{E} \times \vec{B}$. 
$\bullet$  We have $\hat{j} \times \hat{B} = \hat{i}$. 
$\bullet$  From the properties of unit vectors, $\hat{j} \times \hat{k} = \hat{i}$, which means the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ must be along the $z$-direction. 

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate the maximum magnetic field } B_0}$ 
The relationship between the amplitudes of the electric and magnetic fields in a vacuum is $B_0 = \frac{E_0}{c}$. 
$\bullet$  Given $E_0 = 69 \text{ V/m}$ and taking $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$: 
$$B_0 = \frac{69}{3 \times 10^8} = 23 \times 10^{-8} = 2.3 \times 10^{-7} \text{ T}$$ 

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Formulate the final equation}}$ 
In a plane electromagnetic wave, the electric and magnetic fields are in phase. 
$\bullet$  Therefore, the magnetic field $B_z$ will have the same functional form as $E_y$: 
$$B_z = 2.3 \times 10^{-7} \sin \left[0.6 \times 10^3 x - 1.8 \times 10^{11} t\right] \text{ T}$$ 

Therefore, the correct option is $\boxed{A}$.

text:
The electric field of a plane electromagnetic wave, travelling in an unknown nonmagnetic medium is given by,
$$ E_{\mathrm{y}}=20 \sin \left(3 \times 10^6 x-4.5 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{t}\right) \mathrm{V} / \mathrm{m} $$
(where $x, t$ and other values have S.I. units). The dielectric constant of the medium is ________
(speed of light in free space is $3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ )

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Extract wave parameters from the equation}}$
The given wave equation is $E_y = 20 \sin(3 \times 10^6 x - 4.5 \times 10^{14} t)$. Comparing this with the standard wave equation $E = E_0 \sin(kx - \omega t)$, we identify:
$\bullet$ Wave number $k = 3 \times 10^6 \text{ m}^{-1}$
$\bullet$ Angular frequency $\omega = 4.5 \times 10^{14} \text{ rad/s}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate the speed of light in the medium (} v \text{)}}$
The velocity of the wave in the medium is given by the ratio of angular frequency to wave number:
$$v = \frac{\omega}{k} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{14}}{3 \times 10^6} = 1.5 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Determine the refractive index (} n \text{)}}$
The refractive index $n$ is the ratio of the speed of light in vacuum ($c$) to the speed in the medium ($v$):
$$n = \frac{c}{v} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1.5 \times 10^8} = 2$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Find the dielectric constant (} \epsilon_r \text{)}}$
For a nonmagnetic medium (where relative permeability $\mu_r \approx 1$), the refractive index is related to the dielectric constant (relative permittivity) by:
$$n = \sqrt{\epsilon_r} \implies \epsilon_r = n^2$$
Substituting the value of $n$:
$$\epsilon_r = (2)^2 = 4$$

Therefore, the dielectric constant of the medium is $\boxed{4}$.

text: Three masses $200 \text{ kg}, 300 \text{ kg}$ and $400 \text{ kg}$ are placed at the vertices of an equilateral triangle with sides $20 \text{ m}$. They are rearranged on the vertices of a bigger triangle of side $25 \text{ m}$ and with the same centre. The work done in this process is $\_\_\_\_ \text{ J}$. (Gravitational constant $G = 6.7 \times 10^{-11} \text{ N m}^2 / \text{ kg}^2$)

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Calculate initial potential energy (U}_i\text{).}}$
For $r_1 = 20 \text{ m}$:
$$U_i = -G \frac{(m_1m_2 + m_2m_3 + m_3m_1)}{r_1}$$
$$U_i = -\frac{G}{20}(200 \times 300 + 300 \times 400 + 400 \times 200) = -13000G$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate final potential energy (U}_f\text{).}}$
For $r_2 = 25 \text{ m}$:
$$U_f = -\frac{G}{25}(260000) = -10400G$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate work done (W).}}$
Work done equals the negative change in potential energy, $W = -(U_f - U_i)$:
$$W = -(-10400G + 13000G) = 2600G$$
$$W = 2600 \times 6.7 \times 10^{-11} = 1.742 \times 10^{-7} \text{ J}$$

Therefore, the correct option is $\mathbf{B}$.

text:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: A satellite is moving around Earth in an orbit very close to the Earth's surface. The time period of revolution of the satellite depends upon the density of Earth.

Statement II: The time period of revolution of the satellite is $T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{R_e}{g}}$ (for a satellite very close to the Earth's surface), where $R_e$ is the radius of Earth and $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:

options.text: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Evaluate Statement II}}$

For a satellite orbiting very close to the Earth's surface ($h \approx 0$), the orbital velocity is $v = \sqrt{\frac{GM}{R_e}}$. The time period $T$ is given by:
$$T = \frac{2\pi R_e}{v} = \frac{2\pi R_e}{\sqrt{GM/R_e}} = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{R_e^3}{GM}}$$
Since $g = \frac{GM}{R_e^2}$, we can substitute $\frac{R_e^2}{GM} = \frac{1}{g}$ to get:
$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{R_e}{g}}$$
Thus, $\textbf{Statement II is true}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Evaluate Statement I}}$

Substitute the expression for acceleration due to gravity in terms of density $\rho$:
$$g = \frac{G}{R_e^2} \left( \rho \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R_e^3 \right) = \frac{4}{3}\pi G \rho R_e$$
Substituting this into the time period formula:
$$T = 2\pi \sqrt{\frac{R_e}{\frac{4}{3}\pi G \rho R_e}} = \sqrt{\frac{3\pi}{G\rho}}$$
This expression shows that the time period $T$ depends only on the density $\rho$ of the planet. Thus, $\textbf{Statement I is true}$.

Therefore, both Statement I and Statement II are true.

text: The escape velocity from a spherical planet $A$ is $10 \text{ km/s}$. The escape velocity from another planet $B$ whose density and radius are $10\%$ of those of planet $A$, is ____ $\text{m/s}$.

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Express escape velocity in terms of density and radius}}$

The escape velocity $v_e$ is defined as $v_e = \sqrt{\frac{2GM}{R}}$. Substituting mass $M = \rho \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3$:
$$v_e = \sqrt{\frac{2G}{R} \left( \rho \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 \right)} = R\sqrt{\frac{8\pi G\rho}{3}}$$
Thus, $v_e \propto R\sqrt{\rho}$.



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Compare Planet B to Planet A}}$

Given for Planet $B$: $R_B = 0.1R_A$ and $\rho_B = 0.1\rho_A$.
$$\frac{v_B}{v_A} = \frac{R_B}{R_A} \sqrt{\frac{\rho_B}{\rho_A}} = (0.1) \sqrt{0.1} = \frac{1}{10\sqrt{10}}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate the final value in m/s}}$

$$v_B = v_A \times \frac{1}{10\sqrt{10}} = \frac{10 \text{ km/s}}{10\sqrt{10}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{10}} \text{ km/s}$$
Converting to meters per second:
$$v_B = \frac{1000}{\sqrt{10}} = 100\sqrt{10} \text{ m/s}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{100\sqrt{10}}$.

text: Net gravitational force at the center of a square is found to be $F_1$ when four particles having mass $M, 2 M, 3 M$ and $4 M$ are placed at the four corners of the square as shown in figure and it is $F_2$ when the positions of $3 M$ and $4 M$ are interchanged. The ratio $\frac{F_1}{F_2}$ is $\frac{\alpha}{\sqrt{5}}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Set up the force components.}}$
Let the distance from the center to each corner be $r$. The force due to a mass $m$ is $F = \frac{GMm}{r^2}$. Let $k = \frac{G}{r^2}$.
The net force components along the diagonals are:
Config 1: 
$F_x = k(3M - M) = 2kM$
$F_y = k(4M - 2M) = 2kM$
$F_1 = \sqrt{(2kM)^2 + (2kM)^2} = 2\sqrt{2}kM$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate force for swapped positions.}}$
Interchanging $3M$ and $4M$:
$F_x = k(4M - M) = 3kM$
$F_y = k(3M - 2M) = 1kM$
$F_2 = \sqrt{(3kM)^2 + (kM)^2} = \sqrt{10}kM$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Find the ratio and value of } \alpha.}$ 
$$\frac{F_1}{F_2} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}kM}{\sqrt{10}kM} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2} \times \sqrt{5}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{5}}$$
Comparing with $\frac{\alpha}{\sqrt{5}}$, we get $\alpha = 2$.

Therefore, the correct option is $\mathbf{A}$.

text:
Initially a satellite of $100 \text{ kg}$ is in a circular orbit of radius $1.5 R_E$. This satellite can be moved to a circular orbit of radius $3 R_E$ by supplying $\alpha \times 10^6 \text{ J}$ of energy. The value of $\alpha$ is ____.

(Take Radius of Earth $R_E = 6 \times 10^6 \text{ m}$ and $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$)

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Determine the total energy formula}}$

The total energy $E$ of a satellite of mass $m$ in a circular orbit of radius $r$ is given by:
$$E = -\frac{GMm}{2r} = -\frac{gR_E^2 m}{2r}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate initial and final energies}}$

\bullet $\space$ $\pmb{\text{Initial energy (} E_1 \text{)}}$ at $r_1 = 1.5 R_E$:
$$E_1 = -\frac{gR_E^2 m}{2(1.5 R_E)} = -\frac{mgR_E}{3}$$

\bullet $\space$ $\pmb{\text{Final energy (} E_2 \text{)}}$ at $r_2 = 3 R_E$:
$$E_2 = -\frac{gR_E^2 m}{2(3 R_E)} = -\frac{mgR_E}{6}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate energy supplied (} \Delta E \text{)}}$

The energy required is the difference in total energies:
$$\Delta E = E_2 - E_1 = -\frac{mgR_E}{6} - \left( -\frac{mgR_E}{3} \right) = \frac{mgR_E}{6}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Substitute the given values}}$

Given $m = 100 \text{ kg}$, $g = 10 \text{ m/s}^2$, and $R_E = 6 \times 10^6 \text{ m}$:
$$\Delta E = \frac{100 \times 10 \times 6 \times 10^6}{6} = 10^9 \text{ J}$$

To find $\alpha$, express the energy in terms of $10^6 \text{ J}$:
$$10^9 \text{ J} = 1000 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$$

Therefore, the value of $\alpha$ is $\boxed{1000}$.

text:
$\text{Ph}-\text{CH}=\text{CH}_2 \xrightarrow[(\text{PhCOO})_2]{\text{HBr}} \text{Product}$

Consider the above reaction:

$\pmb{\text{A.}}$ The reaction proceeds through a more stable radical intermediate.
$\pmb{\text{B.}}$ The role of peroxide is to generate $\text{H}^\bullet$ (Hydrogen radical).
$\pmb{\text{C.}}$ During this reaction, benzene is formed as a byproduct.
$\pmb{\text{D.}}$ 1-Bromo-2-phenylethane is formed as the minor product.
$\pmb{\text{E.}}$ The same reaction in absence of peroxide proceeds via carbocation intermediate.

Identify the correct statements. Choose the correct answer from the options given below :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze the reaction mechanism (Peroxide Effect)}}$

The reaction of styrene ($\text{Ph-CH=CH}_2$) with $\text{HBr}$ in the presence of benzoyl peroxide follows the $\pmb{\text{Anti-Markovnikov}}$ radical addition mechanism.



$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement A:}}$ The bromine radical ($\text{Br}^\bullet$) attacks the terminal carbon to form a more stable secondary $\pmb{\text{benzylic radical}}$ ($\text{Ph}-\dot{\text{C}}\text{H}-\text{CH}_2\text{Br}$). $\pmb{\text{Statement A is correct.}}$
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement B:}}$ Peroxides generate $\text{Br}^\bullet$ radicals from $\text{HBr}$, not $\text{H}^\bullet$. The formation of $\text{H}^\bullet$ is energetically unfavorable. $\pmb{\text{Statement B is incorrect.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Formation of byproducts (Statement C)}}$

Benzoyl peroxide undergoes homolysis and subsequent decarboxylation to form phenyl radicals ($\text{Ph}^\bullet$):
$$(\text{PhCOO})_2 \rightarrow 2\text{PhCOO}^\bullet \rightarrow 2\text{Ph}^\bullet + 2\text{CO}_2$$
These phenyl radicals can abstract a hydrogen atom from $\text{HBr}$ to form $\pmb{\text{benzene}}$ ($\text{Ph-H}$):
$$\text{Ph}^\bullet + \text{HBr} \rightarrow \text{Ph-H} + \text{Br}^\bullet$$
$\pmb{\text{Statement C is correct.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Major and Minor products (Statement D)}}$

$\bullet$ In the presence of peroxide, the $\pmb{\text{major product}}$ is 1-bromo-2-phenylethane ($\text{Ph-CH}_2\text{-CH}_2\text{Br}$) due to Anti-Markovnikov addition.
$\bullet$ The statement claims it is the minor product. $\pmb{\text{Statement D is incorrect.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Reaction without peroxide (Statement E)}}$

In the absence of peroxide, the reaction follows the $\pmb{\text{Markovnikov}}$ rule via a stable benzylic $\pmb{\text{carbocation}}$ intermediate ($\text{Ph}-\text{CH}^+-\text{CH}_3$).
$\pmb{\text{Statement E is correct.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|} 
\hline 
\text{A} & \text{B} & \text{C} & \text{D} & \text{E} \\ \hline 
\text{Correct} & \text{Incorrect} & \text{Correct} & \text{Incorrect} & \text{Correct} \\ \hline 
\end{array}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is A, C & E Only.

text: The correct order of reactivity of the following benzyl halides towards reaction with KCN is :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Identify the Reaction Mechanism}}$

$\bullet$ The reaction of benzyl halides with $\mathrm{KCN}$ is a nucleophilic substitution reaction.

$\bullet$ Benzyl halides typically undergo $\mathrm{S_N2}$ reactions, where reactivity is enhanced by electron-donating groups (EDG) that stabilize the developing positive charge in the transition state or $\mathrm{S_N1}$ reactions where EDGs stabilize the carbocation intermediate.



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze the Effect of Substituents}}$

$$\begin{array}{|c|l|l|l|} \hline \text{Compound} & \text{Substituent} & \text{Effect} & \text{Reactivity} \\ \hline \text{a} & \text{para-} \mathrm{OH} & \text{Strong EDG (}+R > -I\text{)} & \text{High} \\ \hline \text{b} & \text{para-} \mathrm{NH_2} & \text{Very Strong EDG (}+R > -I\text{)} & \text{Highest} \\ \hline \text{c} & \text{para-} \mathrm{NO_2} & \text{Strong EWG (}-R, -I\text{)} & \text{Lowest} \\ \hline \text{d} & \text{meta-} \mathrm{NO_2} & \text{EWG (}-I \text{ only at meta)} & \text{Low} \\ \hline \end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Establish the Reactivity Order}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Electron-donating groups}}$ ($\mathrm{NH_2 > OH}$) increase reactivity by increasing electron density. Thus, $\mathbf{b > a}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Electron-withdrawing groups}}$ ($\mathrm{NO_2}$) decrease reactivity.

$\bullet$ The $\mathrm{NO_2}$ group at the $\pmb{\text{para position}}$ (c) exerts both strong $-R$ and $-I$ effects, making the carbon highly electron-deficient and least reactive.

$\bullet$ The $\mathrm{NO_2}$ group at the $\pmb{\text{meta position}}$ (d) exerts only an $-I$ effect, which is less deactivating than the combined effects at the para position. Thus, $\mathbf{d > c}$.

$\bullet$ Combining these: $\pmb{\text{b > a > d > c}}$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{B}}$.

text: Consider the following reactions giving major product. Identify the correct reaction.

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Analysis of Reactions:}}$

$$\begin{array}{|c|l|l|} \hline \text{Ref} & \text{Reaction Type} & \text{Evaluation} \\ \hline \text{A} & \text{Nitration} & \text{Incorrect. Nitration occurs on the activated aniline ring, not the benzoyl ring.} \\ \hline \text{B} & \text{Reduction} & \text{Incorrect. Reduction of isocyanides ($R-NC$) yields secondary amines, not primary.} \\ \hline \text{C} & \text{Gabriel Synthesis} & \text{Incorrect. Aryl halides do not undergo $S_N2$ with phthalimide anion.} \\ \hline \text{D} & \text{Hoffmann Bromamide} & \text{Correct. Propanamide reacts with $Br_2/KOH$ to yield ethylamine.} \\ \hline \end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Detailed Step:}}$

$\bullet$ $\textbf{Reaction D}$ is the $\pmb{\text{Hoffmann Bromamide Degradation}}$.

$\bullet$ The balanced chemical equation is: 
$$CH_3CH_2CONH_2 + Br_2 + 4KOH \xrightarrow{\Delta} CH_3CH_2NH_2 + K_2CO_3 + 2KBr + 2H_2O$$.

$\bullet$ This reaction effectively shortens the carbon chain by one carbon atom, converting an amide to a primary amine.

Therefore, the correct reaction is $\boxed{\text{D}}$.

text:
Given below are two statements :

$\pmb{\text{Statement I :}}$ ' $\text{C}-\text{Cl}$ ' bond is stronger in $\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}-\text{Cl}$ than $\text{CH}_3-\text{CH}_2-\text{Cl}$

$\pmb{\text{Statement II :}}$ The given optically active molecule, on hydrolysis gives a solution that can rotate the plane polarized light.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below

options.text: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze Statement I (Bond Strength)}}$

$\bullet$ In vinyl chloride ($\text{CH}_2=\text{CH}-\text{Cl}$), the lone pair of electrons on the chlorine atom is in conjugation with the $\pi$-bond. This resonance gives the $\text{C}-\text{Cl}$ bond a $\pmb{\text{partial double bond character}}$.
[Image of resonance in vinyl chloride]
$\bullet$ Additionally, the carbon in vinyl chloride is $\text{sp}^2$ hybridized, which is more electronegative and forms shorter, stronger bonds than the $\text{sp}^3$ hybridized carbon in ethyl chloride ($\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}$).
$\bullet$ A partial double bond is stronger than a pure single bond. $\pmb{\text{Statement I is true.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze Statement II (Optical Activity and Hydrolysis)}}$

$\bullet$ The problem refers to a standard $S_N1$ hydrolysis of a chiral alkyl halide (typically a secondary or tertiary halide). 
$\bullet$ During $S_N1$ hydrolysis, a planar $\pmb{\text{carbocation intermediate}}$ is formed. The nucleophile (water) can attack from either side with equal probability.
[Image of SN1 reaction mechanism showing racemization]
$\bullet$ This results in $\pmb{\text{racemization}}$, forming a racemic mixture ($50\%$ $d$-isomer and $50\%$ $l$-isomer). 
$\bullet$ A racemic mixture is optically inactive due to external compensation and does $\pmb{\text{not}}$ rotate plane-polarized light. $\pmb{\text{Statement II is false.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

$$\begin{array}{|l|c|} 
\hline 
\text{Statement} & \text{Validity} \\ \hline 
\text{Statement I} & \text{True} \\ 
\text{Statement II} & \text{False} \\ \hline 
\end{array}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{Statement I is true but Statement II is false}}$.

text:
Match List-I with List-II:

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} 
\hline 
\textbf{List-I (Chloro derivative)} & \textbf{List-II (Example)} \\ 
\hline 
A. \text{ Vinyl Chloride} & \text{I. } \text{CH}_2 = \text{CH} - \text{CH}_2\text{Cl} \\ 
\hline 
B. \text{ Benzyl Chloride} & \text{II. } \text{CH}_3 - \text{CH(Cl)CH}_3 \\ 
\hline 
C. \text{ Alkyl Chloride} & \text{III. } \text{CH}_2 = \text{CHCl} \\ 
\hline 
D. \text{ Allyl Chloride} & \text{IV. } \begin{gathered}\text{(1-chloroethyl)benzene} \end{gathered} \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Understand the classification of organic halides}}$

Organic halides are classified based on the hybridization of the carbon atom to which the halogen is attached and its position relative to a double bond or aromatic ring.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Vinyl Halide:}}$ The halogen is attached to an $sp^2$-hybridized carbon of a $\text{C=C}$ double bond.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Benzyl Halide:}}$ The halogen is attached to an $sp^3$-hybridized carbon that is directly bonded to an aromatic ring.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Alkyl Halide:}}$ The halogen is attached to a simple saturated $sp^3$-hybridized alkyl carbon.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Allyl Halide:}}$ The halogen is attached to an $sp^3$-hybridized carbon adjacent to a $\text{C=C}$ double bond.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Match List-I with List-II}}$

$\begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \textbf{List-I Item} & \textbf{Corresponding Match in List-II} \\ \hline \text{A. Vinyl Chloride} & \text{III. } \text{CH}_2 = \text{CHCl} \\ \hline \text{B. Benzyl Chloride} & \text{IV. } \text{Image of (1-chloroethyl)benzene} \\ \hline \text{C. Alkyl Chloride} & \text{II. } \text{CH}_3 - \text{CH(Cl)CH}_3 \\ \hline \text{D.
