Allyl Chloride} & \text{I. } \text{CH}_2 = \text{CH} - \text{CH}_2\text{Cl} \\ \hline \end{array}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Justification for the matches}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{A } \to \text{ III:}}$ $\text{CH}_2 = \text{CHCl}$ has chlorine on the vinylic carbon.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{B } \to \text{ IV:}}$ The image shows a chloroethyl group attached to a phenyl ring; the carbon holding the $\text{Cl}$ is at the benzylic position.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{C } \to \text{ II:}}$ Isopropyl chloride is a standard alkyl halide with an $sp^3$ carbon chain.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{D } \to \text{ I:}}$ Chlorine is at the allylic position (one carbon away from the double bond).

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

The correct matching sequence is $\text{A-III, B-IV, C-II, D-I}$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{C}}$.

text:
Consider the following compounds:

Arrange these compounds in the increasing order of reactivity with nitrating mixture.

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Understand Electrophilic Aromatic Substitution (Nitration)}}$ 

$\bullet$ Nitration is an electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction where the reactivity of the benzene ring depends on the nature of the substituent attached to it. 

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Activating groups}}$ (electron-donating) increase the electron density of the ring, making it more reactive towards electrophiles. 

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Deactivating groups}}$ (electron-withdrawing) decrease the electron density, making the ring less reactive. 

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze the Substituents in Compounds a, b, and c}}$ 

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|} \hline \text{Compound} & \text{Substituent} & \text{Electronic Effect} & \text{Effect on Reactivity} \\ \hline \text{a. Chlorobenzene} & -\text{Cl} & -I > +R \text{ (Deactivating)} & \text{Decreases reactivity} \\ \hline \text{b. Nitrobenzene} & -\text{NO}_2 & -I, -R \text{ (Strongly Deactivating)} & \text{Greatly decreases reactivity} \\ \hline \text{c. Anisole} & -\text{OCH}_3 & +R > -I \text{ (Strongly Activating)} & \text{Increases reactivity} \\ \hline \end{array}$$ 

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Establish the Increasing Order of Reactivity}}$ 

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Nitrobenzene (b)}}$ is the least reactive because the nitro group is one of the strongest deactivating groups. 

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Chlorobenzene (a)}}$ is more reactive than nitrobenzene but less reactive than benzene itself because halogens are weakly deactivating. 

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Anisole (c)}}$ is the most reactive because the methoxy group is a strong activating group due to its significant resonance effect. 

$\bullet$ Therefore, the increasing order is: $\pmb{\text{b < a < c}}$. 

Conclusion: The correct option is $\boxed{\text{D}}$.

text:
As compared with chlorocyclohexane, which of the following statements correctly apply to chlorobenzene?

$\pmb{\text{A.}}$ The magnitude of negative charge is more on chlorine atom.
$\pmb{\text{B.}}$ The $\text{C}-\text{Cl}$ bond has partial double bond character.
$\pmb{\text{C.}}$ $\text{C}-\text{Cl}$ bond is less polar.
$\pmb{\text{D.}}$ $\text{C}-\text{Cl}$ bond is longer due to repulsion between delocalised electrons of the aromatic ring and lone pairs of electrons of chlorine.
$\pmb{\text{E.}}$ The $\text{C}-\text{Cl}$ bond is formed using $\text{sp}^2$ hybridised orbital of carbon.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze Resonance and Bond Character (Statement B)}}$

In chlorobenzene, the lone pairs of electrons on the chlorine atom are in conjugation with the $\pi$-electrons of the benzene ring. This resonance effect results in a $\pmb{\text{partial double bond character}}$ for the $\text{C}-\text{Cl}$ bond. In contrast, chlorocyclohexane has no such resonance, maintaining a pure single bond.

$\pmb{\text{Statement B is correct.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Compare Bond Polarity (Statements A and C)}}$

$\bullet$ In chlorobenzene, the carbon attached to Cl is $\text{sp}^2$ hybridized, which is more electronegative than the $\text{sp}^3$ carbon in chlorocyclohexane.
$\bullet$ The resonance effect also shifts electron density from Cl toward the ring. 
$\bullet$ Consequently, the $\text{C}-\text{Cl}$ bond in chlorobenzene is $\pmb{\text{less polar}}$ (lower dipole moment) and the magnitude of negative charge on Cl is $\pmb{\text{less}}$ compared to chlorocyclohexane.
$\pmb{\text{Statement C is correct; Statement A is incorrect.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Evaluate Bond Length (Statement D)}}$

Because of the partial double bond character (resonance) and the greater s-character of the $\text{sp}^2$ hybridized carbon, the $\text{C}-\text{Cl}$ bond in chlorobenzene is $\pmb{\text{shorter}}$ (approx. 169 pm) than in chlorocyclohexane (approx. 177 pm).
$\pmb{\text{Statement D is incorrect.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Analyze Hybridization (Statement E)}}$

$$\begin{array}{|l|c|c|} 
\hline 
\text{Feature} & \text{Chlorobenzene} & \text{Chlorocyclohexane} \\ \hline 
\text{Hybridization of C} & \text{sp}^2 & \text{sp}^3 \\ 
\text{C-Cl Bond Nature} & \text{Partial Double} & \text{Pure Single} \\ \hline 
\end{array}$$
$\pmb{\text{Statement E is correct.}}$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

Statements B, C, and E are the correct applications to chlorobenzene.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{B, C and E Only}}$.

text:
The correct order of the rate of reaction of the following reactants with nucleophile by $\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 1$ mechanism is :
(Given : Structures I and II are rigid)

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Understand the } \mathrm{S_{N}1} \text{ Mechanism}}$

$\bullet$ The rate of an $\mathrm{S_{N}1}$ reaction depends on the stability of the carbocation intermediate formed in the rate-determining step.

$\bullet$ A carbocation requires a planar ($sp^2$) geometry to be stable.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Evaluate Carbocation Stability for each Structure}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Structure IV}}$: Leads to a $\pmb{\text{triphenylmethyl carbocation}}$. The positive charge is highly stabilized by resonance with three phenyl rings. This is the $\pmb{\text{most stable}}$ intermediate.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Structure III}}$: Leads to a $\pmb{\text{tert-butyl carbocation}}$. It is stabilized by inductive effect and hyperconjugation from nine $\alpha$-hydrogens. It is stable but less so than IV.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Structures I and II}}$: These are bridgehead halides. According to $\pmb{\text{Bredt's Rule}}$, a double bond (or $sp^2$ planar center) cannot be formed at the bridgehead of a small bridged ring system because it would introduce extreme angle strain.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Structure II}}$: The ring system is smaller and more rigid than I, making it $\pmb{\text{nearly impossible}}$ to achieve the planarity required for a carbocation. This is the $\pmb{\text{least reactive}}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Structure I}}$: While still a bridgehead and highly unreactive, the larger ring system provides slightly more flexibility compared to II, allowing for a marginally faster reaction.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Conclusion}}$

$\bullet$ The order of reactivity based on carbocation stability and geometric constraints is: $\pmb{\text{II < I < III < IV}}$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{B}}$.

text:
The correct order of reactivity of $\text{CH}_3\text{Br}$ in methanol with the following nucleophiles is:

$\text{F}^{-}, \text{I}^{-}, \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{O}^{-} \text{ and } \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}^{-}$

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Understand the } \mathrm{S_{N}1} \text{ Mechanism}}$

$\bullet$ The rate of an $\mathrm{S_{N}1}$ reaction depends on the stability of the carbocation intermediate formed in the rate-determining step.

$\bullet$ A carbocation requires a planar ($sp^2$) geometry to be stable.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Evaluate Carbocation Stability for each Structure}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Structure IV}}$: Leads to a $\pmb{\text{triphenylmethyl carbocation}}$. The positive charge is highly stabilized by resonance with three phenyl rings. This is the $\pmb{\text{most stable}}$ intermediate.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Structure III}}$: Leads to a $\pmb{\text{tert-butyl carbocation}}$. It is stabilized by inductive effect and hyperconjugation from nine $\alpha$-hydrogens. It is stable but less so than IV.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Structures I and II}}$: These are bridgehead halides. According to $\pmb{\text{Bredt's Rule}}$, a double bond (or $sp^2$ planar center) cannot be formed at the bridgehead of a small bridged ring system because it would introduce extreme angle strain.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Structure II}}$: The ring system is smaller and more rigid than I, making it $\pmb{\text{nearly impossible}}$ to achieve the planarity required for a carbocation. This is the $\pmb{\text{least reactive}}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Structure I}}$: While still a bridgehead and highly unreactive, the larger ring system provides slightly more flexibility compared to II, allowing for a marginally faster reaction.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Conclusion}}$

$\bullet$ The order of reactivity based on carbocation stability and geometric constraints is: $\pmb{\text{II < I < III < IV}}$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{B}}$.

text:
The correct order of the rate of the reaction for the following reaction with respect to nucleophiles is :

$$\text{CH}_3\text{Br} + \text{Nu}^- \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{Nu} + \text{Br}^-$$

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Analysis of Nucleophilicity:}}$

The given reaction is a nucleophilic substitution reaction of the $\text{S}_\text{N}2$ type. The rate of the reaction is directly proportional to the nucleophilicity of the incoming nucleophile $(\text{Nu}^-)$. In general, for nucleophiles with the same attacking atom (oxygen, in this case), nucleophilicity correlates with basicity, provided steric hindrance is not a dominant factor.



$\pmb{\text{Step-by-step Comparison:}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{OH}^- \text{ (Hydroxide):}}$ This is a strong base and a very strong nucleophile because the negative charge is localized on a small oxygen atom.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{PhO}^- \text{ (Phenoxide):}}$ The negative charge on oxygen is delocalized into the benzene ring through resonance. This makes it a weaker base and a weaker nucleophile than $\text{OH}^-$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{CH}_3\text{COO}^- \text{ (Acetate):}}$ The negative charge is delocalized over two electronegative oxygen atoms via resonance. This significantly reduces the electron density on the attacking oxygen, making it a weaker nucleophile than phenoxide.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{ClO}_4^- \text{ (Perchlorate):}}$ The negative charge is delocalized over four oxygen atoms. Furthermore, chlorine is in its highest oxidation state $(+7)$. It is an extremely stable ion and a very poor nucleophile.



$\pmb{\text{Summary Table:}}$

$\begin{array}{|l|l|c|} \hline \textbf{Nucleophile} & \textbf{Charge Distribution} & \textbf{Nucleophilicity} \\ \hline \text{OH}^- & \text{Localized} & \text{Highest} \\ \hline \text{PhO}^- & \text{Delocalized (Resonance with ring)} & \text{High} \\ \hline \text{CH}_3\text{COO}^- & \text{Delocalized (Two oxygens)} & \text{Low} \\ \hline \text{ClO}_4^- & \text{Highly Delocalized (Four oxygens)} & \text{Lowest} \\ \hline \end{array}$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

The correct order of reactivity (nucleophilicity) is:
$$\text{OH}^- > \text{PhO}^- > \text{CH}_3\text{COO}^- > \text{ClO}_4^-$$

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{B}}$.

text:
$ \text{Arrange the following alkenes in decreasing order of stability:} $

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Stability Criteria for Alkenes}}$

$\bullet$ Alkene stability is primarily governed by the degree of substitution on the double bond. 

$\bullet$ More substituted alkenes are more stable due to greater $\pmb{\text{hyperconjugation}}$ and stronger $\sigma$-bond formation between $sp^2$ and $sp^3$ carbons.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Compare Substitution Levels}}$

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|} \hline \text{Structure} & \text{Substitution Type} & \text{Stability Rank} \\ \hline \text{I} & \text{Tetrasubstituted} & \text{Highest (1st)} \\ \hline \text{III} & \text{Trisubstituted} & \text{High (2nd)} \\ \hline \text{II} & \text{Trans-disubstituted} & \text{Moderate (3rd)} \\ \hline \text{IV} & \text{Cis-disubstituted} & \text{Lowest (4th)} \\ \hline \end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Analyze Isomeric Stability (Cis vs. Trans)}}$

$\bullet$ For disubstituted alkenes, the $\pmb{\text{trans}}$ isomer (II) is more stable than the $\pmb{\text{cis}}$ isomer (IV). 

$\bullet$ This is because the cis isomer suffers from $\pmb{\text{steric strain}}$ caused by the proximity of bulky alkyl groups on the same side of the double bond.

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Conclusion}}$

$\bullet$ Combining the effects of substitution and steric factors, the decreasing order is: $\pmb{\text{I > III > II > IV}}$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{C}}$.

text:
The cycloalkene $(X)$ on bromination consumes one mole of bromine per mole of $(X)$ and gives the product $(Y)$ in which $C: Br$ ratio is 3:1. The percentage of bromine in the product $(Y)$ is $\_\_\_\_$ %. (Nearest integer)
(Given : molar mass in $\text{g mol}^{-1} \text{ H}: 1, \text{ C}: 12, \text{ O}: 16, \text{ Br}: 80$)

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Determine the molecular formula of product (Y)}}$

A cycloalkene with one double bond has the general formula $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n-2}$. 
When it reacts with one mole of $\text{Br}_2$, it undergoes an addition reaction to form a dibromoalkane $(Y)$:
$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n-2} + \text{Br}_2 \rightarrow \text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n-2}\text{Br}_2$$

Given the ratio of Carbon atoms to Bromine atoms in $(Y)$ is $3:1$:
$$\frac{n}{2} = \frac{3}{1} \implies n = 6$$

So, the molecular formula of $(Y)$ is $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{10}\text{Br}_2$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate the molar mass of (Y)}}$

$$\text{Molar mass} = (6 \times 12) + (10 \times 1) + (2 \times 80)$$
$$\text{Molar mass} = 72 + 10 + 160 = 242 \text{ g mol}^{-1}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate the percentage of Bromine}}$

$$\% \text{ Br} = \left( \frac{\text{Mass of Br}}{\text{Total molar mass}} \right) \times 100$$
$$\% \text{ Br} = \left( \frac{160}{242} \right) \times 100 \approx 66.11\%$$

Rounding to the nearest integer, we get $\boxed{66}$.

text:
But-2-yne and hydrogen (one mole each) are separately treated with (i) $\mathrm{Pd} / \mathrm{C}$ and (ii) $\mathrm{Na} /$ liq. $\mathrm{NH}_3$ to give the products X and Y respectively.
Identify the incorrect statements.
A. X and Y are stereoisomers.
B. Dipole moment of $X$ is zero.
C. Boiling point of X is higher than Y .
D. X and Y react with $\mathrm{O}_3 / \mathrm{Zn}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ to give different products.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Identify Product X and Y}}$

When But-2-yne ($\text{CH}_3\text{C}\equiv\text{CCH}_3$) undergoes partial hydrogenation:
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{(i) Pd/C (Lindlar-like catalyst):}}$ Performs $\pmb{\text{syn-addition}}$ to give $\pmb{\text{cis-but-2-ene (Product X)}}$.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{(ii) Na/liq. NH}_3\text{ (Birch Reduction):}}$ Performs $\pmb{\text{anti-addition}}$ to give $\pmb{\text{trans-but-2-ene (Product Y)}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Evaluate the Statements}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement A:}}$ X (cis) and Y (trans) are geometrical isomers, which are a type of $\pmb{\text{stereoisomers}}$. This statement is $\pmb{\text{correct}}$.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement B:}}$ $\text{X (cis-but-2-ene)}$ has a net $\pmb{\text{dipole moment } (\mu \neq 0)}$ because the bond moments do not cancel out. $\text{Y (trans-but-2-ene)}$ has $\mu = 0$. This statement is $\pmb{\text{incorrect}}$.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement C:}}$ $\text{Cis-isomers}$ generally have $\pmb{\text{higher boiling points}}$ than trans-isomers due to higher dipole-dipole interactions. This statement is $\pmb{\text{correct}}$.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement D:}}$ Both X and Y undergo reductive ozonolysis ($\text{O}_3 / \text{Zn}+\text{H}_2\text{O}$) to produce the $\pmb{\text{same product: ethanal (CH}_3\text{CHO)}}$. They do not give different products. This statement is $\pmb{\text{incorrect}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Conclusion}}$

The incorrect statements are $\pmb{\text{B}}$ and $\pmb{\text{D}}$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{B and D Only}}$.

text: $ \text { The final product }[\mathrm{B}] \text { is : } $

Consider the following reaction :
The product Y formed is :

The dibromo compound $[\mathrm{P}]$ (molecular formula: $\mathrm{C}_9 \mathrm{H}_{10} \mathrm{Br}_2$ ) when heated with excess sodamide followed by treatment with dilute HCl gives [Q]. On warming [Q] with mercuric sulphate and dilute sulphuric acid yield $[R]$ which gives positive Iodoform test but negative Tollen's test. The compound $[\mathrm{P}]$ is :

Given below are two statements :
Statement I : Benzene is nitrated to give nitrobenzene, which on further treatment with $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COCl} / \mathrm{AlCl}_3$ will give m-nitroacetophenone.
Statement II : $-\mathrm{NO}_2$ group is a $m$-directing, and deactivating group.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below

options.text: Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Evaluate Statement II}}$

$\bullet$ The nitro group ($-\text{NO}_2$) is a powerful electron-withdrawing group due to both resonance ($-R$) and inductive ($-I$) effects.

$\bullet$ By withdrawing electron density from the ring, it makes the ring less reactive toward electrophiles, thus acting as a $\pmb{\text{deactivating group}}$.

$\bullet$ It specifically withdraws density from the ortho and para positions, leaving the meta position relatively more electron-rich, hence it is a $\pmb{\text{meta-directing group}}$.

$\bullet$ Therefore, $\pmb{\text{Statement II is correct}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Evaluate Statement I}}$

$\bullet$ Benzene is indeed nitrated with a mixture of concentrated $\text{HNO}_3$ and $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ to yield nitrobenzene.

$\bullet$ However, $\pmb{\text{Friedel-Crafts acylation}}$ (using $\text{CH}_3\text{COCl} / \text{AlCl}_3$) $\pmb{\text{does not occur}}$ on rings that are strongly deactivated.

$\bullet$ Nitrobenzene is so deactivated that it is often used as a solvent for Friedel-Crafts reactions because it will not react with the electrophile itself.

$\bullet$ Therefore, nitrobenzene cannot be converted to m-nitroacetophenone via this direct method, making $\pmb{\text{Statement I incorrect}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Conclusion}}$

$\bullet$ Since Statement I is incorrect and Statement II is correct, the most appropriate choice is Option A.

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \text{Statement} & \text{Validity} \\ \hline \text{Statement I} & \text{Incorrect (F.C. Acylation fails on nitrobenzene)} \\ \hline \text{Statement II} & \text{Correct (-NO}_2\text{ is meta-directing and deactivating)} \\ \hline \end{array}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{A}}$.

text: 80 mL of a hydrocarbon on mixing with 264 mL of oxygen in a closed U-tube undergoes complete combustion. The residual gases after cooling to 273 K occupy 224 mL . When the system is treated with KOH solution, the volume decreases to 64 mL . The formula of the hydrocarbon is :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Write the general combustion equation}}$

For a hydrocarbon $\text{C}_x\text{H}_y$:
$$\text{C}_x\text{H}_y(g) + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2(g) \rightarrow x\text{CO}_2(g) + \frac{y}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O}(l)$$
At 273 K, water is in the liquid state and its volume is negligible.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Determine the volumes of gases from the data}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Volume of CO}_2 \text{ produced:}}$ The decrease in volume after adding KOH is due to the absorption of $\text{CO}_2$.
    $$\text{Vol. of CO}_2 = 224\text{ mL} - 64\text{ mL} = 160\text{ mL}$$
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Volume of unreacted O}_2\text{:}}$ The remaining 64 mL of gas is the excess oxygen.
    $$\text{Vol. of O}_2 \text{ consumed} = 264\text{ mL} - 64\text{ mL} = 200\text{ mL}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Solve for } x \text{ and } y}$

From the stoichiometry of the reaction:
1.  $\text{Volume of CO}_2 = x \times \text{Volume of hydrocarbon}$
    $$160 = x \times 80 \implies x = 2$$
2.  $\text{Volume of O}_2 \text{ consumed} = \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) \times \text{Volume of hydrocarbon}$
    $$200 = \left(2 + \frac{y}{4}\right) \times 80$$
    $$2.5 = 2 + \frac{y}{4} \implies \frac{y}{4} = 0.5 \implies y = 2$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Identify the hydrocarbon}}$

With $x=2$ and $y=2$, the formula is $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_2}$.

text: Consider the following reaction of benzene to form the compound shown in the image. In compound $(Q)$, the percentage of oxygen is $\_\_\_\_$ %. (Nearest integer)

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Determine the Molecular Formula of the Compound}}$

$\bullet$ The compound consists of a benzene ring connected to a cyclopentenone ring.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Benzene ring part:}}$ $C_6H_5$ (as one hydrogen is replaced by the attachment).

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Cyclopentenone ring part:}}$ It is a five-membered ring with one double bond and one ketone group. Counting the atoms:
    $\bullet$ Carbon atoms: 5 (in the ring).
    $\bullet$ Oxygen atom: 1 (the carbonyl oxygen).
    $\bullet$ Hydrogen atoms: The attachment point has 0 H, the double bond carbons have 1 H each (but one is the attachment point, so only 1 remains on the other C), the $CH_2$ group near the carbonyl has 2 H, and the other $CH_2$ group has 2 H.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Total atom count:}}$
    $\bullet$ Carbon (C): $6 \text{ (benzene)} + 5 \text{ (cyclopentenone)} = 11$.
    $\bullet$ Hydrogen (H): $5 \text{ (benzene)} + 5 \text{ (cyclopentenone ring: } 1+2+2) = 10$.
    $\bullet$ Oxygen (O): 1.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Molecular Formula:}}$ $C_{11}H_{10}O$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate the Molecular Mass}}$

$\bullet$ $Mass = (11 \times 12) + (10 \times 1) + (1 \times 16)$
$\bullet$ $Mass = 132 + 10 + 16 = 158 \text{ u}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate the Percentage of Oxygen}}$

$\bullet$ $\text{Percentage of Oxygen} = \left( \frac{\text{Mass of Oxygen}}{\text{Total Molecular Mass}} \right) \times 100$
$\bullet$ $\text{Percentage of Oxygen} = \left( \frac{16}{158} \right) \times 100 \approx 10.126\%$

$\bullet$ Rounding to the nearest integer, we get $10\%$.

Therefore, the percentage of oxygen in the compound is $\boxed{10}$%.

text:
Consider a weak base ' B ' of $\mathrm{pK}_{\mathrm{b}}=5.699$. ' $x$ ' mL of 0.02 M HCl and ' y ' mL of 0.02 M weak base ' B ' are mixed to make 100 mL of a buffer of pH 9 at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The values of ' $x$ ' and ' $y$ ' respectively are :
[Given : $\log 2=0.3010, \log 3=0.4771, \log 5=0.699$ ]

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Determine the condition for precipitation of YS}}$

For a salt to precipitate, its ionic product must exceed its solubility product ($K_{sp}$). For the metal sulphide $YS \rightleftharpoons Y^{2+} + S^{2-}$, the condition for precipitation is:
$$[Y^{2+}][S^{2-}] \geq K_{sp}(YS)$$
Given $[Y^{2+}] = 0.01 \text{ M}$ and $K_{sp}(YS) = 4 \times 10^{-16}$:
$$0.01 \times [S^{2-}] = 4 \times 10^{-16}$$
$$[S^{2-}] = \frac{4 \times 10^{-16}}{10^{-2}} = 4 \times 10^{-14} \text{ M}$$
This is the minimum concentration of sulphide ions required to start the precipitation of $YS$.



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Relate sulphide concentration to pH using } H_2S \text{ dissociation}}$

The overall dissociation of $H_2S$ is given by:
$$H_2S \rightleftharpoons 2H^+ + S^{2-}$$
The equilibrium constant for this process is $K = K_{a1} \times K_{a2}$:
$$K_{a1}K_{a2} = \frac{[H^+]^2 [S^{2-}]}{[H_2S]}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Solve for } [H^+]}$

Substitute the given values: $K_{a1}K_{a2} = 1.0 \times 10^{-21}$, $[H_2S] = 0.1 \text{ M}$, and the required $[S^{2-}] = 4 \times 10^{-14} \text{ M}$:
$$1.0 \times 10^{-21} = \frac{[H^+]^2 (4 \times 10^{-14})}{0.1}$$
$$[H^+]^2 = \frac{10^{-21} \times 10^{-1}}{4 \times 10^{-14}}$$
$$[H^+]^2 = \frac{10^{-22}}{4 \times 10^{-14}} = \frac{1}{4} \times 10^{-8}$$
$$[H^+] = \sqrt{0.25 \times 10^{-8}} = 0.5 \times 10^{-4} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ M}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Calculate pH}}$

$$\text{pH} = -\log [H^+] = -\log(5 \times 10^{-5})$$
$$\text{pH} = 5 - \log 5$$
Using the given $\log 5 = 0.70$:
$$\text{pH} = 5 - 0.70 = 4.3$$

The nearest integer is $4$.

Therefore, the numerical value is $\boxed{4}$.

text:
Which of the following mixture gives a buffer solution with $\text{pH} = 9.25$?
Given: $pK_b(\text{NH}_4\text{OH}) = 4.75$

options.text: $0.5 \text{ M NH}_4\text{OH} (0.2 \text{ L}) + 0.2 \text{ M HCl} (0.5 \text{ L})$
$0.2 \text{ M NH}_4\text{OH} (0.5 \text{ L}) + 0.1 \text{ M HCl} (0.5 \text{ L})$
$0.2 \text{ M NH}_4\text{OH} (0.4 \text{ L}) + 0.1 \text{ M HCl} (1 \text{ L})$
$0.4 \text{ M NH}_4\text{OH} (1 \text{ L}) + 0.1 \text{ M HCl} (1 \text{ L})$

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Determine the required pOH for the buffer}}$

Given $\text{pH} = 9.25$. At $25^\circ\text{C}$:
$$\text{pOH} = 14 - \text{pH} = 14 - 9.25 = 4.75$$
Since the given $pK_b$ of $\text{NH}_4\text{OH}$ is also $4.75$, we use the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation for a basic buffer:
$$\text{pOH} = pK_b + \log \left( \frac{[\text{Salt}]}{[\text{Base}]} \right)$$
$$4.75 = 4.75 + \log \left( \frac{[\text{Salt}]}{[\text{Base}]} \right) \implies \log \left( \frac{[\text{Salt}]}{[\text{Base}]} \right) = 0$$
This means for the desired pH, the concentration (or millimoles) of the Salt must equal the concentration (or millimoles) of the Remaining Base:
$$\pmb{[\text{NH}_4^+] = [\text{NH}_4\text{OH}]_{\text{left}}}$$



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Evaluate the options by calculating millimoles (mmol)}}$

When $\text{NH}_4\text{OH}$ reacts with $\text{HCl}$, the reaction is:
$$\text{NH}_4\text{OH} + \text{HCl} \rightarrow \text{NH}_4\text{Cl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|} 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{Option}} & \pmb{\text{mmol of Base (NH}_4\text{OH)}} & \pmb{\text{mmol of Acid (HCl)}} & \pmb{\text{Resulting Composition}} \\ 
\hline 
\text{A} & 0.5 \times 200 = 100 & 0.2 \times 500 = 100 & \text{Neutralization (Only Salt)} \\ 
\hline 
\text{B} & 0.2 \times 500 = 100 & 0.1 \times 500 = 50 & \pmb{\text{Salt}=50, \text{ Base Left}=50} \\ 
\hline 
\text{C} & 0.2 \times 400 = 80 & 0.1 \times 1000 = 100 & \text{Acidic solution (Excess HCl)} \\ 
\hline 
\text{D} & 0.4 \times 1000 = 400 & 0.1 \times 1000 = 100 & \text{Base}=300, \text{ Salt}=100 \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Analysis of Option B:}}$

Starting with $100\text{ mmol}$ of base and adding $50\text{ mmol}$ of acid:
$\bullet$ $50\text{ mmol}$ of $\text{HCl}$ reacts with $50\text{ mmol}$ of $\text{NH}_4\text{OH}$ to produce $50\text{ mmol}$ of salt ($\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}$).
$\bullet$ Remaining $\text{NH}_4\text{OH} = 100 - 50 = 50\text{ mmol}$.
Since $\text{mmol of salt} = \text{mmol of base}$, the $\text{pOH} = pK_b = 4.75$, and $\text{pH} = 9.25$.

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
Option B provides the correct stoichiometric ratio to form the required buffer.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{B}}$.

text:
Consider two Group IV metal ions $\mathrm{X}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Y}^{2+}$.
A solution containing $0.01 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{X}^{2+}$ and $0.01 \mathrm{M} \mathrm{Y}^{2+}$ is saturated with $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}$. The pH at which the metal sulphide YS will form as a precipitate is ____ . (Nearest integer)
(Given: $\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}(\mathrm{XS})=1 \times 10^{-22}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp}}(\mathrm{YS})=4 \times 10^{-16}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, $[\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}]=0.1 \mathrm{M}$ in solution, $\mathrm{K}_{a 1} \times \mathrm{K}_{a 2}(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S})=1.0 \times 10^{-21}, \log 2=0.30$, $\log 3=0.48, \log 5=0.70)$

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Determine the condition for precipitation of YS}}$

For a salt to precipitate, its ionic product must exceed its solubility product ($K_{sp}$). For the metal sulphide $YS \rightleftharpoons Y^{2+} + S^{2-}$, the condition for precipitation is:
$$[Y^{2+}][S^{2-}] \geq K_{sp}(YS)$$
Given $[Y^{2+}] = 0.01 \text{ M}$ and $K_{sp}(YS) = 4 \times 10^{-16}$:
$$0.01 \times [S^{2-}] = 4 \times 10^{-16}$$
$$[S^{2-}] = \frac{4 \times 10^{-16}}{10^{-2}} = 4 \times 10^{-14} \text{ M}$$
This is the minimum concentration of sulphide ions required to start the precipitation of $YS$.



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Relate sulphide concentration to pH using } H_2S \text{ dissociation}}$

The overall dissociation of $H_2S$ is given by:
$$H_2S \rightleftharpoons 2H^+ + S^{2-}$$
The equilibrium constant for this process is $K = K_{a1} \times K_{a2}$:
$$K_{a1}K_{a2} = \frac{[H^+]^2 [S^{2-}]}{[H_2S]}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Solve for } [H^+]}$

Substitute the given values: $K_{a1}K_{a2} = 1.0 \times 10^{-21}$, $[H_2S] = 0.1 \text{ M}$, and the required $[S^{2-}] = 4 \times 10^{-14} \text{ M}$:
$$1.0 \times 10^{-21} = \frac{[H^+]^2 (4 \times 10^{-14})}{0.1}$$
$$[H^+]^2 = \frac{10^{-21} \times 10^{-1}}{4 \times 10^{-14}}$$
$$[H^+]^2 = \frac{10^{-22}}{4 \times 10^{-14}} = \frac{1}{4} \times 10^{-8}$$
$$[H^+] = \sqrt{0.25 \times 10^{-8}} = 0.5 \times 10^{-4} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \text{ M}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Calculate pH}}$

$$\text{pH} = -\log [H^+] = -\log(5 \times 10^{-5})$$
$$\text{pH} = 5 - \log 5$$
Using the given $\log 5 = 0.70$:
$$\text{pH} = 5 - 0.70 = 4.3$$

The nearest integer is $4$.

Therefore, the numerical value is $\boxed{4}$.

text: The first and second ionization constants of $\text{H}_2\text{X}$ are $2.5 \times 10^{-8}$ and $1.0 \times 10^{-13}$ respectively. The concentration of $\text{X}^{2-}$ in $0.1\text{ M H}_2\text{X}$ solution is $\dots\dots \times 10^{-15}\text{ M}$. (Nearest Integer)

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Understand the Ionization of a Diprotic Acid}}$

A diprotic acid $\text{H}_2\text{X}$ dissociates in two sequential steps:
1. $\text{H}_2\text{X} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{HX}^- \quad (K_{a1} = 2.5 \times 10^{-8})$
2. $\text{HX}^- \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{X}^{2-} \quad (K_{a2} = 1.0 \times 10^{-13})$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate [H}^+\pmb{] and [HX}^-\pmb{] }}$ from the First Ionization

Since $K_{a1} \gg K_{a2}$, the concentration of $\text{H}^+$ is predominantly determined by the first dissociation step. Given the initial concentration $C = 0.1\text{ M}$ and a small $K_{a1}$, we use the approximation $[\text{H}_2\text{X}] \approx 0.1\text{ M}$:
$$K_{a1} = \frac{[\text{H}^+][\text{HX}^-]}{[\text{H}_2\text{X}]}$$
Let $[\text{H}^+] = [\text{HX}^-] = y$:
$$2.5 \times 10^{-8} = \frac{y^2}{0.1}$$
$$y^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-9} = 25 \times 10^{-10}$$
$$y = [\text{H}^+] = [\text{HX}^-] = 5 \times 10^{-5}\text{ M}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate [X}^{2-}\pmb{] from the Second Ionization}}$

For the second dissociation step:
$$K_{a2} = \frac{[\text{H}^+][\text{X}^{2-}]}{[\text{HX}^-]}$$
In a solution of a weak diprotic acid where no other substances are added, the second dissociation is so weak that the concentrations of $\text{H}^+$ and $\text{HX}^-$ are not significantly altered from the first step's values ($[\text{H}^+] \approx [\text{HX}^-]$).

Substituting these values into the $K_{a2}$ expression:
$$1.0 \times 10^{-13} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-5}) [\text{X}^{2-}]}{5 \times 10^{-5}}$$
$$[\text{X}^{2-}] = K_{a2} = 1.0 \times 10^{-13}\text{ M}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Convert to the required format}}$

The question asks for the concentration in terms of $10^{-15}\text{ M}$:
$$1.0 \times 10^{-13} = 100 \times 10^{-15}\text{ M}$$

Therefore, the numerical value is $\boxed{100}$.

text: A small block of mass $m$ slides down from the top of a frictionless inclined surface, while the inclined plane is moving towards left with constant acceleration $a_0$. The angle between the inclined plane and ground is $\theta$ and its base length is $L$. Assuming that initially the small block is at the top of the inclined plane, the time it takes to reach the lowest point of the inclined plane is ________.

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze forces in the non-inertial frame}}$
Working in the frame of the accelerating inclined plane, we must account for the pseudo-force acting on the block $m$. Since the plane accelerates left with $a_0$, the pseudo-force $ma_0$ acts on the block toward the right.
$\bullet$ Component of gravity down the incline: $mg \sin \theta$
$\bullet$ Component of pseudo-force up the incline: $ma_0 \cos \theta$



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Determine net acceleration down the incline}}$
The net force along the surface of the incline is $F_{net} = mg \sin \theta - ma_0 \cos \theta$. Dividing by mass $m$, the net acceleration $a$ is:
$$a = g \sin \theta - a_0 \cos \theta$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate the distance to travel}}$
The base length of the incline is $L$. Let the length of the inclined surface be $S$. From trigonometry:
$$\cos \theta = \frac{L}{S} \implies S = \frac{L}{\cos \theta}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Solve for time t}}$
Using the kinematic equation $S = ut + \frac{1}{2}at^2$ with $u=0$:
$$\frac{L}{\cos \theta} = \frac{1}{2} (g \sin \theta - a_0 \cos \theta) t^2$$
$$t^2 = \frac{2L}{\cos \theta (g \sin \theta - a_0 \cos \theta)} = \frac{2L}{g \sin \theta \cos \theta - a_0 \cos^2 \theta}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 5: Simplify using trigonometric identities}}$
To match the options, multiply the numerator and denominator by $2$:
$$t^2 = \frac{4L}{g (2 \sin \theta \cos \theta) - a_0 (2 \cos^2 \theta)}$$
Using $2 \sin \theta \cos \theta = \sin 2\theta$ and $2 \cos^2 \theta = 1 + \cos 2\theta$:
$$t = \sqrt{\frac{4L}{g \sin 2\theta - a_0 (1 + \cos 2\theta)}}$$

Therefore, the time taken is $\boxed{\sqrt{\dfrac{4L}{g\sin 2\theta - a_0 (1+\cos 2\theta)}}}$.

text:
A block of mass 5 kg is moving on an inclined plane which makes an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. Friction coefficient between the block and inclined plane surface is $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. The force to be applied on the block so that the block will move down without acceleration is $\_\_\_\_$ N.
$ \left(g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right) . $

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Calculate the forces acting on the block}}$
For the block to move without acceleration, the net force acting on it must be zero. We first resolve the weight of the block into its components:
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Component of weight along the incline:}}$ $mg \sin(30^{\circ}) = 5 \times 10 \times 0.5 = 25\text{ N}$ (acting downwards along the incline).
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Normal force (N):}}$ $N = mg \cos(30^{\circ}) = 50 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = 25\sqrt{3}\text{ N}$.



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Determine the frictional force}}$
The frictional force ($f$) opposing the downward motion is given by:
$\bullet$ $f = \mu N = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times 25\sqrt{3} = \frac{25 \times 3}{2} = 37.5\text{ N}$ (acting upwards along the incline).

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Apply the equilibrium condition}}$
To move down at a constant velocity (zero acceleration), the total downward force must balance the upward frictional force. Let $F$ be the additional force applied down the incline:
$\bullet$ $mg \sin \theta + F = f$
$\bullet$ $25 + F = 37.5$
$\bullet$ $F = 37.5 - 25 = 12.5\text{ N}$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$ The required force is $\boxed{12.5\text{ N}}$.

text: A particle of mass $m$ falls from rest through a resistive medium having resistive force, $F=-k v$, where $v$ is the velocity of the particle and $k$ is a constant. Which of the following graphs represents velocity $(v)$ versus time $(t)$ ?

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Set up the equation of motion}}$
As the particle falls, it experiences two forces: a downward gravitational force ($mg$) and an upward resistive force ($-kv$). According to Newton's Second Law:
$$m\frac{dv}{dt} = mg - kv$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze initial and terminal conditions}}$
$\bullet$ $t = 0$:The particle starts from rest, so $v = 0$. At this instant, the net force is maximum ($mg$), leading to maximum acceleration $a_0 = g$.
$\bullet$ As $v$ increases:The resistive force increases, which reduces the net force and thus the acceleration.
$\bullet$ Terminal Velocity: Eventually, the resistive force balances gravity, and acceleration becomes zero ($m\frac{dv}{dt} = 0$). The particle reaches a constant terminal velocity $v_t = \frac{mg}{k}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Determine the mathematical relationship}}$
Integrating the equation of motion gives the velocity as a function of time:
$$v(t) = \frac{mg}{k}(1 - e^{-kt/m})$$
This is an exponential growth function that starts at the origin $(0,0)$ and asymptotically approaches the terminal velocity.

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Identify the correct graph}}$

$\bullet$ The graph must start at $(0,0)$ with a steep initial slope ($g$).
$\bullet$ The slope must gradually decrease (flatten) as $v$ increases.
$\bullet$ The curve eventually becomes horizontal at $v = v_t$.

Therefore, the correct graph is the one that displays an increasing curve approaching a horizontal asymptote, which corresponds to $\boxed{\text{Option A}}$.

text: A flexible chain of mass $m$ hangs between two fixed points at the same level. The inclination of the chain with the horizontal at the two points of support is $30^{\circ}$. Considering the equilibrium of each half of the chain, the tension of the chain at the lowest point is $\_\_\_\_$ .

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze the equilibrium of half the chain}}$ 
Consider one half of the chain, from one support point to the lowest point. This section has a mass of $m/2$.



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Identify the forces acting on the half-chain}}$ 
Three primary forces act on this section for it to remain in equilibrium:
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Tension at the support (} T_1 \pmb{\text{):}}}$ Acts at an inclination of $30^{\circ}$ with the horizontal.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Tension at the lowest point (} T_0 \pmb{\text{):}}}$ Acts purely horizontally.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Gravitational force:}}$ The weight of this half-section is $\frac{m}{2}g$, acting vertically downwards.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Apply the equations of equilibrium}}$ 
For horizontal equilibrium, the horizontal component of the support tension must balance the horizontal tension at the lowest point:
$$T_0 = T_1 \cos(30^{\circ}) = T_1 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \quad \text{--- (i)}$$

For vertical equilibrium, the vertical component of the support tension must balance the weight of the half-section:
$$T_1 \sin(30^{\circ}) = \frac{m}{2}g$$
$$T_1 \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{m}{2}g \implies T_1 = mg \quad \text{--- (ii)}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Solve for the tension at the lowest point (} T_0 \pmb{\text{)}}}$ 
Substitute the value of $T_1$ from equation (ii) into equation (i):
$$T_0 = mg \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$
$$T_0 = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} mg$$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$ The tension of the chain at the lowest point is $\boxed{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} mg}$.

text:
A block is sliding down on an inclined plane of slope $\theta$ and at an instant $t=0$ this block is given an upward momentum so that it starts moving up on the inclined surface with velocity $u$. The distance ($S$) travelled by the block before its velocity become zero, is $\_\_\_\_$ .
(g = gravitational acceleration)

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Determine the acceleration of the block}}$
When the block is given an upward velocity $u$ on a frictionless inclined plane, the only force acting along the incline is the component of gravity acting downwards.
$\bullet$ The acceleration $a$ along the incline is given by $a = -g \sin \theta$.



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Apply the kinematic equation}}$
To find the distance $S$ traveled before the velocity becomes zero ($V = 0$), we use the third equation of motion:
$$V^2 = U^2 + 2as$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Solve for the distance S}}$
Substituting the known values ($V = 0$, $U = u$, and $a = -g \sin \theta$):
$$0 = u^2 + 2(-g \sin \theta) \cdot S$$
$$0 = u^2 - 2g \sin \theta \cdot S$$
$$2g \sin \theta \cdot S = u^2$$
$$S = \frac{u^2}{2g \sin \theta}$$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$ The distance traveled by the block before its velocity becomes zero is $\boxed{\frac{u^2}{2g \sin \theta}}$.

Therefore, the correct option is $\pmb{\text{C}}$.

text: A 4 kg mass moves under the influence of a force $\vec{F}=\left(4 t^3 \hat{i}-3 t \hat{j}\right) \mathrm{N}$ where $t$ is the time in second. If mass starts from origin at $t=0$, the velocity and position after $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$ will be:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Find acceleration (}\vec{a}\text{)}}$
Given force $\vec{F} = (4t^3 \hat{i} - 3t \hat{j}) \text{ N}$ and mass $m = 4 \text{ kg}$. Using Newton's second law $\vec{a} = \frac{\vec{F}}{m}$:
$$\vec{a} = \frac{4t^3 \hat{i} - 3t \hat{j}}{4} = t^3 \hat{i} - \frac{3t}{4} \hat{j} \text{ m/s}^2$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Determine velocity (}\vec{v}\text{) at } t=2\text{ s}}$
Integrating $\vec{a}$ with respect to time, with initial condition $\vec{v}(0) = 0$:
$$\vec{v} = \int \vec{a} dt = \frac{t^4}{4} \hat{i} - \frac{3t^2}{8} \hat{j}$$
At $t = 2 \text{ s}$:
$$\vec{v} = \frac{2^4}{4} \hat{i} - \frac{3(2^2)}{8} \hat{j} = 4 \hat{i} - \frac{3}{2} \hat{j} \text{ m/s}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Determine position (}\vec{r}\text{) at } t=2\text{ s}}$
Integrating $\vec{v}$ with respect to time, with initial condition $\vec{r}(0) = 0$:
$$\vec{r} = \int \vec{v} dt = \frac{t^5}{20} \hat{i} - \frac{t^3}{8} \hat{j}$$
At $t = 2 \text{ s}$:
$$\vec{r} = \frac{2^5}{20} \hat{i} - \frac{2^3}{8} \hat{j} = \frac{32}{20} \hat{i} - \frac{8}{8} \hat{j} = \frac{8}{5} \hat{i} - \hat{j} \text{ m}$$

Therefore, the velocity and position are $\boxed{\vec{v}=4 \hat{i}-\frac{3}{2} \hat{j} \text{ and } \vec{r}=\frac{8}{5} \hat{i}-\hat{j}}$.

text:
A long cylindrical conductor with large cross section carries an electric current distributed uniformly over its cross-section. Magnetic field due to this current is:
A. maximum at either ends of the conductor and minimum at the midpoint
B. maximum at the axis of the conductor
C. minimum at the surface of the conductor
D. minimum at the axis of the conductor
E. same at all points in the cross-section of the conductor
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Apply Ampere's Circuital Law}}$

$\bullet$ For a long cylindrical conductor of radius $R$ carrying a current $I$ uniformly distributed over its cross-section, the magnetic field $B$ at a distance $r$ from the axis is calculated using Ampere's Law.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Inside the conductor (r < R):}}$ The enclosed current $I_{encl}$ is proportional to the area $\pi r^2$. Ampere's Law gives:
$$B \cdot 2\pi r = \mu_0 I_{encl} = \mu_0 I \left( \frac{r^2}{R^2} \right)$$
$$B = \frac{\mu_0 I r}{2\pi R^2}$$
$\bullet$ This shows that inside the conductor, $\pmb{B \propto r}$.



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Determine Maxima and Minima}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{At the axis (r = 0):}}$ Substituting $r = 0$ into the internal field formula, we get $B = 0$. This is the $\pmb{\text{minimum}}$ value of the magnetic field.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{At the surface (r = R):}}$ The field is $B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi R}$. This is the $\pmb{\text{maximum}}$ value of the magnetic field.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Outside the conductor (r > R):}}$ The field decreases as $B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$, meaning $B \propto 1/r$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Evaluate the Options}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement B:}}$ Incorrect, as the field is zero (minimum) at the axis.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement C:}}$ Incorrect, as the field is maximum at the surface.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement D:}}$ Correct, the magnetic field is zero at the axis.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement E:}}$ Incorrect, the field varies linearly with $r$ inside the conductor.

Therefore, the correct option is $\boxed{\text{D}}$.

text: The magnetic field at the centre of a current carrying circular loop of radius $R$ is $16 \mu \mathrm{~T}$. The magnetic field at a distance $x=\sqrt{3} R$ on its axis from the centre is $\_\_\_\_$ $\mu \mathrm{T}$.

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Identify the Formulae for Magnetic Field}}$

$\bullet$ The magnetic field at the $\pmb{\text{centre}}$ of a circular loop of radius $R$ carrying current $I$ is: 
$$B_{centre} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2R}$$

$\bullet$ The magnetic field at a point on the $\pmb{\text{axis}}$ at a distance $x$ from the centre is given by: 
$$B_{axis} = \frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2(R^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Relate the Axial Field to the Central Field}}$

$\bullet$ We can write the axial field in terms of the field at the centre: 
$$B_{axis} = B_{centre} \times \frac{R^3}{(R^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Substitute the Given Values and Solve}}$

$\bullet$ Given: $B_{centre} = 16 \mu\text{T}$ and $x = \sqrt{3}R$.

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \text{Calculation Step} & \text{Expression} \\ \hline \text{Substitute } x & B_{axis} = 16 \times \frac{R^3}{(R^2 + (\sqrt{3}R)^2)^{3/2}} \\ \hline \text{Simplify denominator} & B_{axis} = 16 \times \frac{R^3}{(R^2 + 3R^2)^{3/2}} \\ \hline \text{Combine } R^2 \text{ terms} & B_{axis} = 16 \times \frac{R^3}{(4R^2)^{3/2}} \\ \hline \text{Evaluate power} & B_{axis} = 16 \times \frac{R^3}{8R^3} \\ \hline \text{Final result} & B_{axis} = 16 \times \frac{1}{8} = 2 \mu\text{T} \\ \hline \end{array}$$

$\bullet$ Note: $(4R^2)^{3/2} = (\sqrt{4R^2})^3 = (2R)^3 = 8R^3$.

Therefore, the magnetic field at the axial point is $\boxed{2} \mu\text{T}$.

text:
Three long straight wires carrying current are arranged mutually parallel as shown in the figure. The force experienced by 15 cm length of wire $Q$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
$$ \left(\mu_{\mathrm{o}}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~T} . \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{A}\right) $$

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Understand the Magnetic Force between Parallel Wires}}$

$\bullet$ The magnetic force per unit length between two parallel wires carrying currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ separated by a distance $r$ is given by: $f = \frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2\pi r}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Direction Rule:}}$ Parallel currents $\pmb{\text{attract}}$ each other, and anti-parallel currents $\pmb{\text{repel}}$ each other.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate the Force on Wire Q due to Wire P (F}_{QP}\text{)}}$

$\bullet$ Current in $P$ ($I_P$) = 3 A (Upwards), Current in $Q$ ($I_Q$) = 1 A (Downwards). Distance ($r_{PQ}$) = 3 cm = $0.03 \text{ m}$.

$\bullet$ Since currents are anti-parallel, wire $P$ $\pmb{\text{repels}}$ wire $Q$. The force $\vec{F}_{QP}$ acts towards the $\pmb{\text{right}}$ (towards $R$).

$\bullet$ Magnitude of force on length $l = 15 \text{ cm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$:
$$F_{QP} = \frac{\mu_0 I_P I_Q}{2\pi r_{PQ}} \times l = \frac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 3 \times 1 \times 0.15}{0.03} = 30 \times 10^{-7} = 3 \times 10^{-6} \text{ N}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate the Force on Wire Q due to Wire R (F}_{QR}\text{)}}$

$\bullet$ Current in $Q$ ($I_Q$) = 1 A (Downwards), Current in $R$ ($I_R$) = 2 A (Downwards). Distance ($r_{QR}$) = 2 cm = $0.02 \text{ m}$.

$\bullet$ Since currents are parallel, wire $R$ $\pmb{\text{attracts}}$ wire $Q$. The force $\vec{F}_{QR}$ acts towards the $\pmb{\text{right}}$ (towards $R$).

$\bullet$ Magnitude of force on length $l = 0.15 \text{ m}$:
$$F_{QR} = \frac{\mu_0 I_Q I_R}{2\pi r_{QR}} \times l = \frac{2 \times 10^{-7} \times 1 \times 2 \times 0.15}{0.02} = 30 \times 10^{-7} = 3 \times 10^{-6} \text{ N}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Calculate the Net Force}}$

$\bullet$ Both forces act in the same direction (towards $R$).

$\bullet$ $F_{net} = F_{QP} + F_{QR} = 3 \times 10^{-6} + 3 \times 10^{-6} = 6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ N}$.

$\bullet$ The direction is $\pmb{\text{towards R}}$.

Therefore, the force experienced by wire $Q$ is $\boxed{6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ N towards } R}$.

text: Two identical circular loops $P$ and $Q$ each of radius $r$ are lying in parallel planes such that they have common axis. The current through $P$ and $Q$ are $I$ and $4 I$ respectively in clockwise direction as seen from $O$. The net magnetic field at $O$ is :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Identify the formula for magnetic field on the axis of a circular loop}}$

$\bullet$ The magnetic field $B$ at a distance $x$ from the center of a circular loop of radius $r$ carrying current $I$ is given by: $B = \frac{\mu_0 I r^2}{2(r^2 + x^2)^{3/2}}$.

$\bullet$ In this problem, the point $O$ is at a distance $x = r$ from the center of both loops $P$ and $Q$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate the magnetic field due to loop P at O}}$

$\bullet$ For loop $P$, current is $I$ and distance is $r$.

$\bullet$ $B_P = \frac{\mu_0 I r^2}{2(r^2 + r^2)^{3/2}} = \frac{\mu_0 I r^2}{2(2r^2)^{3/2}} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \cdot 2\sqrt{2} r} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{4\sqrt{2}r}$.

$\bullet$ Since the current is clockwise as seen from $O$, the field $\vec{B}_P$ points $\pmb{\text{towards P}}$ (away from $O$).

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate the magnetic field due to loop Q at O}}$

$\bullet$ For loop $Q$, current is $4I$ and distance is $r$.

$\bullet$ $B_Q = \frac{\mu_0 (4I) r^2}{2(r^2 + r^2)^{3/2}} = \frac{4\mu_0 I}{4\sqrt{2}r} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{\sqrt{2}r}$.

$\bullet$ Since the current is clockwise as seen from $O$, the field $\vec{B}_Q$ points $\pmb{\text{towards Q}}$ (away from $O$).

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Determine the net magnetic field at O}}$

$\bullet$ The two fields are in opposite directions along the common axis.

$\bullet$ $B_{net} = B_Q - B_P = \frac{4\mu_0 I}{4\sqrt{2}r} - \frac{\mu_0 I}{4\sqrt{2}r} = \frac{3\mu_0 I}{4\sqrt{2}r}$.

$\bullet$ Since $B_Q > B_P$, the net field is in the direction of $\vec{B}_Q$, which is $\pmb{\text{towards Q}}$.

Therefore, the net magnetic field is $\boxed{\frac{3 \mu_{\mathrm{o}} I}{4 \sqrt{2} r} \text{ towards } Q}$.

text:
The current passing through a conducting loop in the form of equilateral triangle of side $4 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~cm}$ is 2 A . The magnetic field at its centroid is $\alpha \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~T}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$ .
(Given : $\mu_{\mathrm{o}}=4 \pi \times 10^{-7}$ SI units)

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Understand the Geometry of the Equilateral Triangle}}$

$\bullet$ Let the side of the equilateral triangle be $a = 4\sqrt{3} \text{ cm} = 4\sqrt{3} \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}$.

$\bullet$ The distance ($r$) from the centroid to the center of any side is given by:
$$r = \frac{a}{2\sqrt{3}}$$



$\bullet$ Substituting the value of $a$:
$$r = \frac{4\sqrt{3} \times 10^{-2}}{2\sqrt{3}} = 2 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate the Magnetic Field due to One Side}}$

$\bullet$ For a finite wire carrying current $I$, the magnetic field at a distance $r$ is:
$$B_{side} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi r} (\sin \theta_1 + \sin \theta_2)$$

$\bullet$ For an equilateral triangle, the angles subtended by the side at the centroid are $\theta_1 = \theta_2 = 60^{\circ}$.

$\bullet$ Substituting the values ($I = 2 \text{ A}$, $r = 2 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}$):
$$B_{side} = \frac{10^{-7} \times 2}{2 \times 10^{-2}} (\sin 60^{\circ} + \sin 60^{\circ})$$
$$B_{side} = 10^{-5} \left( \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \right) = \sqrt{3} \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate the Total Magnetic Field at the Centroid}}$

$\bullet$ Since there are three identical sides and the current flows in a way that all fields point in the same direction (into or out of the plane):
$$B_{total} = 3 \times B_{side}$$
$$B_{total} = 3 \times (\sqrt{3} \times 10^{-5}) = 3\sqrt{3} \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Identify the value of } \alpha}$

$\bullet$ Comparing $B_{total} = 3\sqrt{3} \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$ with the given form $\alpha \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$:
$$\alpha = 3\sqrt{3}$$

Therefore, the value of $\alpha$ is $\boxed{3\sqrt{3}}$.

text: An infinitely long straight wire carrying current $I$ is bent in a planar shape as shown in the diagram. The radius of the circular part is $r$. The magnetic field at the centre $O$ of the circular loop is :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze the Geometry and Coordinate System}}$

$\bullet$ The wire consists of two $\pmb{\text{semi-infinite straight sections}}$ ($AB$ and $DE$) and one $\pmb{\text{circular loop}}$ ($BCD$).

$\bullet$ From the provided diagram, the straight wires lie along the $x$-axis. The circular loop is in the $xz$-plane, and we are looking for the field at the origin $O$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate Field from Straight Segments}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Segment AB:}}$ Point $O$ lies on the line perpendicular to the end of this semi-infinite wire. The magnitude is $B_{AB} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi r}$. According to the right-hand rule, the field points in the $-\hat{k}$ direction (into the page relative to the $xy$ plane setup).

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Segment DE:}}$ This is also a semi-infinite wire. The field at $O$ is $B_{DE} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{4\pi r}$, also pointing in the $-\hat{k}$ direction.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Combined Straight Field:}}$ $\vec{B}_{straight} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r} (-\hat{k})$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate Field from the Circular Loop}}$

$\bullet$ For a full circular loop of radius $r$, the field at the center is $B_{loop} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2r}$.

$\bullet$ Based on the clockwise current direction shown, the field points into the page. To match the unit vectors in the options (where $\hat{i}$ is used to denote the direction perpendicular to the loop plane in this specific problem's context):
$$\vec{B}_{loop} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2r} (\text{into page})$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Resultant Magnetic Field Calculation}}$

$\bullet$ We sum the vectors, noting that the straight sections and the loop produce fields in opposite directions relative to the coordinate markers in the specific orientation of this problem:

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \text{Term} & \text{Expression} \\ \hline \text{Straight Wires} & \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r} \\ \hline \text{Circular Loop} & -\frac{\mu_0 I}{2r} \\ \hline \text{Net Field (B)} & \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r} - \frac{\mu_0 I}{2r} \\ \hline \end{array}$$

$\bullet$ Factoring out $-\frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}$:
$$\vec{B}_{net} = -\frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r} (\pi - 1) \hat{i}$$

Therefore, the correct option is $\boxed{\text{B}}$.

text: A current carrying solenoid is placed vertically and a particle of mass $m$ with charge $Q$ is released from rest. The particle moves along the axis of solenoid. If $g$ is acceleration due to gravity then the acceleration (a) of the charged particle will satisfy :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze the Magnetic Field inside the Solenoid}}$

$\bullet$ For an ideal current-carrying solenoid, the magnetic field $\vec{B}$ inside is uniform and directed $\pmb{\text{parallel to the axis}}$ of the solenoid.



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Determine the Magnetic Force on the Particle}}$

$\bullet$ The magnetic force $\vec{F}_m$ on a charge $Q$ moving with velocity $\vec{v}$ in a magnetic field $\vec{B}$ is given by the Lorentz force formula: $\vec{F}_m = Q(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$.

$\bullet$ The problem states the particle is released from rest and moves $\pmb{\text{along the axis}}$ of the solenoid. Therefore, the velocity vector $\vec{v}$ is parallel (or anti-parallel) to the magnetic field vector $\vec{B}$.

$\bullet$ Since the angle $\theta$ between $\vec{v}$ and $\vec{B}$ is $0^\circ$ or $180^\circ$, the magnitude of the magnetic force is: $F_m = QvB \sin(0^\circ) = 0$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Analyze the Net Force and Acceleration}}$

$\bullet$ The only unbalanced force acting on the particle is the force of gravity: $F_{net} = mg$.

$\bullet$ Applying Newton's Second Law ($F_{net} = ma$):
$$mg = ma \implies a = g$$

Therefore, the acceleration of the charged particle is $\boxed{g}$.

text:
A boy throws a ball into air at $45^{\circ}$ from the horizontal to land it on a roof of a building of height $H$. If the ball attains maximum height in 2 s and lands on the building in 3 s after launch, then value of $H$ is $\_\_\_\_$ m.
$ \left(\mathrm{g}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right) $

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Determine the vertical component of initial velocity}}$
For a projectile launched at an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with initial velocity $v_0$, the vertical component of the initial velocity is:
$$v_{0y} = v_0 \sin 45^{\circ} = \frac{v_0}{\sqrt{2}}$$



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Use the time to reach maximum height}}$
The maximum height occurs when the vertical velocity $v_y = 0$. Given this happens at $t = 2\text{ s}$:
$$0 = v_{0y} - gt$$
$$\implies v_{0y} = g \times t = 10 \times 2 = 20\text{ m/s}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate the height of the building H}}$
The height $H$ is the vertical displacement of the ball when it lands on the roof at $t = 3\text{ s}$. Using the second equation of motion for vertical displacement:
$$H = v_{0y}t - \frac{1}{2}gt^2$$
$$H = 20 \times 3 - \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times (3)^2$$
$$H = 60 - \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 9$$
$$H = 60 - 45 = 15\text{ m}$$

Therefore, the value of $H$ is $\boxed{15}\text{ m}$.

text: A projectile is thrown upward at an angle $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The speed of the projectile is $20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ when its direction of motion is $45^{\circ}$ with the horizontal. The initial speed of the projectile is ________ $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$.

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Understand the principle of horizontal velocity}}$
In projectile motion, in the absence of air resistance, the horizontal component of velocity remains constant throughout the flight.


$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Express the initial horizontal velocity}}$
Let the initial speed of the projectile be $u$. Given the initial angle $\theta_1 = 60^{\circ}$, the horizontal component is:
$$v_x = u \cos 60^{\circ} = u \times \frac{1}{2} = \frac{u}{2}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Express the horizontal velocity at the second point}}$
At the second point, the speed is $v = 20 \text{ m/s}$ and the angle is $\theta_2 = 45^{\circ}$. The horizontal component here is:
$$v_x = v \cos 45^{\circ} = 20 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{20}{\sqrt{2}}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Equate the horizontal components and solve for } u}$
Since horizontal velocity is constant:
$$\frac{u}{2} = \frac{20}{\sqrt{2}}$$
$$u = \frac{40}{\sqrt{2}}$$
$$u = 20\sqrt{2} \text{ m/s}$$

Therefore, the initial speed of the projectile is $\boxed{20\sqrt{2} \text{ m/s}}$.

text: A river of width 200 m is flowing from west to east with a speed of $18\text{ km/h}$. A boat, moving with speed of $36\text{ km/h}$ in still water, is made to travel one-round trip (bank to bank of the river). Minimum time taken by the boat for this journey and also the displacement along the river bank are ______ and ______ respectively.

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Convert units to m/s}}$
$\bullet$ Width of the river $d = 200\text{ m}$.
$\bullet$ Speed of river $v_r = 18\text{ km/h} = 18 \times \frac{5}{18} = 5\text{ m/s}$.
$\bullet$ Speed of boat in still water $v_b = 36\text{ km/h} = 36 \times \frac{5}{18} = 10\text{ m/s}$.



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Calculate minimum time to cross}}$
For minimum time to cross the river, the boat must head perpendicular to the river flow (at $90^{\circ}$ to the bank).
$\bullet$ Time taken for a single crossing $t = \frac{d}{v_b} = \frac{200}{10} = 20\text{ s}$.
$\bullet$ For a round trip (bank to bank and back), total minimum time $T = 2t = 2 \times 20 = 40\text{ s}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate displacement along the river bank (Drift)}}$
During the crossing, the river flow carries the boat downstream.
$\bullet$ Drift during the first crossing $x_1 = v_r \times t = 5 \times 20 = 100\text{ m}$.
$\bullet$ During the return trip, to minimize time, the boat again heads perpendicular to the flow relative to the water. Since the river continues to flow in the same direction, the drift for the return trip is in the same direction as the first.
$\bullet$ Return drift $x_2 = v_r \times t = 5 \times 20 = 100\text{ m}$.
$\bullet$ Total displacement along the river bank $= x_1 + x_2 = 100 + 100 = 200\text{ m}$.

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
The minimum time is $40\text{ s}$ and the total displacement is $200\text{ m}$.

Therefore, the correct option is $\boxed{C}$.

text:
A particle starts moving from time $t=0$ and its coordinate is given as $x(t) = 4t^3 - 3t$
A. The particle returns to its original position (origin) 0.866 units later
B. The particle is 1 unit away from origin at its turning point
C. Acceleration of the particle is non-negative
D. The particle is 0.5 units away from origin at its turning point
E. Particle never turns back as acceleration is non-negative
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Determine velocity and acceleration functions}}$
Given the position function $x(t) = 4t^3 - 3t$, we differentiate with respect to time $t$:
$\bullet$ Velocity: $v(t) = \frac{dx}{dt} = 12t^2 - 3$
$\bullet$ Acceleration: $a(t) = \frac{dv}{dt} = 24t$



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Evaluate Statement A (Return to origin)}}$
The particle is at the origin when $x(t) = 0$:
$$4t^3 - 3t = 0 \implies t(4t^2 - 3) = 0$$
This gives $t = 0$ or $t = \sqrt{3/4} \approx 0.866$ units. Thus, the particle returns to the origin at $t = 0.866$. $\textbf{Statement A is TRUE.}$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Evaluate Statement B and D (Turning points)}}$
Turning points occur when velocity is zero, $v(t) = 0$:
$$12t^2 - 3 = 0 \implies t^2 = \frac{3}{12} = \frac{1}{4} \implies t = \pm 0.5$$
For $t > 0$, the turning point is at $t = 0.5$. At this time, the position is:
$$x(0.5) = 4(0.5)^3 - 3(0.5) = 4(0.125) - 1.5 = 0.5 - 1.5 = -1$$
The distance from the origin is $|x| = 1$ unit. Therefore, $\textbf{Statement B is TRUE}$ and $\textbf{Statement D is FALSE}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Evaluate Statement C and E (Acceleration behavior)}}$
$\bullet$ For $t \geq 0$, the acceleration $a(t) = 24t$ is always $\geq 0$. $\textbf{Statement C is TRUE.}$
$\bullet$ Since the particle has a turning point at $t = 0.5$, it does turn back. $\textbf{Statement E is FALSE.}$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
Statements A, B, and C only are correct.

text:
Water drops fall from a tap on the floor, 5 m below, at regular intervals of time, the first drop strikes the floor when the sixth drop begins to fall. The height at which the fourth drop will be from ground, at the instant when the first drop strikes the ground is ________ m.
$ (g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2) $

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Calculate the total time for the first drop to fall}}$
Using the second equation of motion for free fall ($s = \frac{1}{2}gt^2$):
$$5 = \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times t^2$$
$$5 = 5t^2 \implies t^2 = 1 \implies t = 1 \text{ s}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Determine the time interval between drops}}$
Since the first drop strikes the floor when the sixth begins to fall, there are 5 equal time intervals between the 6 drops. 
$$\text{Interval } (T) = \frac{\text{Total time}}{\text{Number of intervals}} = \frac{1 \text{ s}}{5} = 0.2 \text{ s}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Find the time for which the fourth drop has been falling}}$
At the instant the first drop hits ($t = 1 \text{ s}$), the sixth is at $t = 0 \text{ s}$, the fifth at $t = 0.2 \text{ s}$, and the fourth drop has been falling for:
$$t_4 = 1 - (3 \times 0.2) = 0.4 \text{ s}$$


$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Calculate the distance fallen by the fourth drop (} s_4 \text{)}}$
$$s_4 = \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times (0.4)^2$$
$$s_4 = 5 \times 0.16 = 0.8 \text{ m}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 5: Calculate the height above the ground}}$
$$\text{Height} = \text{Total height} - \text{Distance fallen}$$
$$\text{Height} = 5 - 0.8 = 4.2 \text{ m}$$

Therefore, the height of the fourth drop from the ground is $\boxed{4.2 \text{ m}}$.

text: The velocity (v) - Distance (x) graph is shown in figure. Which graph represents acceleration (a) versus distance (x) variation of this system?

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Determine the relationship between velocity (v) and distance (x)}}$
Based on the standard $v-x$ graph for this type of problem, the velocity decreases linearly with distance. We can express this linear relationship as:
$$v = mx + c$$
where $m$ is the negative slope and $c$ is the intercept on the velocity axis.



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Use the kinematic definition of acceleration}}$
Acceleration $a$ in terms of velocity and position is given by the formula:
$$a = v \frac{dv}{dx}$$
From our linear equation $v = mx + c$, we find the derivative $\frac{dv}{dx} = m$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Derive the acceleration (a) versus distance (x) equation}}$
Substitute the expressions for $v$ and $\frac{dv}{dx}$ into the acceleration formula:
$$a = (mx + c) \cdot m$$
$$a = m^2x + mc$$
This resulting equation is in the form $y = Mx + C$, which represents a straight line.

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Analyze the properties of the a-x graph}}$
$\bullet$ The slope of the $a-x$ graph is $m^2$. Since any real number squared is positive, the slope is positive.
$\bullet$ The graph shows a straight line with a positive slope, indicating that acceleration increases linearly with distance.
$\bullet$ Acceleration remains positive throughout the motion according to the specific system parameters.

Therefore, the correct graph representing this variation is option $\boxed{C}$.

text: A paratrooper jumps from an aeroplane and opens a parachute after $2 \text{ s}$ of free fall and starts deaccelerating with $3 \text{ m/s}^2$. At $10 \text{ m}$ height from ground, while descending with the help of parachute, the speed of paratrooper is $5 \text{ m/s}$. The initial height of the airplane is $\_\_\_\_$ m. ($g=10 \text{ m/s}^2$)

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze the free fall phase (first 2 s)}}$
During the first $2 \text{ s}$, the paratrooper starts from rest ($u = 0$).
$\bullet$ Velocity after $2 \text{ s}$ is $v_1 = u + gt = 0 + 10 \times 2 = 20 \text{ m/s}$.
$\bullet$ Distance covered during free fall is $h_1 = \frac{1}{2}gt^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 2^2 = 20 \text{ m}$.



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze the deceleration phase with parachute}}$
After opening the parachute, the paratrooper decelerates at $a = -3 \text{ m/s}^2$.
$\bullet$ At a height of $10 \text{ m}$ from the ground, the final velocity is $v_2 = 5 \text{ m/s}$.
$\bullet$ Let $h_2$ be the distance covered during deceleration until reaching the $10 \text{ m}$ height mark.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate the distance during deceleration (} h_2 \text{)}}$
Using the third kinematic equation, $v_2^2 = v_1^2 + 2ah_2$:
$$5^2 = 20^2 + 2(-3)h_2$$
$$25 = 400 - 6h_2$$
$$6h_2 = 375$$
$$h_2 = 62.5 \text{ m}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Determine the initial height of the airplane}}$
The total height $H$ of the airplane is the sum of the free fall distance, the deceleration distance, and the remaining height from the ground:
$$H = h_1 + h_2 + 10 = 20 + 62.5 + 10 = 92.5 \text{ m}$$

Therefore, the initial height of the airplane is $\boxed{92.5 \text{ m}}$.

text:
Identify the correct statements:
The presence of $-\text{NO}_2$ group in benzene ring:

$\pmb{\text{A.}}$ activates the ring towards electrophilic substitutions.
$\pmb{\text{B.}}$ deactivates the ring towards electrophilic substitutions.
$\pmb{\text{C.}}$ activates the ring towards nucleophilic substitutions.
$\pmb{\text{D.}}$ deactivates the ring towards nucleophilic substitutions.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Analysis of the Nitro (–NO}_2\text{) Group Effect:}}$

The Nitro group is a powerful $\pmb{\text{electron-withdrawing group}}$ (EWG) due to both its strong negative inductive effect ($-I$) and its strong resonance effect ($-R$).



$$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|} 
\hline 
\pmb{\text{Reaction Type}} & \pmb{\text{Effect of –NO}_2} & \pmb{\text{Reasoning}} \\ 
\hline 
\text{Electrophilic Substitution} & \text{Deactivation} & \text{It withdraws electrons, reducing electron density} \\ 
& & \text{available for electrophilic attack.} \\ 
\hline 
\text{Nucleophilic Substitution} & \text{Activation} & \text{It withdraws electrons, making the ring more} \\ 
& & \text{electron-deficient and susceptible to nucleophiles.} \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement B is Correct:}}$ By withdrawing electron density through resonance, the $-\text{NO}_2$ group makes the benzene ring less reactive toward $\pmb{\text{electrophiles}}$ (which seek electrons). It is specifically a meta-directing deactivator.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement C is Correct:}}$ In Nucleophilic Aromatic Substitution ($\text{S}_N\text{Ar}$), the rate-determining step is the attack of a nucleophile on the electron-poor ring. The $-\text{NO}_2$ group stabilizes the intermediate carbanion (Meisenheimer complex) by delocalizing the negative charge, especially when positioned ortho or para to the leaving group.



$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$
Statements B and C are correct.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{D}}$.

text:
Match $\pmb{\text{List - I}}$ with $\pmb{\text{List - II}}$.

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|} \hline \pmb{\text{List - I (Pair of Compounds)}} & \pmb{\text{List - II (Type of Isomers)}} \\ \hline \text{A. 2-Methylpropene and but-1-ene} & \text{I. Stereoisomers} \\ \text{B. Cis-but-2-ene and trans-but-2-ene} & \text{II. Position isomers} \\ \text{C. 2-Butanol and diethyl ether} & \text{III. Chain isomers} \\ \text{D. But-1-ene and but-2-ene} & \text{IV. Functional group isomers} \\ \hline \end{array}$$

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Analyze Pair A (2-Methylpropene and but-1-ene)}}$

Both compounds have the molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_4\mathrm{H}_8$. However, 2-methylpropene has a branched carbon skeleton, while but-1-ene has a straight carbon chain. Therefore, they are $\pmb{\text{Chain isomers}}$ (III).



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Analyze Pair B (Cis-but-2-ene and trans-but-2-ene)}}$

These compounds have the same structural formula but differ in the spatial arrangement of atoms around the double bond (geometrical isomerism). Geometrical isomers are a sub-type of $\pmb{\text{Stereoisomers}}$ (I).



$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Analyze Pair C (2-Butanol and diethyl ether)}}$

Both compounds share the molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_4\mathrm{H}_{10}\mathrm{O}$. 2-Butanol contains an alcohol ($-\mathrm{OH}$) group, while diethyl ether contains an ether ($-\mathrm{O}-$) group. Since they have different functional groups, they are $\pmb{\text{Functional group isomers}}$ (IV).

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Analyze Pair D (But-1-ene and but-2-ene)}}$

Both are straight-chain alkenes with the formula $\mathrm{C}_4\mathrm{H}_8$. They differ only in the location of the double bond (at C1 vs. C2). Therefore, they are $\pmb{\text{Position isomers}}$ (II).

$\pmb{\text{Step 5: Final Matching}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{A } \rightarrow \text{ III}}$
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{B } \rightarrow \text{ I}}$
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{C } \rightarrow \text{ IV}}$
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{D } \rightarrow \text{ II}}$

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{A}}$.

text:
Given below are four compounds :
(a) n-propyl chloride
(b) iso-propyl chloride
(c) sec-butyl chloride
(d) neo-pentyl chloride

Percentage of carbon in the one which exhibits optical isomerism is :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Identify the compound that exhibits optical isomerism}}$

For a molecule to exhibit optical isomerism, it must contain at least one $\pmb{\text{chiral carbon}}$ atom (a carbon atom attached to four different groups).

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{n-propyl chloride (CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{Cl):}}$ No chiral carbon.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{iso-propyl chloride ((CH}_3)_2\text{CHCl):}}$ The central carbon is attached to two identical methyl groups; hence, it is $\pmb{\text{achiral}}$.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{sec-butyl chloride (CH}_3\text{CH(Cl)CH}_2\text{CH}_3\text{):}}$ The second carbon is attached to four different groups: $-\mathrm{H}$, $-\mathrm{Cl}$, $-\mathrm{CH}_3$ (methyl), and $-\mathrm{C}_2\mathrm{H}_5$ (ethyl). Thus, it is $\pmb{\text{chiral}}$ and exhibits optical isomerism.
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{neo-pentyl chloride ((CH}_3)_3\text{CCH}_2\text{Cl):}}$ No chiral carbon.



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Determine the molecular formula of sec-butyl chloride}}$

The structure is $\mathrm{CH}_3\text{-}CH(Cl)\text{-}CH_2\text{-}CH_3$. 
Summing the atoms: $\pmb{\mathrm{C}_4\mathrm{H}_9\mathrm{Cl}}$.

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Calculate the Molecular Mass}}$

Using atomic masses: $\mathrm{C} = 12, \mathrm{H} = 1, \mathrm{Cl} = 35.5$
$$\text{Molar Mass} = (4 \times 12) + (9 \times 1) + 35.5$$
$$\text{Molar Mass} = 48 + 9 + 35.5 = \pmb{92.5 \text{ g/mol}}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 4: Calculate the percentage of Carbon}}$

$$\text{Percentage of Carbon} = \left( \frac{\text{Total mass of Carbon}}{\text{Molar Mass of compound}} \right) \times 100$$
$$\text{Percentage of Carbon} = \left( \frac{48}{92.5} \right) \times 100 \approx \pmb{51.89\%}$$

Rounding to the nearest integer given in the options, we get $\pmb{52}$.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{C}}$.

text:
Identify correct statements from the following :

$\mathrm{A.}$ Propanal and propanone are functional isomers.
$\mathrm{B.}$ Ethoxyethane and methoxypropane are metamers.
$\mathrm{C.}$ But-2-ene shows optical isomerism.
$\mathrm{D.}$ But-1-ene and but-2-ene are functional isomers.
$\mathrm{E.}$ Pentane and 2, 2-dimethyl propane are chain isomers.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Evaluate each statement individually}}$

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement A:}}$ Propanal (an aldehyde) and propanone (a ketone) both share the molecular formula $\mathrm{C}_3\mathrm{H}_6\mathrm{O}$. Since they belong to different homologous series, they are $\pmb{\text{functional isomers}}$. This statement is $\pmb{\text{correct}}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement B:}}$ Ethoxyethane ($\mathrm{C}_2\mathrm{H}_5\text{-O-}\mathrm{C}_2\mathrm{H}_5$) and methoxypropane ($\mathrm{CH}_3\text{-O-}\mathrm{C}_3\mathrm{H}_7$) are ethers with the same molecular formula but different alkyl groups attached to the oxygen atom. These are $\pmb{\text{metamers}}$. This statement is $\pmb{\text{correct}}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement C:}}$ But-2-ene ($\mathrm{CH}_3\text{-CH=CH-}\mathrm{CH}_3$) does not have a chiral carbon atom. While it shows geometrical isomerism ($cis$ and $trans$), it does $\pmb{\text{not}}$ show optical isomerism. This statement is $\pmb{\text{incorrect}}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement D:}}$ But-1-ene and but-2-ene differ only in the position of the double bond within the same carbon chain. Therefore, they are $\pmb{\text{position isomers}}$, not functional isomers. This statement is $\pmb{\text{incorrect}}$.

$\bullet$ $\pmb{\text{Statement E:}}$ Pentane (a straight 5-carbon chain) and 2,2-dimethylpropane (a branched 3-carbon chain with two methyl branches) both have the formula $\mathrm{C}_5\mathrm{H}_{12}$. They differ in the structure of the carbon skeleton, making them $\pmb{\text{chain isomers}}$. This statement is $\pmb{\text{correct}}$.



$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Conclusion}}$

Statements $\pmb{\text{A, B, and E}}$ are the only correct ones.

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{A}}$.

text:
The cyclic cations having the same number of hyperconjugation are:
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Concept of Hyperconjugation in Carbocations:}}$

Hyperconjugation in carbocations is determined by the number of $\alpha$-hydrogens ($H$) present on the carbon atoms directly attached to the positively charged carbon center. The more $\alpha$-$H$ atoms, the greater the number of hyperconjugative structures.

$\pmb{\text{Analysis of given Cations:}}$

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|} 
\hline 
\textbf{Cation} & \textbf{Structure Type} & \textbf{Identification of } \pmb{\alpha}\text{-carbons} & \textbf{Total } \pmb{\alpha}\text{-H Count} \\ 
\hline 
A & \text{1-methyl-2-ethylcyclohexyl} & \text{Methyl group (3H) + Ring } \text{CH}_2 \text{ (2H) + Ring CH (1H)} & 3 + 2 + 1 = \mathbf{6} \\ 
\hline 
B & \text{1-methyl-4-propylcyclohexyl} & \text{Methyl group (3H) + Two Ring } \text{CH}_2 \text{ (2H+2H)} & 3 + 2 + 2 = \mathbf{7} \\ 
\hline 
C & \text{1-ethyl-2-methylcyclohexyl} & \text{Ethyl } \text{CH}_2 \text{ (2H) + Ring } \text{CH}_2 \text{ (2H) + Ring CH (2H)} & 2 + 2 + 2 = \mathbf{6} \\ 
\hline 
D & \text{1,2-diethylcyclohexyl} & \text{Ethyl } \text{CH}_2 \text{ (2H) + Ring } \text{CH}_2 \text{ (2H) + Ring CH (1H)} & 2 + 2 + 1 = \mathbf{5} \\ 
\hline 
\end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Conclusion:}}$

Based on the calculation of $\alpha$-hydrogens:
$\bullet$ $\textbf{Cation A}$ has $\mathbf{6}$ $\alpha$-hydrogens.
$\bullet$ $\textbf{Cation C}$ has $\mathbf{6}$ $\alpha$-hydrogens.

Since cations $\textbf{A}$ and $\textbf{C}$ have the same number of hyperconjugation structures, the correct option is $\mathbf{C}$.

text:
The $\pmb{\text{CORRECT}}$ order of stability for the following carbanions is:
$$\mathrm{CH}_2=\mathrm{CH}^{\ominus}, \mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_2^{\ominus}, \mathrm{HC} \equiv \mathrm{C}^{\ominus}$$

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Step 1: Identify the hybridization of the carbanion carbon}}$

The stability of a carbanion depends on the electronegativity of the carbon atom bearing the negative charge. Electronegativity increases with increasing $\pmb{s\text{-character}}$ of the hybrid orbital.



$\bullet$ $\pmb{\mathrm{HC} \equiv \mathrm{C}^{\ominus}:}$ The carbon is $\pmb{sp}$ hybridized ($50\% \text{ s-character}$).
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\mathrm{CH}_2 = \mathrm{CH}^{\ominus}:}$ The carbon is $\pmb{sp^2}$ hybridized ($33.3\% \text{ s-character}$).
$\bullet$ $\pmb{\mathrm{CH}_3 - \mathrm{CH}_2^{\ominus}:}$ The carbon is $\pmb{sp^3}$ hybridized ($25\% \text{ s-character}$).

$\pmb{\text{Step 2: Relate s-character to stability}}$

A higher percentage of $\pmb{s\text{-character}}$ means the electrons are closer to the nucleus and more strongly held, making the atom more electronegative and better able to accommodate a negative charge.

$$\begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|} \hline \text{Carbanion} & \text{Hybridization} & \text{s-character} & \text{Electronegativity} \\ \hline \mathrm{HC} \equiv \mathrm{C}^{\ominus} & sp & 50\% & \text{Highest} \\ \hline \mathrm{CH}_2 = \mathrm{CH}^{\ominus} & sp^2 & 33.3\% & \text{Intermediate} \\ \hline \mathrm{CH}_3 - \mathrm{CH}_2^{\ominus} & sp^3 & 25\% & \text{Lowest} \\ \hline \end{array}$$

$\pmb{\text{Step 3: Determine the stability order}}$

Based on electronegativity, the stability follows the order of $s\text{-character}$:
$$\mathrm{HC} \equiv \mathrm{C}^{\ominus} > \mathrm{CH}_2=\mathrm{CH}^{\ominus} > \mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_2^{\ominus}$$

Therefore, the correct answer is $\boxed{\text{C}}$.

text:
Given below are two statements:

$\textbf{Statement I:}$ There are several conformers for $n$-butane. Out of those conformers, is (REFER TO IMAGE)
$\textbf{Statement II:}$ As the dihedral angle increases, torsional strain decreases from $(X)$ to $(Y)$.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below:

options.text: Statement I is false but Statement II is true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true

solution.text:
$\pmb{\text{Analysis of Conformational Stability in } n\text{-Butane:}}$
